items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 prescribes two distinct elements: procedures and phraseology. Why must the order include both rather than prescribing only controller actions?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-1, the order prescribes both procedures and phraseology. Standardisation of actions alone is insufficient — standardised communication is equally critical for preventing misunderstanding between controllers and pilots. Procedures govern what controllers do; phraseology governs how they say it.

choices.text: Standardised actions alone ensure safety; phraseology is included only as a training convenience.
Procedures govern controller actions and phraseology governs standardised communication; both are independently necessary for safe, coordinated operations.
Phraseology is the only binding element; procedures are advisory suggestions that facilities may modify locally.
The order prescribes phraseology only for international operations; domestic controllers use locally developed procedures.

items.question_text: A facility faces a situation where the standard separation minima in FAA Order JO 7110.65 are unsuitable. A supervisor considers documenting reduced minima in a Letter of Agreement (LOA) between two civilian facilities versus pursuing a waiver. Which mechanism may authorise a reduction below the order's minima for civilian operations, and why?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order and may not specify lower minima except when military authority authorises reduced separation between military aircraft. Per 1-1-11, waivers address exceptional or unusual requirements that dictate procedural deviations. For civilian facilities needing minima below the order, a waiver is the correct mechanism — an LOA between civilian facilities cannot lower the order's minima.

choices.text: Either an LOA or a waiver works equally — the facility may choose whichever is faster to process.
An LOA may lower minima for civilian operations if both facilities sign it; a waiver is needed only for military involvement.
An LOA between civilian facilities cannot lower minima below the order; a waiver under the exceptional-or-unusual standard is the correct mechanism for civilian procedural deviations.
Neither an LOA nor a waiver can change minima — only a full revision of the order through JO 7000.5 can alter separation standards.

items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 describes two distribution mechanisms. What is the key distinction between the mandatory distribution list in subparagraph (a) and the electronic subscription mechanism in subparagraph (b)?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-14, subparagraph (a) lists offices and facilities to which the order IS distributed — a mandatory push to named recipients. Subparagraph (b) places an affirmative duty on organisations to subscribe, view, and download changes — the burden is on the recipient to take action. The two mechanisms are complementary but create different types of obligation.

choices.text: Both are automatic — the order arrives without any action required by the recipient in either case.
Subparagraph (a) lists mandatory distribution recipients; subparagraph (b) places an affirmative duty on organisations to subscribe, view, and download changes.
Subparagraph (a) applies only to military facilities; subparagraph (b) applies only to civilian facilities.
Neither mechanism creates a binding obligation — distribution is informational only.

items.question_text: A trainee compares the Explanation of Changes pages against the marked changes in the order text and finds that the EOC pages include only a subset of the changed text — formatting corrections and punctuation fixes are absent. What principle explains which changes appear on the EOC pages?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-5, the Explanation of Changes pages identify SIGNIFICANT changes. Minor formatting corrections, punctuation fixes, and typographical repairs are not significant changes and are therefore excluded from the EOC pages. The EOC pages provide a summary of substantive revisions, not an exhaustive catalogue of every textual change.

choices.text: The EOC pages should list every change, including formatting; the omissions are errors that must be reported to AJV-P.
The EOC pages identify only significant changes; minor formatting and punctuation corrections are not significant and are therefore excluded.
Formatting changes are never made to the order; any such differences are printing errors.
The EOC pages only list changes that affect separation minima; all other changes are documented in JO 7210.3.

items.question_text: A controller reads that a facility 'should' issue a particular advisory and another paragraph stating the controller 'may' issue a different advisory. What is the operational difference between these two instructions under FAA Order JO 7110.65 word meanings?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-1c and 1-2-1d, 'should' means a procedure is recommended — the controller is expected to do it absent a good reason not to. 'May' means a procedure is optional — the controller decides whether circumstances warrant it. A recommended action carries an expectation; an optional one does not.

choices.text: Both words mean the same thing — the controller may choose whether to comply.
'Should' means recommended (expected absent good reason); 'may' means optional (controller decides based on circumstances).
'Should' means mandatory; 'may' means recommended.
'Should' means the procedure applies only to terminal facilities; 'may' applies to all facilities.

items.question_text: In FAA Order JO 7110.65, how does the function of a note differ from the function of a reference?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-3, notes provide explanatory or factual context relating to directive material. Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information such as another FAA order, NWS directive, CFR, or Advisory Circular. Notes explain; references point to other sources.

choices.text: Notes point to external sources; references provide explanatory context for directive material.
Notes and references serve the same function and the terms are used interchangeably.
Notes are mandatory; references are optional.
Notes provide explanatory or factual context; references direct attention to additional or supporting sources of information.

items.question_text: In TBL 1-2-1, the abbreviation AAR has two listed meanings: Adapted Arrival Route and Airport Arrival Rate. How does a controller determine which meaning applies when reading FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per TBL 1-2-1, several abbreviations have multiple listed meanings. Context determines which applies: AAR as Airport Arrival Rate appears in traffic management discussions with numeric rates; AAR as Adapted Arrival Route appears in route descriptions. The controller must use operational context, not list order, to disambiguate.

choices.text: Use the first meaning listed in TBL 1-2-1, as it is the primary definition.
Determine the correct meaning from the operational context in which the abbreviation is used.
All listed meanings apply simultaneously — the controller must satisfy every possible interpretation.
Use the longer meaning for en route operations and the shorter meaning for terminal operations.

items.question_text: Arrange the three course classifications defined in FAA Order JO 7110.65 in order from the smallest angular difference to the largest: (A) Opposite/reciprocal courses, (B) Same courses, (C) Crossing courses.
items.explanation: Per 1-2-2, same courses have angular difference less than 45 degrees (smallest range); crossing courses cover 45 through 135 degrees inclusive (middle range); opposite/reciprocal courses cover greater than 135 through 180 degrees inclusive (largest range). The correct ascending order is B, C, A.

choices.text: B (Same), C (Crossing), A (Opposite/reciprocal).
C (Crossing), B (Same), A (Opposite/reciprocal).
B (Same), A (Opposite/reciprocal), C (Crossing).
A (Opposite/reciprocal), C (Crossing), B (Same).

items.question_text: How does the initial routing of a procedural change recommendation from FAA facility personnel differ from a recommendation submitted by an industry group?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-8a and 1-1-8b, FAA personnel submit recommended changes to facility management first. Recommendations from other sources — including industry groups — should be submitted through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels, not through facility management. The destination (AJV-P) is the same; the initial routing differs by source type.

choices.text: Both go directly to AJV-P without any intermediate routing.
FAA personnel submit to facility management first; industry groups submit through appropriate FAA, military, or industry/user channels.
FAA personnel submit directly to AJV-P; industry groups must route through facility management.
Both must be submitted through the Federal Register public comment process.

items.question_text: What does the annotation WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION mean when it appears within a paragraph of FAA Order JO 7110.65?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5f, WAKE TURBULENCE APPLICATION inserted within a paragraph means that the remaining information in the paragraph requires the application of wake turbulence procedures. The annotation does not label a separate wake turbulence section — it marks that wake turbulence rules apply to the remainder of the paragraph in which the annotation appears.

choices.text: Only the sentence immediately preceding the annotation requires wake turbulence procedures.
The paragraph is an advisory discussion of wake turbulence and creates no mandatory procedures.
Wake turbulence procedures must be applied to all information that follows the annotation within that section of the order.
Wake turbulence separation is waived for all operations described in that paragraph.

items.question_text: What do the change number and effective date on a revised page of FAA Order JO 7110.65 tell the controller, and are they printed on every page?
items.explanation: Per 1-2-5a, the change number and effective date are printed on each revised or additional page — not on every page of the order. They allow the controller to verify at a glance that the page reflects the current change level. An unrevised page that does not require a change is reprinted in its original form per 1-2-5b.

choices.text: They are printed on every page of the order so controllers can verify currency throughout.
They are printed on each revised or additional page only, allowing quick verification that the page is at the current change level.
They appear only on the Explanation of Changes pages, not on the procedure pages themselves.
They are printed on the cover page only; internal pages use a separate date-tracking system.

items.question_text: FAA Order JO 7110.65 applies to two categories of persons. Which statement correctly identifies both categories?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, the order applies to all ATO personnel AND anyone using ATO directives. The first category covers employees within the Air Traffic Organization regardless of role. The second category captures contractors, military personnel, and others who use ATO-published procedures — regardless of employment relationship.

choices.text: Only FAA-certified air traffic controllers and facility managers.
All ATO personnel and anyone using ATO directives.
Only FAA employees and military personnel assigned to joint-use facilities.
Only persons holding a current FAA medical certificate and security clearance.

items.question_text: Under FAA Order JO 7110.65, what rule determines which FAA region a facility must contact when the order references a non-ATO regional office organization?
items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility contacts the FAA region where the facility is physically located — not the region where its service area office is located. Physical geography governs over organisational alignment for non-ATO regional contacts. The examples given (Communications Center, Flight Standards, Airport offices) are organisations outside the Air Traffic Organization.

choices.text: Contact the region where the facility's service area office is located.
Contact the region where the facility is physically located.
Contact whichever region most recently issued a directive on the subject matter.
Contact Washington headquarters directly in all cases.

items.question_text: A controller is working a busy sector when a pilot requests an airport advisory. The controller is actively separating three converging aircraft. What is the controller's obligation regarding the advisory?
items.explanation: Additional services are not optional — they are required when the work situation permits. However, higher-priority duties (such as active separation) and controller workload are explicitly listed as factors that may preclude providing additional services. The controller must first resolve the higher-priority separation task, then provide the advisory if the work situation then permits.

choices.text: Provide the advisory immediately — additional services are never optional for controllers.
Refuse the advisory request because additional services are entirely at the controller's discretion.
Resolve the separation task first, then provide the advisory if workload and circumstances permit.
Delegate the entire position to another controller so the advisory can be handled without delay.

items.question_text: Which of the following is a valid reason for a controller to deviate from JO 7110.65 procedures and minima?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists three authorized grounds for deviation: (1) conformance with ICAO Documents, National Rules of the Air, or special agreements where the U.S. provides service outside its territory; (2) other procedures/minima prescribed in an LOA, FAA directive, or military document; (3) deviation necessary to assist an aircraft when an emergency has been declared. A controller's independent belief that a different procedure is safer, absent any of these conditions, is not an authorized basis.

choices.text: The controller independently determines a different procedure would be safer.
The pilot requests a different procedure for operational convenience.
Other procedures or minima are prescribed in a letter of agreement, FAA directive, or military document.
The controller's supervisor verbally authorizes an ad hoc procedure change.

items.question_text: A controller is simultaneously handling an aircraft at an unsafe altitude near terrain and another aircraft requesting a weather update for an airport 50 miles ahead. Which action must be performed first?
items.explanation: Separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts is the first priority. The controller must issue the safety alert before providing the weather update, even though both are required duties. The note to the paragraph confirms that the action most critical from a safety standpoint is performed first.

choices.text: Provide the weather update first because it is a specific pilot request.
Issue the safety alert first — separation and safety alerts are the first duty priority.
Handle both simultaneously by giving the weather update while scanning for terrain conflicts.
Ask the supervisor to decide which of the two tasks is more important.

items.question_text: In the duty priority hierarchy, how does 'provide and/or solicit weather information' rank relative to 'provide additional services'?
items.explanation: The duty priority paragraph enumerates duties in order: (a) separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts, (b) supporting national security and homeland defense, (c) providing and/or soliciting weather information, (d) providing additional services contingent upon higher priority duties. Weather information (c) is a separate, higher-priority duty than additional services (d).

choices.text: Weather information is an additional service provided only when workload permits.
Weather information is a distinct duty that comes before additional services in the priority hierarchy.
Weather information and additional services share the same priority level.
Weather information is optional unless the pilot specifically requests it.

items.question_text: What is the first priority duty for all controllers?
items.explanation: The paragraph states: 'Give first priority to separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts as required in this order.' All other provisions are prioritized using good judgment based on the situation.

choices.text: Providing weather information to all aircraft on the frequency.
Providing additional services to the extent possible.
Separating aircraft and issuing safety alerts as required.
Supporting national security and homeland defense activities.

items.question_text: A civil air ambulance flight has 'MEDEVAC' in the remarks section of the flight plan. On initial contact, the pilot uses the standard company call sign without stating 'MEDEVAC.' Is the controller required to provide MEDEVAC priority handling?
items.explanation: MEDEVAC priority handling is triggered when the pilot verbally identifies the flight by stating 'MEDEVAC' followed by the FAA authorized call sign or full civil registration. A 'MEDEVAC' entry in the flight plan remarks is informational only and does not by itself entitle the flight to priority handling.

choices.text: Yes, because MEDEVAC in the flight plan remarks establishes priority handling automatically.
No, because the pilot must verbally state 'MEDEVAC' followed by the authorized call sign to trigger priority handling.
Yes, because any air ambulance flight receives priority regardless of how it identifies itself.
No, because the controller must verify the medical urgency with the destination airport first.

items.question_text: A controller issues an 'expedite' climb to FL240. Before the aircraft reaches FL240, the controller needs to change the altitude to FL280 and says 'Climb and maintain FL280.' Is the expedite instruction still in effect?
items.explanation: If an 'expedite' clearance is issued and subsequently the altitude to maintain is changed or restated without an expedite instruction, the expedite instruction is canceled. By issuing a new altitude without 'expedite,' the controller has canceled the urgency requirement.

choices.text: Yes — the expedite remains in effect until the aircraft reaches the originally assigned altitude.
No — when a new altitude is issued without restating 'expedite,' the expedite instruction is canceled.
Yes — expedite applies to all subsequent altitude assignments for the duration of the flight.
No — expedite is automatically canceled anytime any clearance amendment is issued.

items.question_text: After receiving a terrain safety alert, a pilot responds that the aircraft has the terrain in sight and will maintain visual terrain clearance. May the controller discontinue further safety alerts?
items.explanation: Once the pilot informs the controller that action is being taken to resolve the situation, the controller may discontinue further alerts. The pilot's statement that they have the terrain in sight and are maintaining visual clearance constitutes notification that action is being taken. The pilot then has sole prerogative to determine the course of action.

choices.text: No — safety alerts must continue until the aircraft is at least 1,000 feet above the terrain.
Yes — once the pilot informs the controller action is being taken, further alerts may be discontinued.
No — only a climb to an altitude above the MVA authorizes stopping alerts.
Yes — but only if the controller independently verifies on radar that the terrain conflict is resolved.

items.question_text: What determines whether a controller should issue a safety alert to an aircraft?
items.explanation: The trigger for a safety alert is the controller's awareness and judgment that the aircraft is in a position/altitude placing it in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft. It is not limited to automated system alerts. Sources of recognition can include MSAW, Mode C intruder alerts, PAR observations, or pilot reports.

choices.text: Only when an automated system such as MSAW generates an alert.
Only when a pilot specifically requests a safety alert from the controller.
When the controller is aware the aircraft is in a position or altitude that places it in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft.
Only when another controller calls to advise of an unsafe situation.

items.question_text: A controller learns that a localizer at a nearby airport is producing unreliable course guidance to pilots. To whom should this information be reported as soon as possible?
items.explanation: Controllers must report information concerning components of the NAS or flight conditions that may have an adverse effect on air safety to the appropriate FSS, airport manager's office, ARTCC, approach control facility, operations office, or military operations office, as applicable.

choices.text: Only to the supervisor for internal handling.
To the appropriate FSS, airport manager's office, ARTCC, approach control facility, or operations office.
Only to the pilots on the controller's frequency.
To the FAA regional office within 24 hours through administrative channels.

items.question_text: An aircraft reports what appears to be a malfunctioning VOR. A second aircraft confirms the VOR is operating normally. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: When the second aircraft reports normal operations, the controller continues use of the NAVAID, informs the first aircraft of the normal report, and records the incident on FAA Form 7230-4 or the appropriate military form. Standby activation is only required when the second aircraft confirms the malfunction or no second report is available.

choices.text: Activate standby equipment to verify the second aircraft's report before informing the first aircraft.
Continue use, inform the first aircraft of the normal report, and record the incident on the appropriate form.
Shut down the VOR because one aircraft reported a problem that requires investigation.
Inform technical operations personnel and request advice on whether the aid should remain in service.

items.question_text: Two military aircraft are conducting a MARSA operation in airspace where civilian IFR traffic is also operating. What is ATC's separation responsibility?
items.explanation: ATC facilities do not invoke or deny MARSA. Their sole responsibility is to provide separation between military aircraft engaged in MARSA operations and other nonparticipating IFR aircraft. Separation among participating aircraft rests with the military command.

choices.text: Provide separation between the MARSA-participating aircraft and nonparticipating IFR aircraft.
Provide separation among all aircraft participating in the MARSA operation.
Deny MARSA until the military command certifies pilots are current on procedures.
Provide separation between MARSA aircraft and all other aircraft regardless of IFR or VFR status.

items.question_text: During a formation join-up, two aircraft maneuver within close proximity of each other. Who is responsible for separation between these aircraft during the join-up?
items.explanation: Formation flights are controlled as a single aircraft. Separation responsibility between aircraft within the formation rests with the flight leader and the other pilots, including transition periods during join-up and breakaway. The controller provides separation between the formation (as a single aircraft) and other traffic.

choices.text: The controller must issue traffic advisories and maintain standard IFR separation within the formation.
The flight leader and participating pilots are responsible for separation within the formation, including during join-up.
The controller maintains separation until join-up is complete, after which responsibility transfers to the flight leader.
Both the controller and the flight leader share separation responsibility during join-up.

items.question_text: What three conditions must all be satisfied before ATC can support a formation join-up between two individual aircraft?
items.explanation: Support formation flight join-up for two aircraft when all of the following occur: requested by any participating pilot, all participating pilots concur, and either of the participating pilots reports the other in sight. All three conditions must be met.

choices.text: Pilot request, supervisor approval, and LOA authorization.
Request by a participating pilot, concurrence of all pilots, and a sighting report from either pilot.
Radar contact, altitude separation, and frequency change to a discrete code.
Flight leader designation, altitude assignment, and a minimum of 5 miles visibility.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft is cleared for a visual approach behind a Heavy Boeing 747. The pilot accepts the visual approach. How do wake turbulence procedures apply?
items.explanation: Wake turbulence procedures apply to aircraft operating behind another aircraft when wake turbulence separation is required. The separation minima must continue to touchdown for all IFR aircraft not making a visual approach or maintaining visual separation. An aircraft on a visual approach is excluded from the touchdown continuation rule, but wake turbulence procedures still apply.

choices.text: Wake turbulence separation minima must continue to touchdown because the aircraft is IFR.
Wake turbulence procedures do not apply because the pilot accepted a visual approach.
Wake turbulence procedures apply, but the minima need not continue to touchdown because the aircraft is on a visual approach.
Wake turbulence separation continues to touchdown regardless of approach type when behind a Heavy.

items.question_text: Under what terminal condition must a wake turbulence cautionary advisory be issued to a VFR aircraft?
items.explanation: The paragraph specifies that at terminal facilities, wake turbulence cautionary advisories must be issued to VFR aircraft not being radar vectored when they are behind a larger aircraft that requires wake turbulence separation. The advisory includes position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.

choices.text: Only when the VFR aircraft is on an IFR flight plan within 5 miles of the larger aircraft.
Only when the pilot of the VFR aircraft specifically requests wake turbulence information.
When the VFR aircraft not being radar vectored is behind the larger aircraft.
Only after the larger aircraft has reported wake turbulence on a previous approach.

items.question_text: What three items of information must a wake turbulence cautionary advisory include?
items.explanation: Wake turbulence cautionary advisories must include the position, altitude if known, and direction of flight of the aircraft creating the wake hazard. These three elements give the following aircraft sufficient information to assess the risk.

choices.text: Aircraft type, speed, and estimated time of arrival.
Position, altitude if known, and direction of flight.
Runway assignment, aircraft weight class, and expected departure time.
Call sign, destination, and aircraft manufacturer.

items.question_text: Two VFR aircraft outside Class B, Class C, and TRSA airspace are converging on a controller's frequency. No separation minima apply, but the controller judges their proximity warrants a traffic advisory. What should the controller do?
items.explanation: Where no separation minima applies — such as for VFR aircraft outside Class B/Class C or a TRSA — traffic advisories must be issued when, in the controller's judgment, proximity warrants it. The absence of applicable separation minima does not relieve the controller of the advisory obligation.

choices.text: Issue no advisory because no separation minima applies to these aircraft.
Issue traffic advisories because the controller's judgment is that proximity warrants it.
Issue advisories only if the aircraft are both on IFR flight plans.
Wait until the aircraft report each other in sight, then issue no advisory.

items.question_text: An aircraft reports it is responding to a TCAS RA and begins a climb. A second aircraft is converging with the responding aircraft and remains on its previously assigned route with no RA report. What must the controller do for the second aircraft?
items.explanation: Unless advised by other aircraft that they are also responding to a TCAS RA, the controller must not assume they are involved in the RA or aware of the responding aircraft's intended maneuvers. The controller must continue to provide control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate.

choices.text: Assume the second aircraft is also responding to an RA and issue no instructions.
Continue providing control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate to the second aircraft.
Instruct the second aircraft to follow the first aircraft's climb profile.
Terminate all transmissions to the second aircraft until the RA is resolved.

items.question_text: An aircraft responding to a TCAS RA has returned to its assigned altitude FL330 and is level. Has ATC separation responsibility resumed?
items.explanation: ATC responsibility for approved separation resumes when the responding aircraft has returned to its assigned altitude. This is the first of the three conditions listed. No crew notification is required when the aircraft has simply returned to its assigned altitude.

choices.text: No — the crew must explicitly report the TCAS maneuver is complete first.
Yes — the aircraft returning to its assigned altitude is a condition that resumes separation responsibility.
No — separation responsibility can only resume when the aircraft lands.
Yes — but only if the controller also issues a new clearance.

items.question_text: When an aircraft reports a TCAS RA, what is the controller expressly prohibited from doing?
items.explanation: The paragraph states: 'do not issue control instructions that are contrary to the RA procedure that a crew member has advised you that they are executing.' The controller may still issue traffic advisories and safety alerts. The prohibition is specifically against conflicting instructions.

choices.text: Issuing any transmissions at all to the responding aircraft until the RA is resolved.
Issuing control instructions contrary to the RA procedure the crew has advised they are executing.
Issuing traffic advisories or safety alerts to the responding aircraft.
Handing off the aircraft to another sector during the RA event.

items.question_text: What phraseology must a controller use to deny a non-RVSM aircraft clearance into RVSM airspace?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology to deny clearance into RVSM airspace is 'UNABLE CLEARANCE INTO RVSM AIRSPACE.' This is an exact phrase from the paragraph. 'Negative RVSM' is the correct term for ground-to-ground coordination, not for the denial to the pilot.

choices.text: UNABLE CLEARANCE INTO RVSM AIRSPACE.
RVSM AIRSPACE NOT AVAILABLE, REMAIN BELOW FL290.
NEGATIVE RVSM, CLEARANCE DENIED.
UNABLE RVSM ACCESS, MAINTAIN PRESENT ALTITUDE.

items.question_text: What is the vertical separation minimum between eligible aircraft within RVSM airspace?
items.explanation: RVSM airspace is defined as any airspace between FL 290 and FL 410 inclusive, where eligible aircraft are separated vertically by 1,000 feet. This is a reduction from the standard 2,000 feet above FL 290 in non-RVSM airspace.

choices.text: 2,000 feet, consistent with standard vertical separation above FL 290.
1,000 feet between eligible aircraft.
500 feet when both aircraft are RVSM-capable.
1,500 feet between RVSM and non-RVSM aircraft.

items.question_text: An aircraft reports it is responding to a TAWS alert and begins an immediate climb. The controller is aware of another aircraft 1,000 feet above. What is the controller's obligation?
items.explanation: When an aircraft responds to a TAWS alert, the controller must not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure. Once the aircraft begins a TAWS maneuver, the controller is not responsible for providing approved separation between the responding aircraft and any other aircraft, airspace, terrain, or obstructions. The controller must still provide safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate.

choices.text: Issue an immediate descent instruction to maintain separation.
Do not issue contrary control instructions; provide safety alerts and traffic advisories as appropriate.
Assume full separation responsibility and vector the other aircraft away.
Instruct both aircraft to maintain altitude until the TAWS alert is verified.

items.question_text: Which of the following aircraft identification formats is valid for a flight progress strip?
items.explanation: Military aircraft may use pronounceable words of 3-6 letters followed by a 4-, 3-, 2-, or 1-digit number. 'SAMP316' is a 4-letter word followed by a 3-digit number — 7 characters total, the maximum allowed. 'N12345AB' exceeds 7 characters. 'AMERICAN221' exceeds 7. 'R12345' has too many digits for the single-letter prefix format.

choices.text: SAMP316 — a 4-letter pronounceable word followed by a 3-digit number.
N12345AB — an 8-character civil registration number.
AMERICAN221 — a full company name exceeding seven characters.
R12345 — a military prefix with more than five digits after the letter.

items.question_text: A controller needs to issue a clearance into Class Bravo airspace. Which transmission uses the correct airspace pronunciation?
items.explanation: Class A, B, C, D, E, and G airspace letters are pronounced using ICAO phonetics for clarification. The word 'Class' may be dropped. 'Bravo airspace' correctly uses the ICAO phonetic pronunciation.

choices.text: Cleared to enter Class Bee airspace.
Cleared to enter Bravo airspace.
Cleared to enter Class B airspace.
Cleared to enter Class Baker airspace.

items.question_text: A pilot reports moderate clear air turbulence at FL350. The forecast called for smooth conditions at that altitude. What should the controller do with this PIREP?
items.explanation: Emphasis must be placed on the solicitation and dissemination of PIREPs. Controllers must provide the information in sufficient detail to assist pilots in making decisions pertinent to flight safety. This includes reports of clear air turbulence of moderate or greater intensity, regardless of whether it was forecast.

choices.text: Record it internally — no dissemination is needed since the turbulence was not forecast.
Disseminate the PIREP to potentially affected aircraft in sufficient detail to assist their flight safety decisions.
Forward to the next sector only if the pilot specifically requests dissemination.
Treat it as a routine report with no urgency since clear air turbulence is not convective.

items.question_text: Which of the following uses the correct phraseology format for a weather advisory describing an area of thunderstorms between 11 o'clock and 2 o'clock, 15 to 25 miles from the aircraft?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology is 'WEATHER/CHAFF AREA BETWEEN (number) O'CLOCK AND (number) O'CLOCK (number) MILES, and/or (number) MILE BAND OF WEATHER/CHAFF FROM (fix or distance and direction) TO (fix or distance and direction).'

choices.text: WEATHER AREA BETWEEN ELEVEN O'CLOCK AND TWO O'CLOCK ONE FIVE MILES. TWO ZERO MILE BAND OF WEATHER FROM ONE FIVE TO TWO FIVE MILES AHEAD.
THUNDERSTORMS FROM ELEVEN TO TWO O'CLOCK, FIFTEEN TO TWENTY-FIVE MILES AHEAD.
LINE OF WEATHER TWENTY MILES AHEAD EXTENDING FROM ELEVEN O'CLOCK TO TWO O'CLOCK.
ATTENTION ALL AIRCRAFT, WEATHER AREA BETWEEN ELEVEN O'CLOCK AND TWO O'CLOCK.

items.question_text: Which of the following events requires a controller to make a new ATIS recording?
items.explanation: A new ATIS recording must be made upon receipt of any new official weather regardless of whether values changed, when runway braking action reports indicate worse conditions than in the current broadcast, and when there is a change in other pertinent data such as runway change, approach in use, or new/canceled NOTAMs.

choices.text: Every 30 minutes on a fixed schedule regardless of condition changes.
Upon receipt of any new official weather, when braking action worsens, or when other pertinent data changes.
Only when the ceiling drops below 1,000 feet or visibility drops below 3 miles.
Only when the supervisor authorizes a new recording based on traffic volume.

items.question_text: How is the number '3' pronounced using ICAO phonetics?
items.explanation: Per the ICAO phonetic table, the number 3 is pronounced 'TREE.' The standard ICAO pronunciations for numbers are prescribed to ensure clarity in radiotelephony communications.

choices.text: THREE.
TREE.
THUH-REE.
TRI.

items.question_text: What is the first priority for interphone transmissions?
items.explanation: First priority for interphone transmissions is emergency messages including essential information on aircraft accidents or suspected accidents. After an actual emergency has passed, messages relating to that accident receive a lower priority.

choices.text: Clearances and control instructions.
Emergency messages including essential information on aircraft accidents or suspected accidents.
Movement and control messages such as progress reports.
Movement messages on VFR aircraft.

items.question_text: A local controller is using a certified tower radar display. The controller wants to help a VFR aircraft navigate toward the airport. Which of the following may the controller lawfully do?
items.explanation: A certified tower radar display may be used to provide suggested headings or directions to VFR aircraft as an advisory aid to navigation. The phraseology uses 'PROCEED (direction)-BOUND' or 'SUGGESTED HEADING (degrees)' — these are suggestions, not radar vectors. The note emphasizes the pilot must understand these are advisory in nature.

choices.text: Issue a formal radar vector with a specific heading and altitude assignment.
Provide radar separation services between the VFR aircraft and other IFR traffic.
Provide a suggested heading as an advisory aid to navigation using 'SUGGESTED HEADING' phraseology.
Clear the aircraft for a radar approach to the active runway.

items.question_text: A controller responds to an initial call from an aircraft approaching Class D airspace with the aircraft's call sign followed by 'standby.' Before the controller can provide further service, a runway incursion requires immediate attention. What should the controller do regarding the aircraft waiting to enter?
items.explanation: Responding with the call sign and 'standby' establishes two-way communications, which legally permits the pilot to enter Class D airspace. If workload or traffic conditions then prevent immediate provision of services, the controller must instruct the pilot to REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY. The 'standby' alone permits entry; the explicit 'remain outside' phraseology is needed to keep the aircraft out when services cannot be provided.

choices.text: Do nothing further — the initial 'standby' response already instructs the pilot to wait outside the airspace.
Tell the pilot to contact approach control for entry authorization because the tower is unavailable.
Issue 'REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY' because workload now prevents immediate service provision.
The communications are already established, so the pilot may enter and the controller must fit the aircraft in.

items.question_text: Under what daytime condition must runway edge lights be operated for the runway in use?
items.explanation: Between sunrise and sunset, runway edge lights are turned on following the same operational pattern as the nighttime rule when surface visibility is less than 2 miles. The daytime extension means the lights go on before an aircraft taxies onto the runway (departures) or begins final approach/enters the surface area (arrivals), and stay on until the aircraft leaves the surface area. A common misconception is that runway edge lights are only a nighttime requirement.

choices.text: Only when requested by the pilot, regardless of the reported visibility.
When surface visibility is less than 2 miles, following the same pattern as nighttime operations.
Only when the ceiling is below 1,000 feet or visibility is below 3 miles, whichever occurs first.
Daytime operation of runway edge lights is never required; they are for nighttime use only.

items.question_text: How do the weather conditions required to clear the POFZ differ from those required to clear the final approach OCS?
items.explanation: The POFZ must be clear when the aircraft is within 2 miles of the threshold AND the ceiling is below 300 feet OR visibility is less than 3/4 SM. The final approach OCS must be clear when within 2 miles AND the ceiling is below 800 feet OR visibility is less than 2 SM. The POFZ has stricter weather criteria because it protects a smaller, closer-in volume for aircraft executing an immediate missed approach. The OCS protects a larger volume and triggers at less severe weather minimums.

choices.text: The POFZ uses stricter criteria (ceiling below 300 ft or visibility below 3/4 SM) than the OCS (ceiling below 800 ft or visibility below 2 SM).
Both surfaces use identical weather criteria: ceiling below 300 feet or visibility below 3/4 SM.
The OCS uses stricter criteria than the POFZ because it protects a larger volume.
The POFZ requires only the distance trigger (within 2 miles); the OCS requires only the weather trigger.

items.question_text: At what point does an aircraft cleared for a stop-and-go transition from arriving to departing status?
items.explanation: The paragraph establishes that each maneuver type has a distinct transition point. For a stop-and-go, the aircraft is considered an arriving aircraft until it makes a complete stop; thereafter it is a departing aircraft. For touch-and-go, the transition is at touchdown. For a low approach, it is at crossing the landing threshold. These transition points matter because they determine which separation rules apply.

choices.text: At the moment of touchdown on the runway.
When the aircraft comes to a complete stop.
When the aircraft begins its takeoff roll after the stop.
When the aircraft exits the runway after completing the maneuver.

items.question_text: A preceding aircraft has just landed on Runway 27 and is still rolling out. The controller can see the aircraft is decelerating rapidly and will be clear of the runway well before the next departure, which is holding in position, can begin its takeoff roll. May the controller issue the takeoff clearance now?
items.explanation: The anticipating separation rule permits issuing a takeoff clearance before prescribed separation exists when there is reasonable assurance it will exist at the time the aircraft starts takeoff roll. It does not relax the separation standard — it only permits issuing the clearance earlier than the moment separation is actually achieved.

choices.text: No — a takeoff clearance may never be issued while another aircraft is still on the runway.
Yes, but only if the arriving aircraft has already passed the runway midpoint.
Yes, if there is reasonable assurance that separation will exist when the departure starts its takeoff roll.
No — anticipating separation applies only to landing clearances, not takeoff clearances.

items.question_text: An arriving aircraft reports on a 3-mile final for Runway 18 but the controller cannot visually spot it from the tower. The controller has confirmed via other means that the runway is clear and separation is assured. What should the controller include when issuing the landing clearance?
items.explanation: When an arriving aircraft reports at a position where it should be visible but has not been visually observed, the controller must advise the aircraft as part of the landing clearance that it is not in sight and restate the landing runway. The prescribed phraseology is 'NOT IN SIGHT, RUNWAY (number) CLEARED TO LAND.'

choices.text: Issue a standard landing clearance without modification because the runway has been confirmed clear.
Withhold the landing clearance entirely until visual contact is established with the aircraft.
Instruct the aircraft to go around because visual observation is a prerequisite for any landing clearance.
Include 'NOT IN SIGHT' in the landing clearance and restate the landing runway number.

items.question_text: What is the controller's obligation when an aircraft appears to have committed a regulatory violation while in the traffic pattern?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs controllers not to withhold a landing clearance indefinitely even when it appears a regulatory violation has occurred. The apparent violation might result from an emergency situation. The controller's obligation is to assist the pilot to the extent possible rather than using the landing clearance as an enforcement tool.

choices.text: Withhold the landing clearance and instruct the aircraft to contact the facility by telephone after landing.
Report the violation to the FSDO and withhold services until the investigation is complete.
Do not withhold the landing clearance indefinitely — assist the pilot to the extent possible.
Issue the clearance only after the pilot acknowledges and explains the apparent violation.

items.question_text: A floatplane pilot asks a tower controller to apply the special sea-lane separation provisions from Section 12 for an operation on a river near the airport. The river is an uncontrolled waterway with no formal sea-lane designation. How should the controller respond?
items.explanation: The controlling prerequisite is explicit: Section 12 applies only where sea lanes are established and controlled. An uncontrolled body of water, even if used by floatplanes, does not trigger the section. The controller applies standard control procedures instead.

choices.text: Decline to apply sea-lane provisions because the river lacks an established and controlled sea lane.
Apply the sea-lane provisions as a courtesy since the pilot requested them for a water operation.
Temporarily designate the river as a sea lane for the duration of the pilot's operation.
Apply Section 12 provisions because all water operations by floatplanes automatically qualify as sea-lane operations.

items.question_text: A controller specifies a non-established route for an aircraft. The route passes through controlled airspace for 80 NM, exits controlled airspace for 40 NM, then re-enters controlled airspace for 60 NM. In the altitude band being flown the applicable NAVAID usable radius is 100 NM. Both controlled-airspace segments individually fall within 100 NM. Is the route acceptable under the altitude and distance limitation tables?
items.explanation: The body paragraph states the controller must not exceed the limitations 'on any portion of the route which lies within controlled airspace.' Each controlled-airspace segment is evaluated independently against the applicable table limitations. Since both segments individually respect the 100 NM limit, the route is acceptable. There is no requirement to sum segments across uncontrolled airspace gaps.

choices.text: No. The route must be treated as a single 180 NM path that exceeds the limitation because the aircraft remains on the same flight plan.
No. The total controlled-airspace distance of 140 NM exceeds the 100 NM limit when the two segments are combined.
Yes, but only if the uncontrolled-airspace segment is less than 20 NM wide.
Yes. Each controlled-airspace segment independently respects the 100 NM limit, which is what the 'any portion' rule requires.

items.question_text: A controller exceeds NAVAID altitude and distance limitations for a routing under exception (a): the pilot requested the routing and radar monitoring is provided. Under exception (b), approval from Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Offices was obtained for a different routing. Under exception (c), a third routing uses an MTR. In which cases must the controller ALSO provide the aircraft with information and advice related to significant deviations from the expected flight path?
items.explanation: Note 2 of 4-1-2 states: 'When a clearance is issued beyond the altitude and/or distance limitations of a NAVAID, in addition to being responsible for maintaining separation from other aircraft and airspace, the controller is responsible for providing aircraft with information and advice related to significant deviations from the expected flight path.' This obligation attaches whenever the clearance exceeds the limitations, regardless of which exception authorized it.

choices.text: Exception (a) only, because only radar-monitored routings carry this additional duty.
All three cases — the significant-deviation duty applies whenever the clearance is issued beyond the NAVAID limitations, regardless of which exception is invoked.
Exceptions (a) and (c) only, because exception (b) already involves facility office approval.
Exception (b) only, because operational necessity implies the greatest risk of deviation.

items.question_text: What are the three operational conditions under which altitude and distance limitations are waived?
items.explanation: The three exceptions are: (a) ATC-initiated or pilot-requested routing with radar monitoring, (b) operational necessity with Frequency Management and Flight Inspection Office approval, and (c) requested routing via an MTR. Note 1 provides additional detail for GNSS-equipped aircraft.

choices.text: Pilot request with radar monitoring; operational necessity with office approval; requested routing via an MTR.
Pilot request with radar monitoring; VFR-on-top operations; routing via a published airway.
Operational necessity; VMC conditions; aircraft equipped with dual navigation systems.
Radar monitoring alone; MTR routing alone; pilot request alone.

items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to hold at a fix defined by a VOR. The MVA for the holding area is 4,000 feet, the MEA for the route segment is 6,000 feet, and the L-class VOR's upper altitude limit is below 18,000 feet per TBL 4-1-1. The controller assigns FL190 for the hold, reasoning that this satisfies MVA and MEA requirements and the aircraft is only 20 NM from the VOR. Is the holding altitude consistent with the requirement to use an altitude consistent with the limitations of the aid when clearing an aircraft to cross or hold at a fix?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-1-3 requires the altitude to be 'consistent with the limitations of the aid.' An L-class VOR's normal usable altitude band is below 18,000 feet per TBL 4-1-1. Assigning FL190 exceeds the L-class altitude limitation regardless of MVA, MEA, or distance compliance. MVA/MEA ensure terrain clearance; NAVAID limitations ensure the signal is usable. They serve different purposes and must both be satisfied.

choices.text: Yes. MVA and MEA are the controlling criteria for holding altitude; NAVAID limitations apply only to distance, not altitude.
No. FL190 exceeds the L-class VOR's altitude limitation of below 18,000 feet, regardless of MVA or MEA compliance.
Yes. At only 20 NM from the VOR and with MVA/MEA satisfied, the altitude is operationally sound.
No. Holding altitudes must always be at or below 17,000 feet when using VOR-defined fixes.

items.question_text: A TACAN-only aircraft is operating at FL200 in Class A airspace on an IFR flight plan. The route passes through an area where insufficient TACAN or VORTAC NAVAIDs exist to define the route within service-volume limitations. What should the controller expect regarding the aircraft's ability to maintain VFR-on-top?
items.explanation: The note to 4-1-4 states TACAN-only aircraft are expected to submit VFR-on-top requests 'Except in Class A airspace.' VFR operations are not permitted in Class A airspace. At FL200 in Class A, the VFR-on-top expectation does not apply. The route must be defined within NAVAID service-volume limitations or use another applicable exception from 4-1-2.

choices.text: The controller should solicit a VFR-on-top request from the pilot when the route is issued.
The pilot is expected to request VFR-on-top for the portion of the route with insufficient NAVAID coverage.
The VFR-on-top expectation does not apply because the aircraft is in Class A airspace.
TACAN-only aircraft are exempt from service-volume limitations at and above FL180 regardless of airspace class.

items.question_text: A controller needs to establish an unpublished fix for ATC purposes. The fix can be formed either by the en route radial and a DME distance from the same VORTAC, or by the en route radial and an intersecting radial from an off-route VOR. Both methods are technically feasible. Which method should the controller use?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-1-5b1 states: 'DME must be used in lieu of off-route radials, whenever possible.' This is an explicit preference, not optional. The controller must use the DME method unless DME is unavailable or signal coverage restrictions prevent its use.

choices.text: Use the off-route radial method because intersecting radials produce a uniquely identified geographic point.
Use the off-route radial method because radials provide more precise lateral position definition than DME arcs.
Either method is acceptable; the controller may choose based on operational convenience.
Use the DME method because the rules require DME in lieu of off-route radials whenever possible.

items.question_text: An airborne USAF aircraft receives an altitude assignment. The controller includes the altitude, a heading change, and a frequency change in the same transmission. Is this compliant with the clearance-item rules?
items.explanation: Subparagraph 4-2-1g states that when issuing a clearance to an airborne USAF aircraft containing an altitude assignment, the controller must not include more than one of the following in the same transmission: frequency change, transponder change, heading, altimeter setting, or traffic information containing an altitude. Including both a heading and a frequency change (two listed items) with the altitude assignment violates this rule. Only one listed item may accompany the altitude assignment in a single transmission.

choices.text: Yes. The limitation applies to the number of transmissions, not the number of items within one transmission.
No. Only one of the five listed items may accompany an altitude assignment in the same transmission to an airborne USAF aircraft.
Yes. A heading and a frequency change are both time-critical and must be issued together.
No. No additional items at all may accompany an altitude assignment to an airborne USAF aircraft.

items.question_text: A controller needs to relay a request for information to an aircraft through a non-ATC facility. The controller states the request without any prefix, reasoning that only relayed clearances require the 'A-T-C' prefix. Is this correct?
items.explanation: Subparagraph 4-2-2a requires the prefix for three categories: clearances, information, and requests for information that are relayed through a non-ATC facility. The prefix requirement is not limited to clearances alone. 'A-T-C clears,' 'A-T-C advises,' or 'A-T-C requests' are the three approved prefix forms, each corresponding to a category of relayed communication.

choices.text: Yes. Only relayed clearances require the 'A-T-C' prefix; requests for information may be relayed without a prefix.
No. The prefix requirement applies to relayed clearances, information, and requests for information — 'A-T-C requests' is the correct prefix.
No. A request for information relayed through a non-ATC facility must use 'A-T-C advises' because the information flows in both directions.
Yes. Requests for information are inherently different from clearances and do not need special identification.

items.question_text: What is the correct prefix for relaying an advisory to an aircraft through a non-ATC facility?
items.explanation: The three approved prefixes correspond to the communication type: 'A-T-C clears' for clearances, 'A-T-C advises' for information/advisories, and 'A-T-C requests' for requests for information. For relaying an advisory, 'A-T-C advises' is the correct prefix.

choices.text: A-T-C clears.
A-T-C advises.
A-T-C requests.
A-T-C relays.

items.question_text: A controller has issued an IFR clearance to a general aviation aircraft departing from a busy airport with multiple departure gates. The filed route uses an initial fix that differs from the active runway configuration. What guidance does the controlling rule provide about delivery instructions in this situation?
items.explanation: The controlling text is 'Issue specific clearance delivery instructions, if appropriate.' This means the controller must issue them when circumstances warrant — the 'if appropriate' does not make it optional to ignore operational needs. The second distractor incorrectly assigns sole responsibility to the pilot; the fourth distractor misreads 'if appropriate' as 'always optional.'

choices.text: Do not issue delivery instructions, because the paragraph only applies when the pilot requests clarification.
Issue specific clearance delivery instructions, because the mismatch between the filed route and the active runway configuration makes them appropriate.
Issue the clearance as filed without comment, because the pilot is responsible for navigating from the assigned runway to the filed route.
Do not issue delivery instructions, because the phrase 'if appropriate' makes the entire paragraph optional and controllers may omit it at discretion.

items.question_text: An FSS specialist receives a clearance to relay and notices that the routing contains a VOR name the specialist believes is misspelled because the correct name is the 'Jamestown VOR' and the clearance says 'Jamesburg VOR.' Should the specialist correct the name before relaying the clearance?
items.explanation: The verbatim requirement means the relay must be word-for-word what the issuing controller stated. The FSS specialist should relay exactly as received and separately query the issuing facility if they believe there is a discrepancy. The second distractor is the most plausible error — it seems 'safer' to correct, but the specialist may not know that 'Jamesburg' is in fact a valid fix.

choices.text: Refuse to relay the clearance until the issuing facility confirms the correct VOR name.
Correct the name to 'Jamestown' before relaying, because issuing a clearance with a known wrong name would be unsafe.
Relay the clearance exactly as received and separately query the issuing facility about the suspected error.
Relay with both names to give the pilot the option: 'Cleared via Jamesburg, or possibly Jamestown, VOR.'

items.question_text: How must clearances be relayed from one facility to another?
items.explanation: The controlling rule states: 'Relay clearances verbatim.' The relay facility must transmit exactly what was received, word for word, without paraphrasing, correcting, or summarizing.

choices.text: Verbatim, exactly as received from the issuing controller.
In the relay specialist's own words to ensure clarity.
Summarized to include only the essential routing and altitude information.
With corrections applied for any apparent errors before relaying.

items.question_text: An aircraft was cleared via a SID with published altitude restrictions and a top altitude of FL230. The controller amends the clearance: 'Climb and maintain FL280' without restating the SID altitude restrictions. The aircraft remains on the SID lateral path. What is the effect of restating a maintain altitude without restating previously issued altitude restrictions?
items.explanation: Per 4-2-5b and the accompanying examples, failing to restate altitude restrictions when issuing a new 'maintain' altitude cancels those restrictions. The correct phraseology to preserve them would be 'Climb via SID except maintain FL280.'

choices.text: The SID altitude restrictions remain in effect up to FL230 and the aircraft then continues climb to FL280.
The SID altitude restrictions remain in effect because the aircraft is still on the SID lateral path.
The SID altitude restrictions are canceled because they were not restated in the amended clearance.
The clearance is ambiguous and the controller must clarify whether the restrictions are canceled.

items.question_text: An aircraft is cleared from Airport A to Airport C with an intermediate stop at Airport B. The controller issues: 'Cleared to Airport C, expect stop at Airport B.' Does this satisfy the requirements for a through clearance?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology 'CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix)' structures the clearance with the intermediate stop after 'through' and the final fix after 'to.' The issued transmission reverses this and uses non-standard wording.

choices.text: No. A through clearance requires a separate clearance for each leg of the flight.
Yes. Any transmission that identifies both the intermediate stop and the final destination satisfies the rule.
Yes. The word 'expect' serves the same function as 'through' in identifying the intermediate stop.
No. The prescribed phraseology is 'CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix),' which structures the clearance around the intermediate stop and final destination in that order.

items.question_text: What is the basic phraseology for clearing an aircraft through an intermediate stop?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology is 'CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix).' The intermediate stop follows 'through' and the final destination or fix follows 'to.' This is the standard format for through clearances.

choices.text: CLEARED TO (fix) VIA (airport).
CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix).
CLEARED VIA (airport) TO (fix).
CLEARED TO (fix) WITH STOP AT (airport).

items.question_text: An aircraft is operating under an approved ALTRV for a mission named 'BLUE FLAG.' Separation services have been deferred during the en route cruise phase. Through which flight phases does normal ALTRV coverage extend?
items.explanation: The note explicitly extends ALTRV coverage 'up to and including holding pattern or point/time at which ATC provides separation between aircraft.' The coverage spans departure through arrival — it does not end at top of climb or cruise entry.

choices.text: The ALTRV coverage ends upon initial contact with the arrival facility.
The ALTRV coverage ends at top of climb because after that point the aircraft is in level flight and does not need altitude reservation protection.
The ALTRV coverage ends when the aircraft enters the cruise phase, because the reservation only protects the climb profile.
The ALTRV coverage continues through arrival up to the holding pattern or the point/time ATC provides separation.

items.question_text: When is the phrase 'via approved altitude reservation flight plan' inserted into a clearance?
items.explanation: The body of 4-2-7 states the phrase is used 'if the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.' The ALTRV (Altitude Reservation) must be approved for the phrase to be used.

choices.text: When the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.
Whenever the aircraft is on a military flight plan.
When the aircraft requests a block altitude.
For all flights above FL 600.

items.question_text: An airfile aircraft contacts a center frequency requesting IFR clearance to its destination. The controller is coordinating a handoff for another aircraft and processing a departure release. The airfile aircraft is within the sector's jurisdiction and its flight plan data are available. How should the controller prioritize the airfile request?
items.explanation: The note explicitly states that airfile processing does not have priority over other ATC duties. The controller can sequence the airfile processing among other ongoing duties based on operational priority and workload.

choices.text: The airfile must be processed before the departure release because the airborne aircraft is already in the system.
The airfile must be processed immediately because an aircraft without a clearance in controlled airspace is a separation hazard.
Airfile processing does not have priority over other ATC duties; the controller may complete the handoff and departure release before processing the airfile.
The note has no operational meaning; it is a general disclaimer about controller workload.

items.question_text: What are the three possible outcomes when processing an airfile aircraft request?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-9c lists three outcomes: issue a clearance to the destination, issue a short-range clearance, or instruct the pilot to contact an FSS if the flight plan cannot be processed. Each represents a different level of service based on what ATC can provide.

choices.text: Clearance to destination, short-range clearance, or instruction to contact FSS.
Full route clearance, abbreviated clearance, or hold for release.
Direct clearance, vectored clearance, or radar-handoff clearance.
IFR clearance, VFR clearance, or special VFR clearance.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft requests an IFR clearance while operating 1,000 feet below the minimum IFR altitude. The pilot states that due to scattered clouds they cannot climb to the minimum IFR altitude while remaining VFR. Before issuing any IFR clearance, what must the controller ask?
items.explanation: The mandatory question from 4-2-8d1 must address terrain and obstruction clearance. The other options, while potentially relevant operationally, are not what the paragraph prescribes as the prerequisite question before issuing the clearance.

choices.text: Whether the pilot has filed an alternate airport in case the weather deteriorates further.
Whether the pilot is able to maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.
Whether the pilot can accept a special VFR clearance to climb through the cloud layer.
Whether the pilot is instrument-rated and current for IFR operations.

items.question_text: A ground controller says to a pilot holding short: 'After departure, fly heading zero niner zero.' Is this transmission permitted while the aircraft is holding short?
items.explanation: The rule reserves 'takeoff' only for actual takeoff clearances and takeoff-cancellation instructions. In all other contexts, including heading instructions, the approved substitutes are 'depart,' 'departure,' or 'fly.' The transmission is correct.

choices.text: No. The controller should have said 'After takeoff, fly heading zero niner zero' because heading instructions only apply after the aircraft is airborne.
Yes. The controller used 'departure' instead of 'takeoff,' which is one of the approved substitute terms.
No. The transmission should have used 'fly' without any reference to departure because heading assignments should not reference the takeoff phase.
Yes, but only because the aircraft was holding short; if it had been taxiing, 'takeoff' would have been required.

items.question_text: Which terms should be used instead of 'takeoff' when issuing heading, route, or altitude instructions during taxi or ground operations?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-3-1 specifies 'depart,' 'departure,' or 'fly' as acceptable substitutes for 'takeoff' in clearances. These terms avoid confusion with actual takeoff clearances or takeoff-cancellation instructions.

choices.text: Depart, departure, or fly.
Launch, airborne, or navigate.
Proceed, commence, or initiate.
Roll, lift off, or climb out.

items.question_text: A controller issues a release time of '2215' to a pilot at an airport without an operating control tower. The controller states: 'November One Two Three, released for departure at two two one five.' What additional item must accompany a release time when it uses a specific clock time?
items.explanation: The rule distinguishes between release times using a specific clock time (time check required) and those using a specified number of minutes (no time check required). Since '2215' is a specific clock time, the controller must issue a time check.

choices.text: Yes. Time checks are only required for clearance void times, not for release times.
Yes. Release times never require a time check; the pilot is responsible for maintaining accurate time.
No. A time check is required because the release time was issued as a specific clock time, not as a number of minutes.
No. A release time must always be issued as minutes from now, not as a clock time, to avoid time-check confusion.

items.question_text: What three mechanisms must be issued to departures from an airport without an operating control tower?
items.explanation: The body of 4-3-4 states departures from airports without an operating control tower must be issued 'either a departure release, a hold for release, or a release time.' These are three distinct mechanisms for controlling non-towered IFR departures.

choices.text: A departure release, a hold for release, or a release time.
A clearance void time, a ground stop, or an EDCT.
A departure clearance, a taxi clearance, or a takeoff clearance.
A slot time, a wheels-up time, or a block time.

items.question_text: A ground stop for Chicago O'Hare is in effect. A controller at Milwaukee wants to release a departure whose destination is Detroit, and the aircraft's route passes through Chicago Center's airspace. The controller believes the ground stop applies only to Chicago-destined aircraft. What is the controlling factor for determining whether a ground stop applies to a specific aircraft?
items.explanation: The controlling principle is that the GS applies if it is 'applicable to that aircraft.' The scope is defined by the originator — it could cover a destination, an airspace region, a route, or a time window. The controller must verify scope, not guess.

choices.text: Yes. A ground stop only applies to aircraft whose destination matches the airport named in the ground stop.
Not necessarily. The controller must verify whether the GS scope captures this aircraft; 'applicable to that aircraft' depends on the GS scope, not the destination alone.
No. All ground stops apply to all departures nationwide regardless of destination or route.
Yes. The aircraft can be released because Milwaukee Center, not Chicago Center, controls the departure phase.

items.question_text: Three aircraft elect to take delay on the ground before departure. Aircraft A requested clearance at 1405Z. Aircraft B requested clearance at 1400Z. Aircraft C requested clearance at 1410Z. In what order should they receive departure clearances if practicable?
items.explanation: The rule specifies 'the order in which the requests for clearance were originally made.' B requested first, then A, then C. The qualifier 'if practicable' does not convert this to unlimited discretion.

choices.text: C (1410Z), then A (1405Z), then B (1400Z) — reverse order so the longest-waiting aircraft departs last.
B (1400Z), then A (1405Z), then C (1410Z) — the order in which clearance requests were made.
Any order the controller chooses; delay sequencing is entirely at controller discretion.
A (1405Z), then B (1400Z), then C (1410Z) — in the order they called, not the order they requested clearance.

items.question_text: An ARTCC controller anticipates that departures from a satellite airport will experience 20-minute delays due to volume at the departure gate. Which facilities must the controller inform of anticipated departure delays?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs the controller to inform the relevant facilities of anticipated delays. There is no minimum delay threshold, no requirement to wait for delays to materialize, and no exemption for towers. The obligation is anticipatory — 'anticipated departure delays.'

choices.text: Inform only the approach control facility because towers do not need departure delay information.
Wait until the delay actually materializes before informing other facilities.
Inform the tower and any affected approach control facilities of the anticipated departure delays.
Do nothing — delay information only needs to be forwarded when delays exceed 30 minutes.

items.question_text: What does a flashing 'DM' in the data block signify?
items.explanation: Note 2 to paragraph 4-3-10 states 'the letters DM flashing in the data block signify unsuccessful transmission of a departure message.' The controller must then manually forward the departure time.

choices.text: Unsuccessful transmission of a departure message.
The departure time has been successfully transmitted.
A delayed departure requiring controller action.
A data mismatch between the flight plan and the clearance.

items.question_text: A departure point is 10 minutes flying time from the transferring facility's boundary. A letter of agreement exists between the two facilities that specifies automatic coordination for all departures. Must the controller coordinate with the receiving facility before this aircraft's departure?
items.explanation: The 15-minute coordination requirement is subject to the exception for active automated procedures. The letter of agreement providing automated coordination satisfies this, so manual pre-departure coordination is not required.

choices.text: Yes. The letter of agreement only covers departures beyond 15 minutes flying time from the boundary.
Yes. The 15-minute rule applies regardless of any letter of agreement; all departures within 15 minutes require manual coordination.
No. The letter of agreement providing automated coordination procedures satisfies the exception to the manual coordination requirement.
No. At only 10 minutes, the departure point is so close that the controller may assume the receiving facility is aware.

items.question_text: A pilot at a non-towered airport has filed an IFR flight plan but requests a VFR departure due to improving weather. The controller obtains the pilot's departure intentions and a void time. The controller also coordinates with the clearance delivery facility. Before releasing the aircraft, what else must the controller obtain from the pilot?
items.explanation: Per 4-3-9a, the controller must obtain the pilot's statement that VFR conditions exist for the departure to the IFR activation point. This ensures the pilot is not accepting an IFR clearance they cannot safely reach under VFR.

choices.text: The pilot's instrument approach currency status to ensure the IFR portion can be completed.
The pilot's estimated time en route to the destination, to compute the IFR clearance validity period.
Confirmation that the pilot has filed an alternate airport in case VFR conditions deteriorate.
A statement from the pilot confirming that VFR conditions exist for the departure to the IFR activation point.

items.question_text: What must a controller obtain from a pilot before issuing a VFR release of an IFR departure?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-3-9a requires obtaining a statement from the pilot confirming that the departure can be accomplished in VFR conditions to the point where the IFR clearance will be activated. This ensures the pilot can safely reach IFR conditions.

choices.text: A statement that the departure can be accomplished in VFR conditions to the IFR activation point.
The pilot's instrument currency information and medical certificate date.
Confirmation that an alternate airport is available within 30 minutes flying time.
The pilot's total IFR flight time within the preceding 90 days.

items.question_text: A terminal controller observes 'DM' flashing in the data block for a departure. The controller decides to manually forward the departure time to the receiving facility. Is manual forwarding required, or is it merely precautionary?
items.explanation: A flashing DM indicates unsuccessful transmission. The automated procedure exception is no longer in effect because the automated message failed. Manual forwarding is the correct response.

choices.text: No. The DM indicator means the departure message was delayed, not failed; manual forwarding would create a duplicate.
No. The flashing DM is informational only and the controller should wait for the system to retry automatically.
Yes, and this is the correct response because the flashing DM indicates the automated departure message was not successfully transmitted.
Yes, but only if the receiving facility specifically requests the departure time after seeing the DM indicator.

items.question_text: A military flight plan for operations at FL200 proposes a degree-distance route with fixes 300 miles apart. The NAVAIDs selected are authorized for use at that altitude and are within published service volume. Should the controller accept this flight plan?
items.explanation: The distance limits are altitude-dependent: below FL 180 the maximum is 80 miles; at FL 180 and above it is 260 miles; celestial navigation routes are 260 miles at all altitudes. FL200 is above FL 180, so the 260-mile limit applies.

choices.text: Yes. As long as the NAVAIDs are authorized for FL200 and within service volume, any fix spacing is acceptable.
No. At FL200, the maximum distance between degree-distance fixes is 260 miles; 300 miles exceeds this limit.
Yes. The 260-mile limit only applies to celestial navigation routes, not degree-distance routes.
No. At FL200, the maximum distance between fixes is 80 miles because FL200 is below the transition altitude.

items.question_text: A NAVAID outage makes a portion of an airway unusable. The controller clears a non-RNAV aircraft via a different published airway using the word 'substitute' before the route. An RNAV-capable aircraft on the same airway proceeds to the NAVAID location beyond the outage. What must the controller tell the RNAV aircraft about the NAVAID?
items.explanation: The note creates a duty to inform RNAV aircraft proceeding to the NAVAID location that the NAVAID is out of service. While RNAV capability means the aircraft does not need the NAVAID signal, the pilot still needs to know the facility status.

choices.text: Yes. The substitute route for the non-RNAV aircraft was handled correctly; RNAV aircraft are handled separately with no informational requirement.
Yes. RNAV aircraft do not rely on ground-based NAVAIDs for navigation and therefore do not need outage information.
No. The controller must inform the RNAV aircraft of the NAVAID outage even though the aircraft can navigate to the location without using the NAVAID signal.
No. The controller should have also cleared the RNAV aircraft via the substitute airway, because all aircraft must avoid the outage area.

items.question_text: When an airway or route is unusable due to NAVAID status, what word must immediately precede an alternative published route in the clearance?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-4a specifies: 'Use the word substitute immediately preceding the alternative route in issuing the clearance.' This signals to the pilot that the assigned route replaces the normally-used route that is unavailable.

choices.text: Substitute.
Alternate.
Via.
Amended.

items.question_text: A pilot files a flight plan with an MTR segment that passes through Class G airspace. The pilot later asks the controller to remove the MTR segment from the route. The controller agrees but continues to include a direct routing through the same Class G airspace. What authorization is needed to continue routing the aircraft through Class G airspace after the MTR segment is removed?
