items.explanation: The body rule is: include Class G routes only when requested by the pilot. The notes create implicit requests through specific filing actions (MTR segments, random RNAV routes). If the filing action that created the implicit request is removed, the pilot must separately request Class G routing.

choices.text: Yes. The original filing through Class G airspace constitutes a permanent authorization for that routing.
Yes. Once Class G routing is established in a flight plan, it can be maintained regardless of subsequent route changes.
No. With the MTR segment removed, the implicit pilot request for Class G routing no longer exists unless the pilot separately requests it.
No. Class G airspace routing is never permitted for IFR aircraft regardless of pilot request.

items.question_text: What flight plan filing actions constitute an automatic pilot request for routing through Class G airspace?
items.explanation: Notes 2 and 3 to 4-4-5 identify two implicit requests: filing random RNAV routes through Class G airspace, and filing MTR segments in or through Class G airspace. In both cases, the filing itself is treated as a pilot request for Class G routing.

choices.text: Filing a random RNAV route through Class G or filing MTR segments through Class G.
Filing any IFR flight plan that transits uncontrolled airspace.
Specifically requesting 'Class G routing' in the remarks section.
Filing a flight plan that originates or terminates at an airport in Class G airspace.

items.question_text: An ARTCC controller wants to issue a direct routing that takes an aircraft off its flight plan route past the last fix in the facility's airspace to a fix in the adjacent center's airspace. The controller believes saving 10 minutes of flight time justifies the change, but the pilot has made no such request and operational necessity is absent. Under what conditions is a controller permitted to issue routing changes past the last fix in facility airspace?
items.explanation: The rule in 4-4-6b is explicit: do not issue routing changes past the last fix in your airspace unless requested by the pilot or operational necessity dictates. Neither exception applies. Efficiency alone is not operational necessity.

choices.text: Yes. Saving 10 minutes of flight time is always sufficient justification for a direct clearance.
No. The controller may not issue a direct clearance past the last fix in the facility's airspace unless the pilot requests it or operational necessity dictates.
Yes. The controller may issue the clearance and coordinate with the receiving facility afterwards.
No. Direct clearances that cross facility boundaries are never permitted under any circumstances.

items.question_text: What coordination is required when a flight routing within a protected segment is amended by an ARTCC TMU for amendments outside their facility?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-6a2(c) requires ARTCC TMU to coordinate with ATCSCC for amendments outside their facility. The coordination chain escalates with the scope of the amendment: internal (ATCS with TMU), terminal (with overlying ARTCC TMU), and external ARTCC (with ATCSCC).

choices.text: Coordinate with ATCSCC.
Coordinate with the affected terminal facility TMU.
No coordination is required for ARTCC TMU amendments.
Coordinate with the destination airport tower.

items.question_text: Two military aircraft are operating at FL630. One is supersonic and the other is subsonic. What vertical separation minimum applies between them?
items.explanation: The rule hierarchy is: general above FL410 = 2,000 feet; oceanic supersonic above FL450 = 4,000 feet; military above FL600 = 5,000 feet. The military-above-FL600 rule (most specific) controls when both aircraft are military and the altitude exceeds FL600.

choices.text: 4,000 feet, because one aircraft is supersonic and the oceanic separation standard should be applied.
5,000 feet, because both aircraft are military and operating above FL 600.
2,000 feet, which is the standard above-FL410 minimum.
1,000 feet, because above FL 600 the standard vertical separation reverts to the up-to-FL410 value.

items.question_text: A controller at an ARTCC sector needs to assign an even cardinal altitude to an eastbound aircraft at FL350 due to traffic conditions. The aircraft will proceed beyond the facility's area into an adjacent center's airspace. There is a letter of agreement between the two facilities covering random altitude assignments for traffic-condition exceptions. May the controller assign the even altitude?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-3a2, when aircraft will proceed beyond the facility's area, random altitude assignments are permitted only if covered in an LOA between the facilities. The LOA here satisfies that condition.

choices.text: No. Even altitudes are never assignable to eastbound aircraft, regardless of traffic conditions or agreements.
No. The traffic-condition exception only applies to aircraft that remain within the facility's area.
Yes, but only if the controller also obtains verbal approval from the adjacent center's sector controller at the time of assignment.
Yes. The LOA between the facilities covering random altitude assignments satisfies the condition for aircraft proceeding beyond the facility's area.

items.question_text: When traffic conditions prevent assignment of directionally-prescribed altitudes, what altitude may be assigned below FL 410?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-5-3 permits assigning 'any cardinal altitude or flight level below FL 410 or any odd cardinal flight level at or above FL 410 without regard to direction of flight' when traffic, weather, or aircraft limitations prevent standard directional assignment, subject to the coordination requirements in subparagraphs a-e.

choices.text: Any cardinal altitude or flight level without regard to direction of flight.
Only the altitude immediately above the conflicting traffic.
Only odd cardinal altitudes, same as standard eastbound assignment.
Any altitude at or above FL 410 only.

items.question_text: The altimeter setting in a controller's area is 28.85 inches. According to TBL 4-5-2, what is the lowest usable flight level?
items.explanation: The altimeter setting 28.85 falls in the 28.91-27.92 band, which corresponds to lowest usable FL 200 per TBL 4-5-2. The bands are fixed ranges; values do not round between bands.

choices.text: FL 190 (29.91 to 28.92 inches band).
FL 200 (28.91 to 27.92 inches band).
FL 180 (29.92 or higher).
FL 210, because 28.85 rounds up to the next pressure band.

items.question_text: What is the lowest usable flight level when the altimeter setting is 29.50 inches?
items.explanation: TBL 4-5-2: altimeter settings 29.92 or higher = FL 180; 29.91 to 28.92 = FL 190; 28.91 to 27.92 = FL 200. At 29.50 inches, the setting falls in the 29.91-to-28.92 band, making FL 190 the lowest usable flight level.

items.question_text: The prescribed minimum IFR altitude for a route segment is 18,000 feet MSL. The current altimeter setting is 29.35 inches. According to TBL 4-5-3, what is the adjusted minimum flight level?
items.explanation: TBL 4-5-3: altimeter 29.35 falls in the 29.41-28.92 band, requiring a 1,000-foot adjustment. FL 180 + 1,000 feet = FL 190 (odd cardinal, which is also directionally correct). The 500-foot band is 29.91-29.42, which is a different range.

choices.text: FL 180 (minimum altitude with no adjustment needed).
FL 190 (FL 180 plus 1,000 feet for the 29.41 to 28.92 band).
FL 185 (FL 180 plus 500 feet for the 29.91 to 29.42 band).
FL 195 (FL 180 plus 1,500 feet for the 28.91 to 28.42 band).

items.question_text: When the altimeter setting is 29.35 inches and the minimum IFR altitude is 18,000 feet MSL, what adjustment factor from TBL 4-5-3 must be applied?
items.explanation: TBL 4-5-3: altimeter 29.41 to 28.92 inches requires adding 1,000 feet. At 29.35 inches, the setting falls in this band. The flight level equivalent of 18,000 feet (FL 180) plus 1,000 feet gives FL 190 as the adjusted minimum.

choices.text: 500 feet.
1,000 feet.
1,500 feet.
2,000 feet.

items.question_text: An aircraft at FL320 needs to climb to FL360, but traffic prevents the climb for approximately 40 miles. The controller tells the pilot: 'Expect higher in four zero miles.' Is a distance-based expectation appropriate in this situation?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES' explicitly allows both miles and minutes. The controller correctly used the mileage format. The paragraph does not restrict the distance or impose FL290-based limitations.

choices.text: No. The controller should have issued 'REQUEST ALTITUDE CHANGE FROM (next facility)' because the climb cannot be accomplished in the current sector.
Yes. The controller used the prescribed phraseology and format to inform the pilot when to expect the climb clearance.
No. The controller should have used minutes instead of miles because altitude changes are time-based expectations.
Yes, but only if the aircraft is above FL 290; below FL 290, altitude changes cannot be anticipated more than 20 miles ahead.

items.question_text: What is the prescribed phraseology to inform an aircraft when to expect a higher altitude in 40 miles?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology is 'EXPECT HIGHER/LOWER IN (number of miles or minutes) MILES/MINUTES.' The correct format is 'EXPECT HIGHER IN FOUR ZERO MILES.' The alternatives use incorrect wording or wrong distances.

choices.text: EXPECT HIGHER IN FOUR ZERO MILES.
CLIMB IN FOUR ZERO MILES.
EXPECT CLIMB AT FOUR ZERO MILES.
HIGHER ALTITUDE IN FOUR ZERO MILES.

items.question_text: A nonradar controller receives an initial contact from an aircraft at FL240. The pilot states: 'Center, November One Two Three, level at flight level two four zero.' What are the circumstances under which a controller must request a pilot to confirm assigned altitude when the pilot has already volunteered the altitude information?
items.explanation: Exception 1 to the altitude confirmation requirement is that 'the pilot states the assigned altitude.' The pilot stating 'level at flight level two four zero' communicates the assigned altitude without being prompted, satisfying the exception. The controller is not required to independently verify what the pilot already provided.

choices.text: Yes, but only because the aircraft is at FL240, which is a flight level — the exception is limited to flight level operations.
No. The pilot must use the exact phrase 'assigned altitude' for the exception to apply.
No. The exception only applies when the controller assigns a new altitude to the aircraft.
Yes. The pilot stated the assigned altitude on initial contact, which is one of the listed exceptions to the verification requirement.

items.question_text: A flight's assigned route includes a charted holding pattern at the clearance limit. The controller tells the pilot: 'Cleared to DRYER, hold as published.' The pilot requests complete holding instructions. When a pilot requests complete holding instructions for a charted pattern, what must the controller provide?
items.explanation: The rule has two parts: (1) the controller may omit instructions for charted patterns except direction and 'as published,' but (2) 'Always issue complete holding instructions when the pilot requests them.' The pilot's request overrides the charted-pattern shortcut unconditionally.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the holding pattern is not depicted on the pilot's current chart cycle.
No. Since the pattern is charted and the pilot has been told to hold as published, the controller has met all requirements.
No. The pilot should look at the chart; the controller only needs to provide holding instructions for unpublished patterns.
Yes. The rule unconditionally requires the controller to issue complete holding instructions when the pilot requests them.

items.question_text: A controller expects no delay for an aircraft approaching its clearance limit. The aircraft is 3 minutes from the fix, and the clearance beyond the fix is ready. Is issuing it at this time appropriate?
items.explanation: The rule has both an 'as soon as possible' obligation and a 'whenever possible, at least 5 minutes' preference. The 5 minutes is a target, not an absolute requirement. Issuing the clearance late is still better than not issuing it and forcing a hold.

choices.text: The controller should wait until the aircraft reaches the fix to issue the clearance beyond it.
The clearance is too late; the aircraft must hold at the fix because the 5-minute requirement is mandatory.
The timing is perfectly compliant; 'at least 5 minutes' is a maximum, not a minimum.
The controller should issue the clearance now, but the preferred timing of at least 5 minutes before the fix has passed.

items.question_text: Arrival delays at an airport are expected to reach 30 minutes. The airport has ATIS broadcasting the delay information. An arriving aircraft enters the center's area and the pilot makes no inquiry about delays. What conditions allow a controller to omit issuing delay information under the 30-minute arrival delay rule?
items.explanation: The exception in 4-6-3b3 allows omission when both conditions are met: the pilot hasn't requested the information, and ATIS is broadcasting it. The 30-minute trigger in b requires action, but b3 provides the ATIS-based exception to that action.

choices.text: No. ATIS can only substitute for controller-issued delay information when delays are less than 20 minutes.
Yes. The 30-minute threshold makes delay information mandatory regardless of ATIS availability.
Yes. The controller must issue delay information because it is the first center controller communicating with the aircraft.
No. The controller may omit the delay information because the pilot did not request it and ATIS is broadcasting it.

items.question_text: A controller issues holding instructions specifying the direction, fix, radial, and leg length. Standard right turns are to be used, and neither the pilot nor the controller considers turn direction necessary. Under what specific circumstances must the direction of holding pattern turns be specified?
items.explanation: Turn direction is specified 'only if left turns are to be made, the pilot requests it, or you consider it necessary.' Standard right turns with no pilot request and no controller concern means turn direction is correctly omitted.

choices.text: No. Leg length in miles makes the instruction incomplete; leg length must be specified in minutes for all holdings.
No. Turn direction must always be specified in holding instructions regardless of whether turns are standard.
Yes. Turn direction is only required when left turns are used, the pilot requests it, or the controller considers it necessary.
Yes, but only because DME leg length was specified; without DME, turn direction is mandatory.

items.question_text: A controller wants to use a water tower as a holding fix for a VFR aircraft. The pilot states he is familiar with the area and can identify the water tower visually. The controller issues: 'Hold at the water tower until one five three zero.' Is this a valid use of a visual holding point?
items.explanation: The rule requires pilot familiarity and visual identification capability — both are met. There is no IFR-only restriction, no chart requirement, and no time limit for visual holding points.

choices.text: No. Visual holding points may only be used for IFR aircraft, not VFR aircraft.
Yes. The pilot confirmed familiarity with the visual reference point and the controller used the prescribed phraseology format.
No. Visual holding points require a published chart reference in addition to pilot familiarity.
Yes, but only if the holding is limited to 10 minutes or less.

items.question_text: A pilot holding at a fix requests to deviate from the prescribed holding flight path to avoid building cumulus clouds. Traffic in the holding pattern includes one aircraft at a higher altitude, and no known obstacles exist in the requested deviation area. May the controller approve the deviation?
items.explanation: The rule has only two conditions: obstacles and traffic must permit the deviation. Weather avoidance is a valid operational reason; the rule does not limit deviation approval to emergencies.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the pilot declares an emergency or minimum fuel status.
No. Deviations from prescribed holding flight paths are never permitted under any circumstances.
No. Weather avoidance is not a valid reason for holding pattern deviation — only emergencies qualify.
Yes. Obstacles and traffic conditions both permit the deviation as required by the paragraph.

items.question_text: Under what two conditions may a controller approve a pilot's request to deviate from a prescribed holding flight path?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-6-6 states the controller may approve the deviation 'if obstacles and traffic conditions permit.' Both conditions must be satisfied for approval.

choices.text: If obstacles and traffic conditions permit.
If the pilot declares an emergency or minimum fuel.
If the deviation reduces the holding pattern by at least 50 percent.
If the weather is VMC and the aircraft is in radar contact.

items.question_text: An aircraft is holding at an unmonitored NAVAID. If the NAVAID fails, the holding aircraft will drift onto a course occupied by another aircraft. What separation obligation does the controller have for an aircraft holding at an unmonitored NAVAID?
items.explanation: The rule requires specific separation from aircraft on the course the holding aircraft would follow upon NAVAID signal loss. This means the controller must protect that course — it is a specific duty beyond standard IFR separation.

choices.text: Monitor the NAVAID signal personally since it is unmonitored.
Issue a new clearance to the holding aircraft because unmonitored NAVAIDs cannot be used for holding.
Separate the holding aircraft from the other aircraft by ensuring the lost-signal course is not occupied.
No special action is required; standard IFR separation applies without modification.

items.question_text: The weather at an airport is reported as ceiling 600 feet and visibility 1.5 miles. An aircraft requests to hold at the ILS outer marker at 3,000 feet AGL, inbound toward the airport, directly on the localizer course, 0.5 NM from the localizer antenna. Is the controller authorized to approve this holding location?
items.explanation: All elements of the restriction are met: ceiling < 800 ft, visibility < 2 SM, holding below 5,000 ft AGL, on/within 1 SM of the localizer, inbound toward the airport. The USAF note adds a narrower scope only for that service; the base rule applies to all operations.

choices.text: Yes. The restriction applies only to USAF operations and this is a civilian aircraft.
Yes. The restriction only applies to aircraft between the OM and the runway, and 3,000 feet AGL is above the restriction altitude.
No. The ceiling is below 800 feet and visibility is below 2 miles, triggering the ILS protection restriction for holding below 5,000 feet AGL on or within 1 SM of the localizer.
No. Holding is never permitted at the OM regardless of weather conditions.

items.question_text: An aircraft is landing at an airport located in an adjacent center's control area near the common boundary. The controller at the first center needs to issue a lower altitude. What two actions must the controller take when exercising control of aircraft landing at an airport in an adjacent center's area near the boundary?
items.explanation: Both subparagraphs (a) and (b) create duties: coordinate with the receiving facility AND start early enough for normal descent. Coordination without issuing, or issuing without coordination, are both incomplete.

choices.text: Issue the descent clearance immediately, then coordinate with the receiving facility after the aircraft has started descent.
Transfer communications to the adjacent center and let that facility issue the descent clearance.
Coordinate with the receiving facility for the lower altitude and initiate the action at a sufficient distance for normal descent and speed reduction.
Coordinate with the receiving facility only; the adjacent center issues the actual descent clearance.

items.question_text: A military turbojet aircraft is in a nonradar environment, and the controller plans to use the en route descent procedure. Radar capability exists to vector the aircraft to the final approach course of a published high altitude instrument approach. However, the controller anticipates a 10-minute vectoring delay due to traffic volume. May the en route descent procedure be used?
items.explanation: The paragraph has a two-part test for nonradar use: (1) radar capability must exist for the vector, AND (2) the controller must not anticipate other-than-normal vectoring delays. Both must be satisfied.

choices.text: Yes. The delay only prohibits use in a radar environment; in a nonradar environment, the delay restriction does not apply.
Yes. Radar capability exists to vector the aircraft, which satisfies the only condition for using the procedure.
No. The 10-minute vectoring delay is 'other than normal,' which specifically prohibits use of this procedure.
No. The en route descent procedure can never be used in a nonradar environment.

items.question_text: Who is responsible for requesting a high altitude approach when normal arrival handling is unsuitable for a military turbojet aircraft?
items.explanation: The note to 4-7-5 states: 'It is the responsibility of the pilot to request a high altitude approach if he/she does not want normal arrival handling.' The controller provides normal arrival procedures by default; the pilot must affirmatively request the high altitude alternative.

choices.text: The pilot, who must request a high altitude approach.
The controller, who must offer it to all military aircraft.
The operations officer, who must specify it in the flight plan remarks.
The receiving facility, which determines the approach type.

items.question_text: An en route center controller has an arriving aircraft destined for an airport where the reported ceiling is 800 feet and visibility is 2 miles. The aircraft is being handed off to approach control and the weather will be transmitted through a nonapproach control facility. What must the controller do regarding weather information?
items.explanation: Both the ceiling threshold (below 1,000 feet, or whichever is higher between 1,000 feet and the highest circling minimum) and the visibility threshold (less than 3 miles) are triggered. The en route center is responsible for the transmission when it communicates directly with the pilot or relays through an authorized station.

choices.text: Tell the pilot to request weather from the destination ATIS upon arrival.
Do nothing — weather transmission is the responsibility of the approach control facility receiving the handoff.
Transmit the weather report and any special observations because both ceiling (below 1,000 feet) and visibility (below 3 miles) trigger the requirement.
Transmit only the ceiling because visibility at 2 miles is above the 1-mile threshold for mandatory weather reporting.

items.question_text: What weather conditions trigger the requirement to transmit weather reports to arriving aircraft prior to or as part of the approach clearance?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-7 triggers the weather transmission requirement when the ceiling is below 1,000 feet (USAF: 1,500 feet) or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is higher, or when visibility is less than 3 miles. Both ceiling and visibility have independent trigger thresholds.

choices.text: Ceiling below 1,000 feet (or highest circling minimum) or visibility less than 3 miles.
Ceiling below 500 feet or visibility less than 1 mile.
Any IFR conditions at the destination airport.
Whenever the destination ATIS is out of service.

items.question_text: An arriving aircraft reports that weather conditions at the destination are below the pilot's landing minima. What are the controller's required actions upon receiving a pilot report of below-minima weather at the destination?
items.explanation: The controller must issue alternatives (hold or alternate airport) and manage the landing sequence. The note makes clear that the pilot determines weather adequacy for landing, but the controller must still provide operational alternatives.

choices.text: Immediately clear the aircraft for the approach since determining weather adequacy is the pilot's responsibility.
Issue instructions to hold or proceed to another airport, and adjust the landing sequence of other aircraft as necessary.
Declare an emergency on behalf of the aircraft and provide priority handling.
Ask the pilot to attempt the approach anyway because weather observations can be inaccurate.

items.question_text: An aircraft is 8 NM from its clearance limit. The receiving facility needs to issue a clearance beyond that limit. When should the transferring controller initiate the communications transfer?
items.explanation: The timing standard is functional, not fixed: early enough for the receiving facility to issue the clearance before the limit. No specific distance or altitude is prescribed — the controller must judge based on aircraft speed, workload, and the receiving facility's needs.

choices.text: Exactly at the clearance limit, because control responsibility cannot transfer before the limit is reached.
Early enough that the receiving facility can clear the aircraft beyond the limit before the aircraft reaches it.
At least 10 NM before the clearance limit, as prescribed by standard transfer procedures.
As soon as the aircraft enters the receiving facility's airspace, regardless of distance to the clearance limit.

items.question_text: An en route controller knows that the ILS glideslope at the destination airport is out of service. The ATIS broadcast includes this information. The pilot checks in and states the ATIS code. Must the controller separately inform the pilot of the glideslope outage?
items.explanation: The rule provides a two-condition exception: ATIS contains the information AND the pilot states the ATIS code. Both are satisfied. There is no separate mandatory-notification requirement for glideslope outages beyond what the ATIS exception covers.

choices.text: Yes. The ATIS code confirmation only exempts surface wind and altimeter information, not navigational aid status.
Yes. Glideslope outages are a mandatory separate notification regardless of ATIS content.
No. The information may be omitted because it is contained in the ATIS broadcast and the pilot has confirmed receipt of the ATIS code.
No, but only if the ATIS broadcast is less than 30 minutes old.

items.question_text: A STAR connects to an IAP at fix TULEX, which is an Intermediate Fix. The aircraft is on the STAR at 8,000 feet. At what minimum distance from TULEX must the controller issue the approach clearance, and what must be specified?
items.explanation: The rule applies equally to IAFs and IFs: 'at least 3 NM prior to the IAF/IF and specify the name of the connection fix.' The altitude-to-maintain requirement applies only to ATS Route arrivals, not STAR arrivals. Since the aircraft is on a STAR, only the fix name is required.

choices.text: At least 3 NM before TULEX, with the fix name and an altitude to maintain until the fix.
At least 5 NM before TULEX, with the fix name and the altitude to maintain.
At the IAF only; IFs do not qualify for the 3 NM advance clearance rule.
At least 3 NM before TULEX, specifying the connection fix name in the clearance.

items.question_text: An approach procedure chart shows a mandatory altitude of 3,000 feet at the final approach fix. The controller needs this altitude for separation from another aircraft on a missed approach from a crossing runway. Must the controller specify 3,000 feet in the approach clearance?
items.explanation: The trigger for specifying the altitude is when 'adherence to that altitude is required for separation.' Since separation from another aircraft depends on this altitude, the controller must specify it. Published altitudes alone do not guarantee the controller needs them for separation.

choices.text: No, because mandatory altitudes on approach charts are automatically part of every approach clearance without restatement.
No, because the altitude is already published on the approach chart and the pilot is expected to comply with published mandatory altitudes.
Yes, because the controller needs the mandatory altitude for separation from another aircraft.
Yes, but only if the pilot has not previously flown this approach procedure.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft intends to land at an airport without an operating control tower. The aircraft is on final approach at 5 NM, and the controller no longer requires direct communications. The pilot requests a frequency change to the CTAF. What is the correct controller response?
items.explanation: The rule is permissive ('approve a change') when the controller no longer requires direct communications. The prescribed phraseology is 'Change to advisory frequency approved.' There is no requirement for the aircraft to report back or to use FSS instead.

choices.text: Approve the change but require the pilot to report back on frequency after landing.
Deny the request because IFR aircraft must remain on the approach frequency until landing is completed.
Approve the change with 'Change to advisory frequency approved.'
Transfer communications to flight service instead of the CTAF.

items.question_text: Under what condition may a controller approve an IFR aircraft's request to change to the advisory frequency at a non-towered airport?
items.explanation: The controller may approve the frequency change 'when you no longer require direct communications' with the aircraft. This ensures the controller has completed all necessary IFR control instructions before releasing the aircraft to the CTAF.

choices.text: When the controller no longer requires direct communications.
When the aircraft reports the airport in sight.
Immediately upon request, regardless of controller needs.
Only after the aircraft has landed.

items.question_text: An IFR aircraft on an ILS approach executes a missed approach at the decision altitude. The controller needs the aircraft to follow the published alternate missed approach procedure instead of the primary procedure. The alternate procedure appears on FAA Form 8260 but requires controller issuance of the full description because it is pending NOTAM activation. Under what condition may the controller omit issuing the full description of an alternate missed approach procedure?
items.explanation: The NOTAM exception in 4-8-9c only relieves the controller of the duty to issue the full description when the alternate is 'published and in effect via NOTAM.' Without NOTAM activation, the full Form 8260 description must be issued per 4-8-9b.

choices.text: Issue only the missed approach point and altitude, because additional details are available on the approach chart.
Simply tell the pilot to fly the alternate missed approach — since the procedure is published on Form 8260, the pilot has access to it.
The alternate procedure cannot be used because it is not activated by NOTAM.
Issue the full description of the alternate missed approach procedure as published on the FAA Form 8260, because it is not activated by NOTAM.

items.question_text: A controller's sector is saturated with IFR arrivals and departures. A VFR aircraft requests a practice instrument approach. The controller cannot approve it due to workload. What must the controller do?
items.explanation: The rule requires two things when denying a practice approach due to workload: state the reason and give the anticipated delay if applicable. The delay must not be indefinite. There is no fixed delay standard; the controller provides a realistic estimate.

choices.text: Deny the request without explanation; controllers are not required to explain workload-based denials.
Advise the pilot of the reason for the denial and, if possible, provide the anticipated delay before the approach can be approved.
Approve the approach despite workload, because practice approaches cannot be refused under any circumstances.
Tell the pilot to try again in 15 minutes; the paragraph specifies a standard 15-minute delay for all practice approach denials.

items.question_text: When a controller cannot approve a practice instrument approach due to workload, what two things must be communicated to the pilot?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-11a requires: 'advise the pilot of the reason, and if applicable, the anticipated delay until the operation can be approved.' The reason must always be given; the delay estimate is required when applicable.

choices.text: The reason for the denial and, if applicable, the anticipated delay.
The reason for the denial and an alternate airport suggestion.
The anticipated delay and the frequency for the next sector.
The reason for the denial and the supervisor's name.

items.question_text: What are the three basic categories of routes via which aircraft may be cleared for IFR route assignments?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-4-1 lists three methods: (a) designated ATS routes, (b) radials/courses/azimuths to or from NAVAIDs, and (c) random routes. Each method has specific phraseology and requirements described in the subparagraphs.

choices.text: Designated ATS routes; radials/courses/azimuths to or from NAVAIDs; and random routes.
Direct routes; victor airways; and jet routes only.
Published airways; unpublished airways; and GPS-direct routing.
Low-altitude routes; high-altitude routes; and transition routes.

items.question_text: What altitude should be assigned to an aircraft at or below FL 410 on a magnetic course of 090 degrees?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1, aircraft on magnetic courses 0 through 179 degrees (eastbound) at or below FL 410 are assigned odd cardinal altitudes or flight levels at intervals of 2,000 feet (e.g., 3,000, 5,000, FL 310, FL 330). Course 090 is eastbound, so an odd cardinal altitude is required.

choices.text: An odd cardinal altitude or flight level (e.g., 5,000, FL 310, FL 330).
An even cardinal altitude or flight level (e.g., 4,000, FL 320, FL 340).
Any altitude the controller considers appropriate for traffic flow.
An altitude at or above FL 410 only.

items.question_text: What altitude should be assigned to an aircraft on a magnetic course of 270 degrees at or below FL 410?
items.explanation: Per TBL 4-5-1, aircraft on magnetic courses 180 through 359 degrees (westbound) at or below FL 410 are assigned even cardinal altitudes or flight levels at intervals of 2,000 feet (e.g., 4,000, 6,000, FL 320, FL 340). Course 270 is westbound, so an even cardinal altitude is required.

choices.text: An even cardinal altitude or flight level (e.g., 4,000, FL 320, FL 340).
An odd cardinal altitude or flight level (e.g., 5,000, FL 310, FL 330).
Any altitude between the MEA and the aircraft's service ceiling.
Only flight levels at or above FL 290.

items.question_text: What two elements of a STAR must be included in an arrival clearance when a STAR is assigned?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-1b requires the clearance to include 'the name and transition, if necessary, of the STAR/RNAV STAR to be flown.' Both the STAR name and transition must be specified when a transition is needed.

choices.text: The STAR name and, if necessary, the transition.
The STAR name and the initial approach altitude only.
The transition and the landing runway only.
The STAR name and the expected approach type.

items.question_text: When may approach information contained in the ATIS broadcast be omitted from a controller's transmission to an arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-10a allows omission of ATIS-contained approach information 'if the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.' The pilot's acknowledgment of the ATIS code confirms receipt of the current broadcast information.

choices.text: When the pilot states the appropriate ATIS code.
Whenever the destination airport has ATIS available.
When the controller has verified the ATIS broadcast is current.
ATIS information can never be omitted; it must always be restated.

items.question_text: What is the distance limitation for clearing an aircraft using VOR navigation below the MEA but at or above the MOCA?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-5-6a1 permits below-MEA clearance for aircraft using VOR/VORTAC/TACAN only 'within 22 miles of that NAVAID.' The altitude must also be at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace.

choices.text: Only within 22 miles of the VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN.
At any distance if the aircraft is at or above the MOCA.
Only when radar monitoring and lost communications instructions are provided.
At any distance if the pilot accepts responsibility for terrain clearance.

items.question_text: When issuing a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach procedure, what restriction may be omitted?
items.explanation: Per 4-5-7a and Note 2, a crossing altitude restriction may be omitted for airports without a published instrument approach because it is not possible to satisfy the requirement for a crossing altitude that ensures terrain clearance until the aircraft reaches a fix, point, or route where altitude information is available. The pilot then determines the minimum IFR altitude per 14 CFR 91.177.

choices.text: The crossing altitude restriction, because terrain clearance to a published reference point cannot be assured.
The destination airport name, because the pilot already knows the destination.
The cruise altitude itself, because the pilot can select any altitude.
The entire clearance beyond the initial fix.

items.question_text: When forwarding arrival information to approach control facilities before transfer of control jurisdiction, what information may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-6b4 states the clearance limit 'may be omitted when provided for in a letter of agreement.' Similarly, b5 allows omission of the time/fix/altitude of control transfer when specified in an LOA. LOAs can streamline the arrival information forwarding requirements.

choices.text: The clearance limit and the time, fix, or altitude of control transfer.
The aircraft identification and type.
The ETA and actual time over the clearance limit.
The aircraft equipment suffix and proposed altitude.

items.question_text: What information must approach control facilities forward to nonapproach control towers for arriving aircraft?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-11a requires forwarding: aircraft identification, type of aircraft, ETA, and the type of instrument approach procedure to be executed, or SVFR entry direction and restrictions, or contact approach position. These items allow the tower to adjust traffic flow.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, type, ETA, and type of approach or SVFR/contact approach details.
Aircraft identification and ETA only; other information is obtained from the flight plan.
Only the type of approach procedure; other information is forwarded separately.
Aircraft identification, ETA, and parking gate assignment.

items.question_text: When changing from one ILS to another at an airport equipped with multiple ILS systems that operate one at a time, what coordination is required?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-7-13 requires coordination 'with the facilities which use the fixes formed by reference to these NAVAIDs.' When the ILS changes, any fixes defined by reference to that ILS (such as localizer-based intersections) are affected, and all facilities using those fixes must be notified.

choices.text: Coordinate with the facilities that use fixes formed by reference to the ILS NAVAIDs.
Coordinate with the airport operator and the ILS maintenance technician.
No coordination is required; the change is handled through NOTAMs.
Coordinate only with the TRACON, which handles all ILS changes.

items.question_text: When is a controller authorized to clear an aircraft for a side-step maneuver?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-8-7 states the side-step maneuver may be used 'when authorized by an instrument approach procedure.' The procedure must be published authorizing the side-step; the controller cannot independently decide to use it. Note that side-step maneuvers require higher weather minima/MDA.

choices.text: When authorized by a published instrument approach procedure.
Whenever the pilot requests to land on a parallel runway.
When the primary runway is occupied and a parallel runway is available.
At any airport with parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet.

items.question_text: How should ATC respond when a pilot cancels an IFR flight plan?
items.explanation: The prescribed phraseology is '(Call sign) IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.' This acknowledges receipt of the cancellation and confirms the IFR flight plan is closed.

choices.text: (Call sign) IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.
(Call sign) ROGER, CANCEL IFR.
(Call sign) CANCEL FLIGHT PLAN.
(Call sign) IFR CLOSED.

items.question_text: What are the five conditions under which a frequency change is permitted during a Single Frequency Approach?
items.explanation: The five exceptions are: (a) daylight hours in VFR conditions, (b) on pilot request, (c) when the pilot cancels IFR, (d) in an emergency, and (e) when cleared for a visual approach. These exceptions balance the SFA safety benefit against operational necessity.

choices.text: Daylight VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, emergency, or visual approach clearance.
Pilot request, radar contact, VMC, DME available, and landing assured.
Emergency only, with no other exceptions permitted.
Night VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, equipment failure, or visual approach clearance.

items.question_text: A pilot declares an emergency. The aircraft is already radar identified and the controller has established radio contact. Should Code 7700 be assigned?
items.explanation: Code 7700 is assigned when the pilot declares an emergency and the aircraft is not radar identified. If the aircraft is already radar identified, the identification purpose of the code assignment is less critical. After radio and radar contact have been established, the controller may request the aircraft (except single-piloted helicopters and single-piloted turbojet aircraft) to change from Code 7700 to a computer-assigned code. The code is primarily for non-radar-identified emergency aircraft.

choices.text: Yes — Code 7700 must be assigned to every aircraft that declares an emergency regardless of identification status.
No — Code 7700 is specifically for when the aircraft is not radar identified at the time of the emergency declaration.
Yes — but only if the aircraft is above FL 180.
No — Code 7700 is reserved for hijack situations only.

items.question_text: A controller observes three different emergency code displays on three separate aircraft: one shows 7500, one shows 7600, and one shows 7700. Which aircraft's display requires the controller to apply communications failure procedures?
items.explanation: Code 7600 specifically indicates a communications failure and triggers the procedures in paragraph 10-4-4. Code 7500 indicates hijack/unlawful interference and triggers different procedures. Code 7700 indicates a general emergency, which also has its own separate handling. The controller must correctly identify which code maps to which procedural response rather than treating all emergency codes identically.

choices.text: The aircraft displaying 7500.
The aircraft displaying 7600.
The aircraft displaying 7700.
All three equally, since each indicates an emergency condition.

items.question_text: An aircraft squawks Code 7600 and the controller's ERAM display shows 'RDOF.' The pilot has been communicating normally on frequency. What is the controller's correct response?
items.explanation: The note states an aircraft experiencing loss of two-way radio communications can be expected to squawk Code 7600 — this describes a typical association, not a conditional dependency on observed silence. The controller's procedural obligation is triggered by observing the code display regardless of current communication status, because the code may reflect an intermittent or developing failure. The controller should investigate and apply 10-4-4 procedures as appropriate.

choices.text: Disregard the code display because the pilot is responding, so the indication must be erroneous.
Apply communications failure procedures because the code display triggers the obligation regardless of current voice communication status.
Instruct the pilot to recycle the transponder to clear what appears to be a false indication.
Treat the code as a hijack indicator and apply hijack procedures instead.

items.question_text: Two controllers coordinate separation using the term 'traffic.' Controller A issues the traffic and Controller B accepts the traffic with restrictions. Which controller is responsible for ensuring approved separation is maintained between the involved aircraft?
items.explanation: When using the term 'traffic' for coordinating separation, the controller issuing traffic must issue appropriate restrictions, and the controller accepting the restrictions is responsible to ensure that approved separation is maintained between the involved aircraft. The accepting controller bears the separation responsibility because they have accepted the traffic with the restrictions the issuing controller provided.

choices.text: Controller A, the issuing controller, retains full separation responsibility after issuing the restrictions.
Controller B, the accepting controller, is responsible for ensuring approved separation is maintained.
Both controllers share equal and undivided responsibility for the separation.
Neither controller is responsible once the term 'traffic' is used.

items.question_text: An RNAV aircraft is operating at FL 430 on a Q route in domestic airspace. Is radar separation mandatory or discretionary for this aircraft?
items.explanation: Radar separation must be applied to all RNAV aircraft operating at and below FL 450 on Q routes or random RNAV routes, excluding oceanic airspace. The controlling requirement is mandatory ('must'), not discretionary. The exception is for point-to-point routes via an impromptu route, which does not apply here since the aircraft is on a Q route.

choices.text: Discretionary — radar separation may be applied but is not required for RNAV aircraft.
Mandatory — radar separation must be applied because the aircraft is an RNAV aircraft at or below FL 450 on a Q route.
Discretionary because the aircraft is above FL 290.
Not applicable because all RNAV aircraft are exempt from radar separation requirements.

items.question_text: A controller is using Mode C altitude information for vertical separation. In which of the following situations is this use prohibited?
items.explanation: Mode C must not be used to effect vertical separation with an aircraft on a cruise clearance, on a contact approach, or as specified in paragraph 5-14-4, subparagraph f3. These situations involve aircraft that may change altitude at pilot discretion without controller assignment, making Mode C-based vertical separation unreliable.

choices.text: Only for aircraft in Class A airspace where all aircraft operate under IFR.
An aircraft on a cruise clearance, a contact approach, or as specified in paragraph 5-14-4, subparagraph f3.
Any aircraft conducting a visual approach after being cleared by the tower.
Only aircraft operating in oceanic airspace beyond radar coverage.

items.question_text: In terminal operations, a controller is using a single site ASR sensor. Two aircraft are on crossing courses and one has crossed the projected course of the other. What minimum angular difference between their courses is required to apply passing or diverging separation?
items.explanation: For single site ASR or FUSION mode, the required angular difference is at least 15 degrees. For single site ARSR or FUSION mode when ISR is displayed, the requirement increases to at least 45 degrees. The sensor type drives the minimum angular difference — the longer-range ARSR with slower refresh requires a wider angular separation for the controller to be confident that passing or diverging separation is safe.

choices.text: At least 45 degrees, because all crossing-course scenarios require the same angular difference.
At least 15 degrees, because the controller is using single site ASR.
At least 30 degrees, which is the standard for all terminal separation scenarios.
No minimum angular difference is required as long as the targets do not touch.

items.question_text: A controller evaluates three aircraft for speed adjustment. Aircraft A is at FL 400 and the pilot has consented. Aircraft B is flying a published high altitude instrument approach procedure. Aircraft C is established in a holding pattern. To which aircraft may speed adjustment be assigned?
items.explanation: Speed adjustments must not be assigned to aircraft at or above FL 390 without pilot consent, aircraft executing a published high altitude instrument approach procedure, or aircraft in a holding pattern. All three aircraft fall into prohibited categories. However, Aircraft A could receive a speed adjustment if pilot consent is obtained — the prohibition is on assignment without consent, not an absolute ban.

choices.text: All three aircraft may receive speed adjustments because operational need overrides the restrictions.
Aircraft A only, but the consent makes it permissible whereas the other two remain prohibited.
Aircraft A only, because the pilot has consented and the other two are in prohibited categories.
Aircraft B and C only, because they are on established procedures.

items.question_text: A terminal controller releases a departing aircraft when an arriving aircraft on final approach is 2 miles from the runway. The antenna distance is 25 miles. What must happen within 1 minute after takeoff for this to be legal?
items.explanation: The paragraph permits separating a departing aircraft from an arriving aircraft on final by a minimum of 2 miles at the time of release, provided the separation will increase to a minimum of 3 miles (or 5 miles when 40 miles or more from the antenna) within 1 minute after takeoff. At 25 miles from the antenna, the 3-mile threshold applies. If this separation increase cannot be achieved within 1 minute, the procedure may not be used.

choices.text: Separation must increase to a minimum of 5 miles within 1 minute after takeoff.
Separation must increase to a minimum of 3 miles within 1 minute after takeoff.
The departing aircraft must reach at least 1,000 feet AGL within 1 minute.
No additional requirement applies because 2 miles of separation is sufficient on its own.

items.question_text: An arriving aircraft is being vectored to the final approach course and has been assigned an altitude of 3,000 feet to maintain until established on the localizer. The aircraft is still en route to the localizer intercept point. When is the controller authorized to issue the approach clearance?
items.explanation: Approach clearance may be issued only after the aircraft is established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure, or is assigned an altitude to maintain until established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure. In this case, the aircraft has been assigned an altitude to maintain until established on the localizer, satisfying the second condition. Without either condition being met, approach clearance must be withheld.

choices.text: Issue the approach clearance only if the aircraft is conducting a radar approach.
Withhold the approach clearance until the aircraft reports the airport in sight.
Issue the approach clearance immediately because the aircraft is being vectored toward the final approach course.
Issue the approach clearance because the aircraft has been assigned an altitude to maintain until established on a segment of a published route or instrument approach procedure.

items.question_text: The tower provides visual separation between two radar arrivals on final approach. Who is responsible for separation between these aircraft?
items.explanation: The radar controller performing the approach control function is responsible for separation of radar arrivals unless visual separation is provided by the tower, or a letter of agreement or facility directive authorizes otherwise. When the tower provides visual separation, that responsibility transfers to the tower. Without such a transfer, the radar controller retains separation responsibility.

choices.text: The radar controller retains all separation responsibility regardless of visual separation.
The tower, because visual separation has been provided and this transfers responsibility from the radar controller.
The pilots, because they can see each other once visual separation is applied.
Responsibility is shared equally between the tower and the radar controller.

items.question_text: Two parallel runways have centerlines 4,500 feet apart and simultaneous dependent approaches are being conducted. What minimum diagonal radar separation is required between successive aircraft on adjacent final approach courses?
items.explanation: For runway centerlines more than 3,600 feet but no more than 8,300 feet apart, the required minimum diagonal radar separation is 1.5 miles. The spacing thresholds are: 2,500 to 3,600 ft = 1 mile; more than 3,600 to 8,300 ft = 1.5 miles; more than 8,300 to 9,000 ft = 2 miles. Each tier increases separation as runway spacing increases.

choices.text: 1 mile, because all dependent approaches use the same diagonal minimum.
1.5 miles, because the runway centerlines are more than 3,600 feet but no more than 8,300 feet apart.
2 miles, because runways over 3,600 feet always require the maximum diagonal spacing.
3 miles, because that is the standard radar separation minimum for all approaches.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are conducting simultaneous dependent approaches with reduced separation. The lead aircraft executes a go-around. When must the controller issue control instructions to increase separation?
items.explanation: Control instructions must be issued expeditiously and prior to losing the approved reduced separation. The controller must act to establish approved separation (e.g., altitude and/or lateral separation via divergence) before the reduced separation is lost. Wake turbulence cautionary advisories must also be issued in accordance with paragraph 2-1-20.

choices.text: Only after the reduced separation is lost, because the go-around aircraft must first establish a positive climb rate.
Expeditiously and prior to losing the approved reduced separation, establishing approved separation via altitude and/or lateral divergence.
Within 2 minutes, since the reduced separation provides a buffer for transitional maneuvers.
Only for the go-around aircraft; the other aircraft can continue its approach unchanged.

items.question_text: A pilot accepts a radar approach during IFR conditions with weather below published minima for the airport. Who is responsible for determining whether the approach and landing are authorized under the existing weather minima?
items.explanation: The note explicitly states that the pilot is responsible for determining if the approach and landing are authorized under the existing weather minima. Acceptance of a radar approach by the pilot does not waive the prescribed weather minima for the airport or for the particular aircraft operator. The controller may offer or provide a radar approach, but the pilot bears the separate regulatory responsibility for weather compliance.

choices.text: The controller offering the approach assumes responsibility for weather minima compliance.
The pilot is responsible for determining whether the approach and landing are authorized under the existing weather minima.
The radar approach itself waives the published weather minima by regulation.
Responsibility is shared equally between the controller and the pilot.

items.question_text: During repeated radar approaches, the pilot has already received a full approach information briefing. Which information item must the controller continue to provide on each subsequent approach?
items.explanation: The paragraph states that all items except for subparagraph 3 (altimeter setting) may be omitted after the first approach if repeated approaches are made and no change has occurred. The altimeter setting must be issued on every approach because it is critical for proper altitude reference and may change between approaches.

choices.text: Ceiling and visibility, which must be updated on every approach.
Altimeter setting, which is the one item that must be provided on every approach.
The missed approach procedure, because it is the most safety-critical item.
All items must be repeated in full on every approach with no exceptions.

items.question_text: During a surveillance final approach, the controller observes the target moving steadily left of the extended runway centerline at a rate that requires prompt correction. Which combination of phraseology elements correctly communicates the course deviation and the correction trend?
items.explanation: The controller must communicate both the deviation position and the trend. The course deviation is described with 'SLIGHTLY/WELL LEFT/RIGHT OF COURSE' and trend information uses 'Going left/right of course' or 'Left/right of course and holding/correcting,' modified by 'RAPIDLY' or 'SLOWLY' as appropriate. The combination 'WELL LEFT OF COURSE, GOING LEFT OF COURSE RAPIDLY' accurately describes the concerning trend and prompts immediate pilot action.

choices.text: ON COURSE — heading correction to the right needed.
WELL LEFT OF COURSE, GOING LEFT OF COURSE RAPIDLY — heading correction to the right.
SLIGHTLY LEFT OF COURSE, CORRECTING — no heading change needed.
LEFT OF COURSE, TURN LEFT to intercept the course.

items.question_text: How do the two E-MSAW suppression functions differ in their intended operational use?
items.explanation: The specific alert suppression message inhibits the E-MSAW alerting display on a single flight for a specific alert — it handles a one-time occurrence. The indefinite alert suppression message must be used exclusively to inhibit E-MSAW alerts on aircraft known to fly at altitudes that will activate the alert feature of one or more MIA areas within an ARTCC, such as military flights on low-level routes below established minimum IFR altitudes. The indefinite suppression persists for the flight's active status duration.

choices.text: Specific suppression removes E-MSAW alerts for the entire facility; indefinite suppression removes them for one sector only.
Specific suppression targets a single alert on a single flight; indefinite suppression targets aircraft routinely flying at MIA-activating altitudes for the flight's duration.
Both functions are interchangeable and the controller may use either for any E-MSAW alert.
Specific suppression is exclusively for military flights; indefinite suppression is for civilian flights only.

items.question_text: Mode C altitude information is unavailable for an en route aircraft climbing from FL 200 to FL 350. The aircraft passes through FL 280, reaches FL 350, and is subsequently cleared to descend to FL 300. At which points must reported altitudes be entered into the computer?
items.explanation: Three triggers exist: (a) when the aircraft reaches the assigned altitude (FL 350), (b) when an aircraft at an assigned altitude is issued a clearance to climb or descend (upon receiving the clearance to descend to FL 300), and (c) a minimum of each 10,000 feet during climb to or descent from FL 180 and above (during the climb at each 10,000-foot interval — e.g., FL 200, FL 300 — and during the descent similarly). The altitude updates assure maximum accuracy in applying slant range correction formulas.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft reaches FL 350 and again at FL 300.
At each 10,000-foot interval during the climb from FL 200, upon reaching FL 350, and upon receiving the descent clearance to FL 300.
Only at each 10,000-foot interval during climb only, since descent entries are not required.
Upon reaching FL 350 only, because the other triggers apply only when Mode C is available.

items.question_text: A sector applies 1,000 feet vertical separation at lower altitudes and 2,000 feet vertical separation at higher flight levels. What additional altitude display buffer must be added to the sector's altitude stratum for each case?
items.explanation: Where 1,000 feet vertical separation is applicable, sectors must add at least 1,200 feet above the highest and below the lowest altitude or flight level of the sector. Where 2,000 feet vertical separation is applicable, sectors must add at least 2,200 feet above the highest and below the lowest flight level. The larger buffer at higher flight levels ensures approaching aircraft with Mode C are visible before entering the sector's vertical stratum for Merging Target Procedures.

choices.text: 1,000 feet where 1,000-foot separation applies; 2,000 feet where 2,000-foot separation applies.
1,200 feet where 1,000-foot separation applies; 2,200 feet where 2,000-foot separation applies.
2,200 feet in all cases regardless of the applicable separation minimum.
1,200 feet where 2,000-foot separation applies; 2,200 feet where 1,000-foot separation applies.

items.question_text: A facility wants to reduce the altitude display buffer for a particular sector below the standard additional requirements of 1,200 feet or 2,200 feet. Under what conditions may such an exception be authorized?
items.explanation: The note states exceptions to the selected altitude limit requirements may be authorized for specific altitudes in certain ARTCC sectors if defined in appropriate facility directives and approved by the respective service area operations directorate. This requires both documented local direction and service-area-level approval — it cannot be done unilaterally at the sector or facility level.

choices.text: Exceptions are never permitted under any circumstances.
Any facility may make the adjustment at the sector level without higher-level approval.
Only if defined in appropriate facility directives and approved by the respective service area operations directorate.
Only if the aircraft operators using that airspace submit a written request.

items.question_text: The ASR that supports the ASDE system at a tower goes out of service, and MLAT is inoperative at this facility. What must the tower position responsible for aircraft on approach do?
items.explanation: The paragraph requires that during outages of the ASR that supports an ASDE system where MLAT is inoperative or is not present, the tower position responsible for aircraft on approach must enable the ADS-B indicator on the tower display workstation. The note clarifies that the ADS-B indicator will only display if the TDW is operating in Fused Display Mode, so the controller must also verify that the TDW is in that mode.

choices.text: Suspend all approach operations until the ASR and MLAT are restored.
Enable the ADS-B indicator on the tower display workstation, which requires the TDW to be in Fused Display Mode to display.
Switch to a backup radar display that does not require ADS-B indicators.
Revert to using only the two-letter ICAO designators as the sole source of aircraft identification.

items.question_text: A terminal controller receives a CA alert involving an aircraft practicing Air Defense Command intercept maneuvers. These operations are exempt from standard approved separation criteria. Which alert management function is appropriate for this recurring situation?
items.explanation: The inhibit function must only be used for aircraft routinely engaged in operations where approved separation criteria do not apply, such as ADC practice intercept operations and air shows. The suppress function may be used to suppress a specific CA/MCI alert for cases that don't fit this narrow category. Using suppress for a routine intercept scenario would be incorrect because it would require repeated actions; inhibit is the proper tool for recurring non-separation-standard operations.

choices.text: Use the suppress function because each alert must be individually managed for intercept operations.
Use the inhibit function because the aircraft is routinely engaged in operations where approved separation criteria do not apply.
Allow every CA alert to display without any suppression or inhibition for ADC intercept practice.
Use the inhibit function for any CA alert that distracts the controller, regardless of operation type.

items.question_text: When EA activity interferes with the operational use of radar in a terminal facility, what is the correct procedure?
items.explanation: For terminal facilities, the controller must request suspension of the EA activity through the ARTCC. If immediate cessation is required, the controller broadcasts the request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notifies the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible. This differs from en route procedure where the controller requests suspension directly from the responsible military unit or aircraft.

choices.text: Request suspension through the ARTCC; if immediate cessation is needed, broadcast directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC as soon as possible.
Ignore the interference since EA activity is always authorized and cannot be suspended.
Directly contact the responsible military unit to request suspension, the same as en route procedure.
Terminate all radar operations in the affected sector until the EA activity concludes on its own.

items.question_text: What is the primary operational purpose of manual fix posting on a radar display?
items.explanation: Manual fix posting allows controllers to mark significant geographic positions or navigation fixes on the radar display, providing spatial reference points for vectoring, position reporting, and separation. It is used when automated fix display is unavailable or when the controller needs to mark a specific location not in the automated system.

choices.text: To provide controllers with spatial reference points for navigation fixes or geographic positions on the radar display.
To automatically generate flight plan amendments when an aircraft passes a designated point.
To replace the need for aircraft position reports entirely.
To calculate groundspeed and estimate times for all aircraft in the sector.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose of instructing a pilot to place the transponder in standby?
items.explanation: Standby operation is used when beacon returns from an aircraft are derogating the radar presentation or interfering with other beacon targets. The controller instructs the pilot to place the transponder in standby to temporarily stop beacon transmissions while maintaining the ability to quickly restore normal operation.

choices.text: To temporarily stop beacon transmissions when returns are derogating the radar presentation or interfering with other targets.
To permanently disable the transponder when an aircraft exits controlled airspace.
To test whether the aircraft's transponder is functioning correctly.
To initiate a transponder code change to a new assigned code.

items.question_text: What principle governs when a controller should change an aircraft's assigned beacon code?
items.explanation: Controllers should keep beacon code changes to a minimum. When a code change is required, the controller must ensure the new code is not being used by another aircraft in the area of responsibility. The paragraph emphasizes minimizing code changes because each change creates workload for both the controller and the pilot and introduces a potential point of error or misidentification.

choices.text: Beacon code changes should be kept to a minimum, and new codes must not duplicate codes assigned to other aircraft in the area.
Codes should be changed at every sector boundary to ensure each sector has unique identification.
Codes should be changed whenever it is operationally convenient for the controller, regardless of duplication risk.
Code changes are prohibited once an aircraft is airborne unless an emergency is declared.

items.question_text: When should an aircraft's radar beacon service be terminated?
items.explanation: Beacon service should be terminated when the aircraft no longer requires radar service, such as when landing, exiting controlled airspace, or when the pilot requests termination. The controller issues the appropriate phraseology to instruct the aircraft to stop beacon transmissions.

choices.text: When the aircraft no longer requires radar service, such as upon landing or when exiting controlled airspace.
Only when the aircraft reaches FL 600 and above.
Beacon service continues automatically and never needs to be terminated by the controller.
Only when the transponder fails and cannot be repaired in flight.

items.question_text: What is the key distinguishing feature of a handoff versus a point out in radar coordination?
items.explanation: A handoff transfers both identification and control jurisdiction of an aircraft to the receiving controller. A point out advises another controller of an aircraft's position or intended action without transferring control jurisdiction. The transferring controller retains separation responsibility in a point out, while a completed handoff transfers that responsibility.

choices.text: A handoff transfers control jurisdiction; a point out shares position information without transferring control.
A point out transfers control jurisdiction; a handoff only shares position information.
Both terms mean the same thing and are used interchangeably in ATC coordination.
A handoff is used only for VFR aircraft; a point out is used only for IFR aircraft.

items.question_text: What are the three standard methods for transferring radar identification of an aircraft between controllers?
items.explanation: The three standard methods are: handoff (transfers both identification and control jurisdiction), point out (shares position without transferring control), and automated information transfer or AIT (uses automation to exchange flight data between sectors or facilities). Each method serves a different coordination purpose.

choices.text: Handoff, point out, and automated information transfer (AIT).
Radar contact, radar service terminated, and radar contact lost.
Primary radar, secondary radar, and ADS-B identification.
Direct communication, relay through FSS, and CPDLC message.

items.question_text: What must the controller ensure about radar target display separation?
items.explanation: The controller must ensure that radar targets remain distinct and do not touch or overlap on the display. The minimum display separation correlates with the applicable separation minimum, and the controller must verify that the visual representation corresponds to the required physical separation.

choices.text: Targets must remain distinct on the display and must not touch or overlap.
A single pixel of display separation is sufficient as long as the data blocks do not overlap.
Targets may touch briefly as long as the controller can see both data blocks simultaneously.
The controller's visual assessment of target separation is advisory only; separation is tracked by automation.

items.question_text: How does target resolution differ from target separation on a radar display?
items.explanation: Target resolution refers to the radar system's inherent ability to display two closely-spaced aircraft as distinct, separate targets — it is a system capability. Target separation is the controller-applied minimum display distance between targets that corresponds to approved separation minima — it is an operational standard based on that capability.

choices.text: Target resolution is a system capability; target separation is the controller-applied standard based on that capability.
Both terms refer to the same concept of distance between displayed targets.
Target resolution applies only to primary radar; target separation applies only to secondary radar.
Target resolution is a controller judgment; target separation is a system setting.

items.question_text: Under what general condition may a terminal controller vector an IFR departure below the minimum IFR altitude?
items.explanation: Terminal controllers may vector IFR departures below the minimum IFR altitude when the procedure is described in facility directives and separation from prominent obstacles is applied in accordance with specified criteria. This is not a routine practice and requires specific conditions to be met, including obstacle separation and climb gradient assurance.

choices.text: When described in facility directives and separation from prominent obstacles is applied per specified criteria.
Any time the controller determines it is operationally necessary, without additional requirements.
Only when the pilot specifically requests a lower altitude in writing.
Only for VFR aircraft that are not on an ATC-assigned altitude.

items.question_text: When should speed adjustments be terminated?
items.explanation: Speed adjustments must be terminated when they are no longer needed to achieve or maintain the required or desired spacing. The controller should not leave speed restrictions in place beyond their operational usefulness, as they constrain the pilot's ability to operate the aircraft efficiently and may interfere with normal profile management.

choices.text: When the speed adjustments are no longer needed to achieve or maintain the required or desired spacing.
Only when the aircraft reaches the final approach fix.
Speed adjustments automatically terminate when the aircraft changes altitude.
Speed adjustments must be maintained until the aircraft lands regardless of spacing achieved.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose of the approach gate in radar arrival procedures?
items.explanation: The approach gate is a designated point on the final approach course, typically 1 to 2 miles from the final approach fix, by which an arriving aircraft should be established on the final approach course. It ensures aircraft are properly aligned and configured before beginning the final approach segment.

choices.text: A designated point on final approach course by which an arriving aircraft should be established on the final approach course.
A physical gate at the airport perimeter that aircraft must pass before landing clearance is issued.
The point where the controller transfers communications to the tower.
The published missed approach point for the instrument approach procedure.

items.question_text: Why is pilot acknowledgment of controller transmissions critical during a radar approach?
items.explanation: The controller must receive acknowledgment to confirm the pilot received and understood the instructions. During critical phases of the approach, the controller cannot provide effective radar service without confirming the pilot's receipt of guidance. If acknowledgment is not received, lost communication procedures must be initiated.

choices.text: It confirms the pilot received and understood the instructions; absent acknowledgment, lost communication procedures apply.
No acknowledgment is required during radar approaches because the controller can see the aircraft on radar.
Acknowledgment is only required for altitude changes, not for heading or speed instructions.
The controller assumes compliance unless the pilot specifically objects to an instruction.

items.question_text: What must a controller do when radar contact is lost with an aircraft on a radar approach?
items.explanation: When radar contact is lost during a radar approach, the controller must immediately advise the pilot that radar contact has been lost and instruct the pilot to execute the appropriate missed approach procedure or provide alternate instructions. The controller can no longer provide radar service to that aircraft.

choices.text: Immediately advise the pilot that radar contact is lost and issue alternate instructions or a missed approach.
Continue issuing approach guidance based on the last known position until radar contact is regained.
Wait 30 seconds to see if radar contact is regained before advising the pilot.
Transfer the aircraft to the tower without advising of the lost radar contact.

items.question_text: At what point during a radar approach should the final controller changeover occur?
items.explanation: The final controller changeover should occur at a point that ensures continuity of approach guidance. The transfer must be completed before the aircraft reaches a critical portion of the approach, and the receiving controller must acknowledge having the aircraft on radar and confirm readiness to continue the approach.

choices.text: At a point that ensures continuity of guidance, before the aircraft reaches a critical approach portion.
Only after the aircraft has passed the final approach fix.
At any point the current controller chooses, without coordination requirements.
Only when the aircraft requests a different controller.

items.question_text: During a surveillance approach, the pilot reports the runway environment in sight. What must the controller verify before the pilot may continue visually to the runway?
items.explanation: When the pilot reports the runway or airport in sight during a surveillance approach, the controller must ensure the aircraft is in a position from which a normal approach to the runway can be made before authorizing the pilot to continue visually. Premature visual continuation authorization could place the aircraft in an unsafe position relative to the runway environment.

choices.text: The aircraft must be in a position from which a normal approach to the runway can be made.
The pilot must report the specific runway number in sight, not just the airport environment.
The weather must be at least 1,000-foot ceiling and 3 miles visibility.
The tower controller must independently confirm visual contact with the aircraft.

items.question_text: What is the controller's primary planning objective when issuing descent instructions on a surveillance approach?
items.explanation: The controller must plan descent so that the aircraft reaches the minimum descent altitude (MDA) at or before reaching the missed approach point. Descent should be planned to provide a stabilized approach path that allows the pilot adequate time to acquire the visual references needed for landing.

choices.text: Plan descent so the aircraft reaches the MDA at or before the missed approach point on a stabilized path.
Begin descent at the final approach fix regardless of the aircraft's altitude.
Descent rate is entirely at the pilot's discretion once on final approach.
Complete descent within 2 miles of the runway threshold regardless of altitude.

items.question_text: What information must a PAR controller provide when the aircraft reaches the glidepath during a precision approach?
