items.explanation: The controller must advise the pilot when the aircraft reaches the glidepath so the pilot can begin the descent. This notification includes the distance from touchdown and serves as the starting point for the final descent profile of the precision approach.

choices.text: Notify the pilot that the aircraft has reached the glidepath, along with the distance from touchdown.
Only provide glidepath notification if the pilot specifically requests it.
Wait until the aircraft is 2 miles past glidepath intercept before notifying the pilot.
The glidepath notification is provided by the tower controller, not the PAR controller.

items.question_text: What is the controller's required response when a Conflict Alert (CA) activates?
items.explanation: When a CA activates, the controller must immediately assess the situation to determine whether a conflict actually exists and take appropriate action if needed. The CA is an automated warning, not a substitute for controller judgment, and the controller must verify whether separation is adequate or action is required.

choices.text: Immediately assess the situation to determine if a conflict actually exists and take appropriate action if needed.
The CA automatically resolves the conflict, so no controller action is necessary.
The controller should suppress all CA alerts to avoid distraction during complex operations.
The controller must wait for a second CA activation before taking any action.

items.question_text: Why must flight plan information be entered into the en route computer system?
items.explanation: Flight plan information must be entered into the computer to enable automated tracking, conflict probe processing, and flight data distribution between sectors and facilities. The entry must be accurate and complete to ensure the automation system can properly support the controller with conflict prediction and data block information.

choices.text: To enable automated tracking, conflict probe processing, and flight data distribution between sectors and facilities.
To create a permanent record solely for billing and administrative purposes.
Flight plan entry is optional since controllers can manage all functions manually without it.
To automatically generate weather briefings for all aircraft in the sector.

items.question_text: What minimum information must be entered into ERAM when an aircraft is assigned a holding pattern?
items.explanation: When assigning a hold, the controller must enter the holding fix, the direction of holding, and the expect further clearance (EFC) time into ERAM. This information enables the automation to track the holding aircraft and provide accurate estimates for when the aircraft will resume its route.

choices.text: The holding fix, direction of holding, and expect further clearance (EFC) time.
Only the holding fix; other information is automatically calculated by ERAM.
The holding fix and the aircraft's current fuel state in hours.
Only the EFC time; the holding fix is derived from the aircraft's current position.

items.question_text: What distinguishes MSAW alert suppression from inhibition in the terminal environment?
items.explanation: Suppression handles a specific alert occurrence and must be applied individually for each alert. Inhibition is for recurring alert situations where a known aircraft routinely triggers MSAW due to its operational profile, similar to the en route E-MSAW indefinite suppression function. The controller must understand which function is appropriate for each situation.

choices.text: Suppression handles a specific alert occurrence; inhibition is for known aircraft that routinely trigger alerts due to their operational profile.
Both functions are identical and interchangeable in terminal operations.
Suppression is for en route use only; inhibition is for terminal use only.
Suppression permanently removes the MSAW function; inhibition temporarily silences it.

items.question_text: Mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima require direct pilot/controller voice communications. Which of the following satisfies this communications requirement?
items.explanation: The rule requires direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. A controller speaking directly with the pilot on a VHF frequency satisfies the requirement. Relayed communications, regardless of the relay method, do not. The controlling principle is that the communications path must be direct — no intermediary.

choices.text: The pilot communicates through a third aircraft relaying on the same frequency.
The controller and pilot are in direct VHF voice communication.
The pilot communicates through company dispatch, which relays to the controller.
The controller receives position data via datalink while voice is on a separate relay frequency.

items.question_text: An aircraft was estimated over a fix at 1432Z. The position report affecting separation is overdue. By what time must the controller take action to obtain the report?
items.explanation: The 5-minute deadline runs from the estimate time. 1432Z + 5 minutes = 1437Z. Action must be taken no later than 1437Z. The controlling principle is that the clock starts at the estimate time, not when the controller notices the omission.

choices.text: 1442Z, allowing 5 minutes from the time the controller would reasonably notice.
1432Z; action must be immediate upon the estimate time.
No fixed deadline applies when only one report is missed.

items.question_text: Which statement about duplicate position reports is operationally correct?
items.explanation: The rule is an absolute prohibition: an aircraft must not be required to make the same position report to more than one facility. Facility type, operational convenience, and whether one facility heard the report to another are all irrelevant.

choices.text: An aircraft may be required to make the same position report to a tower and a center if both need the information.
An aircraft must not be required to make the same position report to more than one facility.
Duplicate position reports are permitted when the two facilities coordinate the request in advance.
The prohibition applies only when both facilities are of the same type, such as two towers.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly pairs a terminal adjacent-airport wake turbulence category combination with its required separation?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a3 specifies 2 minutes for Category E, F, G, H, or I behind Category C. The other options mismatch the required values: Category F behind A requires 3 minutes (not 2), Category I behind E requires 2 minutes (not 3), and Category D behind B requires 2 minutes (not 3).

choices.text: Category F behind Category A — 2 minutes.
Category H behind Category C — 2 minutes.
Category I behind Category E — 3 minutes.
Category D behind Category B — 3 minutes.

items.question_text: In en route arrival operations, a small aircraft behind a B757 requires 3 minutes when landing to the same runway but only 2 minutes when landing to a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet. Why does the same-runway case require more time?
items.explanation: When landing to the same runway, the trailing aircraft flies directly through the leading aircraft's wake. On a parallel runway (even one separated by less than 2,500 feet), the flight paths diverge, reducing the duration and intensity of wake exposure. This operational distinction explains why the same-runway minima are higher for the B757 case specifically.

choices.text: Aircraft on the same runway fly through the same wake path for a longer duration than on parallel or crossing runways.
The B757 produces more wake on the same runway than on parallel runways.
The same-runway minimum includes an extra coordination minute that the parallel-runway case does not require.
The parallel-runway minimum uses radar miles while the same-runway minimum uses timed minutes.

items.question_text: Which list correctly states the three-tier diverging-course departure minima in order from the smallest to largest separation value?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a lists: (1) immediately after takeoff — 1 minute, (2) within 5 minutes after takeoff — 2 minutes, (3) within 13 miles DME/ATD after takeoff — 3 miles. Each tier increases the separation value as the time/distance before divergence increases, reflecting longer exposure to potential conflict.

choices.text: 1 minute immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
2 minutes immediately after takeoff; 3 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 5 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
1 minute immediately after takeoff; 3 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 5 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
3 miles immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 1 minute within 13 miles DME/ATD.

items.question_text: For which divergence angle must the preceding aircraft have both passed the runway intersection point AND commenced a turn before the succeeding aircraft may take off from intersecting runways?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c2(b) adds the turn requirement specifically for intersecting runways with 15 to 29 degrees of divergence. For 30 degrees or more under c2(a), only intersection passage is required. The turn ensures positive divergence at shallower angles.

choices.text: 30 degrees or more.
Any divergence angle on intersecting runways.
15 to 29 degrees inclusive.
Less than 15 degrees.

items.question_text: For nonintersecting runways, a divergence of less than 15 degrees requires a centerline distance of at least _____ feet for simultaneous takeoffs.
items.explanation: Subparagraph c1(b)(2) requires at least 3,500 feet when the runways diverge by less than 15 degrees. Compare with c1(b)(1): 2,000 feet for 15-29 degrees. The smaller the divergence, the greater the centerline separation required.

items.question_text: The 3-minute same-course departure minimum applies only when what specific condition is met?
items.explanation: The rule applies when the following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft. If the following aircraft will level off below the leading aircraft's altitude, the trigger condition is absent and this rule does not apply. The controlling principle is that the climb-through condition defines the rule's scope.

choices.text: The following aircraft will climb through the altitude assigned to the leading aircraft.
Both aircraft depart from the same runway.
The aircraft are on courses diverging by 45 degrees or more.
The following aircraft is faster than the leading aircraft.

items.question_text: For mixed DME/ATD aircraft pairs on the same course, the DME aircraft must be either at or below _____ feet or outside of _____ miles from the DME NAVAID to use the 5-mile minimum.
items.explanation: The rule requires the DME aircraft to be at or below 10,000 feet, or outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID. Either condition satisfies the qualification — they are alternatives, not cumulative requirements.

items.question_text: In terminal operations with a valid fix at 6 miles, the takeoff direction is aligned with the final approach course reciprocal (differing by less than 45 degrees). What must the departing aircraft accomplish before the arriving aircraft reaches the fix?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b applies when the takeoff direction is other than in subparagraph a. It requires the departing aircraft to be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course before the arrival leaves the fix. This is stricter than subparagraph a, which only requires takeoff to have occurred.

choices.text: Be established on a course diverging by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course.
Have taken off before the arriving aircraft leaves the fix.
Have reached 4 miles from the airport.
Be at or above the arriving aircraft's altitude before reaching the fix.

items.question_text: Which is the correct phraseology for requiring an aircraft to cross a fix no earlier than a specified time?
items.explanation: The published phraseology provides two formats: CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time) sets a deadline, CROSS (fix) AT OR AFTER (time) sets an earliest crossing time. For 'no earlier than,' AT OR AFTER is correct.

choices.text: CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time).
CROSS (fix) AT OR AFTER (time).
PROCEED TO (fix), ARRIVE NO EARLIER THAN (time).
CROSS (fix) NOT BEFORE (time).

items.question_text: Which speed advantage from the leading aircraft, combined with DME/ATD equipment, authorizes a 5-mile longitudinal separation minimum on same, converging, or crossing courses?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a requires the leading aircraft to maintain a speed at least 44 knots faster for the 5-mile tier (or 3 minutes for non-DME). Subparagraph b requires at least 22 knots for the 10-mile tier (or 5 minutes for non-DME). The fallback under subparagraph d is 20 miles or 10 minutes when neither speed advantage is met.

choices.text: At least 20 knots faster.
At least 30 knots faster.
At least 22 knots faster.
At least 44 knots faster.

items.question_text: For non-DME aircraft climbing or descending through another aircraft's altitude, the 5-minute longitudinal minimum requires all of three conditions. Which condition concerns the maximum permitted vertical distance at the start of the altitude change?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c2(b) requires the aircraft to be separated by not more than 4,000 feet when the altitude change started. The other two conditions are (a) descending leads or climbing follows, and (c) the change starts within 10 minutes after the following aircraft reports over a fix reported by the leading aircraft or acknowledges a crossing-time clearance.

choices.text: Not more than 2,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 4,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 5,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.
Not more than 10,000 feet apart when the altitude change started.

items.question_text: Opposite-course aircraft must be assigned different altitudes from 10 minutes before until 10 minutes after they are estimated to pass. This creates a total protected window of _____ minutes.
items.explanation: The rule specifies a 20-minute total window: 10 minutes before plus 10 minutes after estimated passing equals 20 minutes. Vertical separation may be discontinued sooner if one of the listed conditions is met.

items.question_text: Two aircraft on opposite courses have reported passing the same intersection and are 4 minutes apart. May vertical separation be discontinued?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b permits discontinuing vertical separation when both aircraft have reported passing the same intersection or waypoint AND they are at least 3 minutes apart. With 4 minutes, this condition is met. The controlling principle is that a shared reference point plus a minimum time interval confirms the aircraft have passed each other.

choices.text: Yes, because they reported passing the same intersection and are at least 3 minutes apart.
No, because they must report passing different NAVAIDs indicating they have passed each other.
No, because vertical separation must continue for the full 10 minutes after estimated passing.
Yes, but only if they are also at least 8 miles apart.

items.question_text: Two RNAV aircraft on opposite courses report passing the same position. The route is greater than 8 miles in width and the segment requires extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles. What minimum distance must separate the aircraft?
items.explanation: Subparagraph c provides three RNAV distance tiers: 8 miles for routes ≤8 miles wide, 18 miles for routes >8 miles wide, and 30 miles for any route segment requiring extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles. The extended-distance qualifier overrides the route-width tier and mandates 30 miles.

choices.text: 8 miles because the aircraft reported the same position.
18 miles because the route is greater than 8 miles in width.
30 miles because the segment requires extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles.
3 minutes because time-based separation applies when extended distance limitations are in effect.

items.question_text: Which of the following is an approved method for procedural lateral separation?
items.explanation: The paragraph lists four methods: (a) clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose widths do not overlap, (b) clear aircraft below 18,000 to different geographical locations, (c) clear aircraft to hold over different fixes whose holding pattern airspace does not overlap, and (d) clear departing aircraft to fly specified headings that diverge by at least 45 degrees.

choices.text: Clear aircraft on different airways or routes whose protected airspace widths do not overlap.
Assign different altitudes to aircraft on the same airway.
Require aircraft to report position every 50 miles along the route.
Apply 5 miles of radar separation between aircraft on parallel tracks.

items.question_text: Clear departing aircraft to fly specified headings that diverge by at least _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Subparagraph d requires headings that diverge by at least 45 degrees for lateral separation of departing aircraft. This is the same 45-degree threshold used in 6-2-1 for diverging-course departure separation.

items.question_text: The diverging-radials lateral separation procedure requires radials of the same NAVAID to diverge by at least _____ degrees.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 requires at least 15 degrees of divergence. This is the minimum threshold; below this angle the procedure does not apply regardless of aircraft distance from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: The non-DME diverging-radials table specifies a required distance for each divergence angle. When the actual divergence angle falls between two values in the table, which value is used?
items.explanation: When the divergence angle falls between two table values, the next higher distance value is used. This conservative approach ensures separation is never understated when the exact angle does not match a table entry.

choices.text: The next lower value in the table.
The average of the two adjacent values.
The next higher value in the table.
The value for the closest whole-degree entry.

items.question_text: Between different DME arcs about the same NAVAID at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID, the minimum lateral separation is _____ miles.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 specifies 10 miles for arc-to-arc separation at 35 miles or less. At more than 35 miles, subparagraph a2 increases this to 20 miles. The doubling reflects greater position uncertainty farther from the NAVAID.

items.question_text: A controller assigns one aircraft to fly a 30-mile DME arc about a VOR, and needs to protect other airspace inside that arc. The airspace to be protected is within 35 miles of the NAVAID. What minimum must be maintained between the arc and that airspace?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b1 specifies 5 miles between an arc and other airspace to be protected at 35 miles or less from the NAVAID. This is half the arc-to-arc minimum for the same distance range, reflecting that one aircraft is on a defined arc while the other is in a designated airspace volume.

choices.text: 5 miles.
10 miles, the same as arc-to-arc separation.
20 miles because the arc is at 30 miles from the NAVAID.
No minimum applies because the airspace is inside the arc.

items.question_text: For a course change of 75 degrees at FL200, the overflown-side protection is 14 miles. For a course change of 120 degrees at the same altitude, the protection is 28 miles. What general principle explains this difference?
items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c establish two angle ranges (16-90 and 91-180 degrees) with different protection values. Larger turns sweep through more airspace on the overflown side, requiring more protection. At FL180-230, the values are 14 miles (16-90°) and 28 miles (91-180°). The controlling principle is geometric: a wider turn arc covers more area before the aircraft establishes on the new course.

choices.text: Larger course changes sweep through more airspace on the overflown side before the aircraft establishes on the new course.
The 91-180 degree range always requires exactly double the protection because aircraft accelerate during sharp turns.
The 28-mile value includes extra buffer for wake turbulence that only appears during turns greater than 90 degrees.
Higher-altitude turns require more protection regardless of the turn angle.

items.question_text: For RNAV aircraft on diverging or crossing courses, lateral separation exists when one aircraft is beyond the point where protected airspaces cease to overlap. On a route greater than 8 miles in width where extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles are required, this distance is _____ miles.
items.explanation: Subparagraph b specifies 9 miles for routes >8 miles wide, except 15 miles when extended usable distance limitations beyond 130 miles apply. The extended-distance qualifier overrides the standard route-width tier.

items.question_text: What is the required non-overlap distance for RNAV aircraft on a route that is 8 miles or less in width?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a specifies 4 miles for routes 8 miles or less in width. This is the smallest non-overlap distance in the RNAV diverging/crossing table, reflecting the narrower protected airspace.

choices.text: 4 miles.
9 miles.
8 miles.
15 miles.

items.question_text: REPORT LEAVING ODD/EVEN ALTITUDES/FLIGHT LEVELS.
items.explanation: This is the published phraseology for requesting reports at each odd or even altitude change during climb or descent. It is distinct from 'REPORT LEAVING/REACHING (altitude/flight level)' which requests a single report at one specific altitude.

choices.text: True
False

items.question_text: When severe turbulence is reported, what must an aircraft previously at an altitude report before that altitude can be assigned to another aircraft?
items.explanation: The exception at subparagraph a triggers the stricter rule: the aircraft must report at or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minimum. This is a more demanding requirement than the basic rule (merely report leaving), reflecting the increased risk of altitude deviations in severe turbulence.

choices.text: That it has left the altitude.
At or passing through another altitude separated from the first by the appropriate minimum.
That severe turbulence has subsided.
That it is ready for the next altitude assignment.

items.question_text: An aircraft on a cruise clearance verbally reports leaving 9,000 feet in descent. May the pilot later return to 9,000 feet without a new clearance?
items.explanation: The note states that when a pilot on a cruise clearance verbally reports leaving an altitude in descent, the pilot may not return to that altitude. The cruise clearance gives a block of airspace, but the verbal descent report permanently surrenders the higher portions of that block.

choices.text: Yes, because the cruise clearance assigns a block of airspace up to the cruising altitude.
No, because the verbal report of leaving 9,000 feet in descent means the pilot may not return to that altitude.
Yes, provided the controller confirms via Mode C that 9,000 feet is unoccupied.
No, because a cruise clearance prohibits any altitude changes once a descent has begun.

items.question_text: During pilot-maintained vertical separation, which aircraft is authorized to maintain separation during a climb, and which during a descent?
items.explanation: The rule authorizes the lower aircraft if climbing, or the upper aircraft if descending. The aircraft whose maneuver closes the vertical distance is the one responsible for maintaining separation — the climbing lower aircraft closes from below, the descending upper aircraft closes from above.

choices.text: The higher aircraft in both cases.
The lower aircraft during climb, the upper aircraft during descent.
The lower aircraft in both cases.
Both aircraft must maintain separation simultaneously.

items.question_text: When only one missed approach procedure is available for timed approaches, the existing ceiling must be at or above the highest _____ minimum established for the airport.
items.explanation: The rule requires the ceiling to be at or above the highest circling minimum. This ensures that an aircraft executing the sole missed approach can safely transition to a circling maneuver if needed.

choices.text: circling

items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies the elements that must be included when clearing the succeeding aircraft for a timed approach?
items.explanation: Subparagraph a requires: the approach clearance, the time to leave the approach fix inbound, and the time to start the approach procedure turn if applicable. These three elements give the pilot complete timing information for the sequence.

choices.text: Approach clearance, time to leave the approach fix inbound, and time to start procedure turn if applicable.
Approach clearance, assigned altitude, and missed approach procedure.
Time to leave the approach fix, radar vector heading, and frequency change.
Approach clearance, release to tower, and landing runway assignment.

items.question_text: During a timed-approach sequence, at what point must the controller release the succeeding aircraft to the tower?
items.explanation: The rule requires the aircraft to be released to the tower before it reaches the final approach fix. This ensures the tower has positive control during the final approach segment.

choices.text: Before the aircraft reaches the final approach fix.
After the aircraft reports established on the final approach course.
Before the aircraft begins the procedure turn.
After the preceding aircraft has landed and cleared the runway.

items.question_text: The standard minimum interval between successive timed approaches is _____ minutes or a _____-mile radar interval. Larger intervals apply for wake turbulence situations specified in subparagraphs b and c.
items.explanation: Subparagraph a specifies 2 minutes or a 5-mile radar interval as the standard minimum. Wake turbulence exceptions in subparagraphs b and c may increase these values for specific aircraft pairs.

items.question_text: In en route timed approaches, a small aircraft behind a heavy aircraft requires what wake turbulence interval?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b2 specifies small behind heavy: 3 minutes or 6 miles. This is the same interval as heavy behind super (b1(a)), reflecting similar wake vulnerability.

choices.text: 3 minutes or 6 miles.
4 minutes or 8 miles.
3 minutes or 7 miles.
2 minutes or 5 miles.

items.question_text: An aircraft on a timed approach misses. Under what condition may the next aircraft continue its approach?
items.explanation: Subparagraph b permits the next aircraft to continue if it has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure. Remaining aircraft must be held or retained under radar control until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.

choices.text: Only if the next aircraft has been assigned an alternative missed approach procedure.
Only if the preceding aircraft reports clear of the missed approach path.
Only if the weather is at or above VFR minimums.
The next aircraft must always discontinue the approach and hold after any missed approach.

items.question_text: What does a pilot's use of the phrase 'have numbers' indicate regarding ATIS receipt and the controller's obligation to issue landing information?
items.explanation: Per 7-1-3a, the controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter when the pilot uses 'have numbers.' However, the note explicitly states that pilot use of 'have numbers' does not indicate receipt of the ATIS broadcast. The phrase refers only to having received the required landing data — it is not equivalent to stating an ATIS code. The controller may still need to provide traffic information on a workload-permitting basis.

choices.text: The pilot has received the ATIS broadcast and the controller may omit all landing information.
The pilot has received the required landing data, but this does not indicate ATIS receipt; the controller may omit wind, runway, and altimeter.
The pilot is reporting that they have the current METAR and the controller should issue only traffic information.
The controller must still issue full landing information because 'have numbers' is not a recognized phraseology.

items.question_text: Between sunset and sunrise, may a controller use VFR-on-top to separate aircraft holding at the same fix from each other or from en route aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-3-1c, a controller must not clear an aircraft to maintain VFR-on-top between sunset and sunrise to separate holding aircraft from each other or from en route aircraft unless restrictions are applied to ensure appropriate IFR vertical separation. The concern is that visual see-and-avoid is compromised in darkness, so IFR separation must be maintained.

choices.text: Yes, VFR-on-top may be used to separate holding aircraft at any time provided the aircraft remain in VMC.
Yes, but only if both pilots report the other aircraft in sight and accept visual separation.
No, unless restrictions are applied to ensure appropriate IFR vertical separation.
No, because VFR-on-top is never authorized for holding aircraft regardless of time of day.

items.question_text: When another aircraft is already in the approach sequence ahead of it, under which conditions may a controller still clear a trailing aircraft for a visual approach?
items.explanation: Per 7-4-3c, an aircraft may be cleared for a visual approach when: (1) it is number one in the approach sequence; (2) at a towered airport, it is to follow a preceding aircraft and the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow; or (3) at a towered airport, the pilot reports the airport or runway in sight but not the preceding aircraft — radar separation must be maintained until visual separation is provided.

choices.text: Only when the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight and is instructed to follow.
When the pilot reports the preceding aircraft in sight, OR when the pilot reports the airport/runway in sight with radar separation maintained until visual separation is established.
Whenever the pilot reports the airport in sight, regardless of preceding traffic.
Only when approved separation exists and the aircraft is within 10 miles of the airport.

items.question_text: How does the controller's response differ when handling a pilot-reported flight visibility below 1 mile at an airport with no weather reporting, compared to officially reported ground visibility below 1 mile at an airport with weather reporting?
items.explanation: When weather is not reported and the pilot advises flight visibility below 1 mile, the controller requests the intentions of an arriving aircraft already within the surface area. When ground visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, the controller first informs the aircraft of the reported visibility and then requests intentions using the specific phraseology '(Airport) VISIBILITY LESS THAN 1 MILE. ADVISE INTENTIONS.' The operational distinction is that with no reported weather, the controller relies on pilot-reported flight visibility; with reported weather, the controller cites the official ground visibility measurement.

choices.text: There is no difference; in both cases the controller must deny any clearance.
When no weather is reported, the controller must immediately issue a safety alert; when weather is reported below 1 mile, the controller simply informs the pilot.

When no weather is reported, the controller requests intentions from an aircraft already inside the surface area; when visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, the controller first informs the pilot of the reported visibility and then requests intentions using specific prescribed phraseology.
When no weather is reported, the controller may issue an SVFR clearance; when visibility is officially reported below 1 mile, no clearance is possible.

items.question_text: What must a local controller accomplish before positioning a visually sighted VFR aircraft into the landing sequence?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-3, an aircraft sighted by the local controller at the time of first radio contact may be positioned in the landing sequence only after coordination with approach control. The controller cannot unilaterally insert the aircraft into the sequence — coordination with the radar facility is a prerequisite.

choices.text: Verify that the aircraft has received the current ATIS broadcast.
Coordinate with approach control before positioning the aircraft in the landing sequence.
Confirm the aircraft's transponder is operating and squawking the assigned code.
No additional step is required; the local controller may position any sighted aircraft at their discretion.

items.question_text: Under what two conditions may an approach controller instruct a VFR aircraft to contact the tower before advising the tower of the aircraft's position?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-8b, the aircraft may be instructed to contact the tower prior to the tower being advised of the aircraft's position only when both conditions are met: (1) the tower advises the aircraft is in sight, and (2) space is available in the landing sequence. Both conditions must be satisfied.

choices.text: When the aircraft is within 5 miles of the airport and the tower frequency is not congested.
When both conditions are met: the tower advises the aircraft is in sight AND space is available in the landing sequence.
When the tower has a BRITE or TDW display showing the aircraft's position.
Early transfer is never authorized; the tower must always be advised of position first.

items.question_text: When a control tower is closed, which services is the approach controller authorized to provide to VFR aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 7-6-12, when the tower is not in operation, the controller must provide: (1) wind direction and velocity, (2) traffic information, and (3) notification when radar service is terminated. Assigning a landing sequence is explicitly prohibited per 7-6-12b — the tower normally controls the landing order. All three required services are authorized; only landing sequence assignment is forbidden.

choices.text: Issue wind direction and velocity information, provide traffic information, and inform aircraft when radar service is terminated.
Issue wind direction and velocity information, assign a landing sequence, and provide traffic information.
Assign a landing sequence, issue approach clearances, and provide traffic information.
Terminate all services and instruct aircraft to contact the nearest FSS for advisories.

items.question_text: Does TRSA service replace the basic radar services for VFR aircraft, or is it applied in addition to them?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-1, TRSA procedures are applied IN ADDITION TO the basic services described in Chapter 7, Section 6 (Basic Radar Service to VFR Aircraft - Terminal). TRSA is an overlay — it does not replace basic radar services. The controller must provide both the basic services (safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring, sequencing) and the additional TRSA procedures.

choices.text: Yes, TRSA procedures replace basic radar services entirely within the TRSA boundary.
No, TRSA procedures are applied in addition to the basic radar services described in Chapter 7, Section 6.
TRSA procedures replace basic services only for participating VFR aircraft; non-participating aircraft still receive basic services.
TRSA and basic services are identical; the controller may choose either one.

items.question_text: When a VFR aircraft in a TRSA is assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, when must the controller instruct the aircraft to resume appropriate VFR altitudes?
items.explanation: Per 7-7-5c, when a VFR aircraft is assigned an altitude contrary to 14 CFR 91.159 for separation purposes, the controller must advise the aircraft to resume altitudes appropriate for the direction of flight when the altitude assignment is no longer needed for separation OR when leaving the TRSA. The prescribed phraseology is 'RESUME APPROPRIATE VFR ALTITUDES.'

choices.text: Immediately after issuing the altitude assignment, so the pilot knows it is temporary.
When the altitude is no longer needed for separation or when the aircraft leaves the TRSA.
Only when the pilot requests to return to a standard VFR cruising altitude.
Never, because altitude assignments for separation override 14 CFR 91.159 permanently.

items.question_text: What is the operational purpose of Class C services?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-1, Class C services are designed to keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations. The paragraph explicitly states this purpose and notes that operations without radio or transponder can be conducted through LOAs, facility directives, or special arrangements. Class C is about awareness and coordination, not exclusion.

choices.text: To exclude all VFR aircraft from the airspace surrounding primary airports.
To keep ATC informed of all aircraft within Class C airspace, not to exclude operations.
To provide full IFR separation services to all aircraft regardless of flight rules.
To ensure that only aircraft with two-way radio communications and transponders may ever operate in the area.

items.question_text: What separation is required between a hot air balloon and an IFR aircraft in Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-6b, hot air balloons need not be separated from IFR aircraft in Class C airspace. However, traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate. This is a specific exception to the general Class C separation requirement.

choices.text: Standard Class C separation must be applied; the hot air balloon is a VFR aircraft.
No separation is required; traffic information and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.
The balloon must be kept at least 500 feet vertically from all IFR aircraft.
Target resolution separation must be maintained until the balloon exits Class C airspace.

items.question_text: What level of Class C service must aircraft departing satellite controlled airports receive when their route will penetrate Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-7a, aircraft that will penetrate Class C airspace after departing controlled airports within or adjacent to Class C airspace must be provided the same services as those aircraft departing the primary airport. Procedures must be covered in a LOA or facility directive.

choices.text: Basic radar services only, since the aircraft is not departing the primary airport.
The same services as aircraft departing the primary airport, with procedures covered in a LOA or facility directive.
No special services; the aircraft must establish contact with approach control after departure.
Only traffic advisories until the aircraft is radar identified inside Class C airspace.

items.question_text: When does Class C service terminate for an aircraft landing at a secondary airport within the outer area?
items.explanation: Per 7-8-8, unless aircraft are landing at secondary airports or have requested termination of service while in the outer area, services must be provided until the aircraft departs the associated outer area. For an aircraft landing at a secondary airport, service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate frequency for traffic and airport information. The phraseology is 'CHANGE TO ADVISORY FREQUENCY APPROVED' or 'CONTACT (facility identification).'

choices.text: Class C service continues until the aircraft lands at the secondary airport.
Service is terminated at a sufficient distance from the secondary airport to allow the pilot to change to the appropriate advisory frequency.
Class C service never terminates for aircraft within the outer area, even after landing.
Service terminates automatically when the aircraft leaves Class C airspace proper, regardless of outer area position.

items.question_text: How does the helicopter separation exception in Class B airspace (7-9-6) differ from the helicopter separation exception in Class C airspace (7-8-6)?
items.explanation: In Class B (7-9-6), VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from VFR or IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft. In Class C (7-8-6a), VFR helicopters and powered-lift aircraft need not be separated from IFR helicopters or powered-lift aircraft only. The Class B exception is broader — it exempts separation from both VFR and IFR helicopters/powered-lift, while the Class C exception exempts separation only from IFR helicopters/powered-lift. In both cases, traffic advisories and safety alerts must be issued as appropriate.

choices.text: There is no difference; both paragraphs contain identical helicopter separation exceptions.
Class B exempts VFR helicopters from separation from VFR or IFR helicopters/powered-lift; Class C exempts separation only from IFR helicopters/powered-lift.
Class C has no helicopter exception; only Class B provides helicopter separation relief.
Class C exempts all helicopter separation; Class B requires full separation for all helicopters.

items.question_text: Which of the three VFR flight plan operating areas in Oceanic FIRs permits operations based on a distance-from-shore limit rather than an altitude ceiling?
items.explanation: The Oakland FIR is unique among the three VFR operating areas: its limit is based on distance from shore (less than 100 NM seaward within controlled airspace), while the CTAs use an altitude ceiling (below FL 180). The all-FIR category applies below Oceanic CTAs.

choices.text: The Miami, Houston, and San Juan Oceanic CTAs.
The Oakland FIR, limited to less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace.
All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs, limited to 50 NM seaward.
All three areas use the same distance-from-shore limit.

items.question_text: A controller needs to report an oceanic error event. Which of the following IS a reportable category under JO 7210.632?
items.explanation: JO 7210.632 establishes reporting procedures for Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and SAO verification. Height errors are a reportable category. Speed deviations, radar ID failures, and CPDLC message errors are not among the five listed categories.

choices.text: Speed deviations.
CPDLC message errors.
Radar identification failures.
Height errors.

items.question_text: An ARTCC has a 2-minute revision to an oceanic flight's boundary estimate. Must this revision be forwarded to the receiving ATS facility?
items.explanation: ARTCCs must forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more to the appropriate ATS facility. A 2-minute revision falls below the mandatory reporting threshold and does not require forwarding.

choices.text: No, because any change to an estimate must be forwarded regardless of magnitude.
No, because all boundary crossing times must be coordinated regardless of revision size.
Yes, because only revisions of 3 minutes or more require forwarding.
Yes, but only if the receiving facility has agreed to waive the forwarding requirement in an LOA.

items.question_text: Two oceanic facilities have installed AIDC hardware. One facility manager wants to begin using AIDC for coordination immediately. Is this permitted?
items.explanation: AIDC use for ATC coordination requires three elements: the capability must be implemented, a regional Interface Control Document must govern its use, and a letter of agreement must support it between the facilities. Hardware installation alone is insufficient without the governance documents.

choices.text: No, because AIDC use also requires a regional Interface Control Document and a letter of agreement between the facilities.
Yes, because AIDC capability has been implemented, which is the only prerequisite.
Yes, but only if the facility manager notifies the regional office within 24 hours.
No, AIDC may not be used for ATC coordination under any circumstances.

items.question_text: Two aircraft on reciprocal tracks have been vertically separated. Both report passing a significant point and the controller confirms they are now separated by the applicable same-direction longitudinal minimum. What may the controller do?
items.explanation: Vertical separation on reciprocal tracks may be discontinued when both aircraft have reported passing a significant point AND they are separated by at least the applicable minimum for same-direction longitudinal spacing. The second condition — confirmed longitudinal separation — is required before vertical separation is dropped.

choices.text: Discontinue vertical separation, because both aircraft have reported passing a significant point.
Discontinue all separation, because reciprocal-track aircraft have passed each other.
Discontinue vertical separation, because passing a significant point and achieving the same-direction minimum are the required conditions.
Maintain vertical separation for another 15 minutes regardless.

items.question_text: A controller working in the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace needs to determine the applicable lateral separation minimum for a flight path defined by Grid System waypoints. What minimum applies?
items.explanation: Within the Gulf of America Low Offshore airspace, a specific minimum of 12 NM applies between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints. This is a geographically specific rule that supersedes the general lateral separation minimum.

choices.text: 50 NM, the general oceanic lateral minimum.
23 NM, the standard reduced lateral minimum for RNP-approved aircraft.
10 NM, the standard en route radar separation minimum.
12 NM between aircraft whose flight paths are defined by published Grid System waypoints.

items.question_text: Two aircraft are on intersecting flight paths. Aircraft A has 50 NM of protected airspace on each side. Aircraft B has 10 NM of protected airspace on each side. At what perpendicular distance from the flight path of the other aircraft does lateral separation exist?
items.explanation: For intersecting flight paths with constant but different width protected airspace, lateral separation exists when either aircraft is at or beyond a distance equal to the SUM of the protected airspace of both flight paths measured perpendicular to the other's flight path. Here, 50 + 10 = 60 NM.

choices.text: 60 NM, the sum of the protected airspace widths of both flight paths.
10 NM, the smaller of the two protected airspace widths.
50 NM, the larger of the two protected airspace widths.
30 NM, the average of the two protected airspace widths.

items.question_text: A controller is working a route at FL250 that has been satisfactorily flight checked with notice given to users. What is the maximum reduction in route protected airspace width permitted at this altitude before it begins widening?
items.explanation: At and above FL240, the width of protected airspace may be reduced to 10 miles on each side of the route centerline to a distance of 114.29 miles from the NAVAID, before increasing on a 5-degree angle. Below FL240, the reduced width is 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles.

choices.text: 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID.
10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles from the NAVAID.
15 miles on each side to 150 miles from the NAVAID.
No reduction is permitted at FL250.

items.question_text: Why must control action be taken when vertical separation is applied between aircraft crossing the offshore/oceanic boundary below FL 180?
items.explanation: Below FL 180, aircraft may be using local altimeter settings (QNH) on the offshore side and standard settings (QNE, 29.92) on the oceanic side. The difference between these settings can erode vertical separation if not accounted for.

choices.text: Because aircraft below FL 180 cannot maintain assigned altitudes in oceanic airspace.
Because vertical separation is never valid when aircraft cross any airspace boundary.
Because radar coverage is always lost at the offshore/oceanic boundary.
Because differences between standard altimeter setting (QNE) and local altimeter setting (QNH) could compromise the separation.

items.question_text: A controller chooses to separate an aircraft from a temporary stationary airspace reservation by vertical separation. How is the protected airspace around the reservation defined for this purpose?
items.explanation: When vertical separation is used to separate aircraft from a temporary stationary reservation, the protected geographical area is the reservation PLUS a buffer around the perimeter equivalent to one-half the lateral separation minimum. This buffer accounts for navigation uncertainty around the reservation boundary.

choices.text: The stationary reservation area plus a buffer of one-half the lateral separation minimum around the perimeter.
The stationary reservation area only, with no buffer.
The stationary reservation area plus a buffer of the full lateral separation minimum around the perimeter.
A cylinder of 5 NM radius centered on the reservation's geographic coordinates.

items.question_text: How does the longitudinal separation method for a temporary moving airspace reservation differ from the lateral method for the same reservation type?
items.explanation: For temporary moving reservations, longitudinal separation places the applicable minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft operating within the reservation, treating the reservation as a moving block. Lateral separation ensures the aircraft's protected airspace does not overlap the time-dependent geographical area of the moving reservation. The third method, vertical, uses the reservation plus a perimeter buffer.

choices.text: Longitudinal is used only for stationary reservations; lateral is used only for moving reservations.
They are identical; the terms are interchangeable for moving reservations.
Longitudinal separation applies the minimum ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation, while lateral separation keeps aircraft protected airspace clear of the geographic area.
Longitudinal separation requires a buffer of one-half the minimum; lateral does not.

items.question_text: When Section 7 North Atlantic procedures differ from the general Chapter 8 procedures, which procedures take precedence in the North Atlantic ICAO Region?
items.explanation: The North Atlantic regional application rule states that ATC services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 7 except when noted otherwise. When Section 7 procedures differ from general Chapter 8, the Section 7 procedures control as the more specific regional rule.

choices.text: The general Chapter 8 procedures, because they apply uniformly throughout all oceanic airspace.
The procedures in Section 8, because the Caribbean and North Atlantic regions share the same rules.
Whichever set of procedures provides the larger separation minimum.
The Section 7 procedures, because they are the region-specific rules that govern the North Atlantic ICAO Region.

items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, which aircraft qualify for the 60 NM or 1-degree latitude lateral minimum?
items.explanation: The 60 NM / 1-degree minimum applies to two categories: (1) supersonic aircraft operating above FL 275, and (2) aircraft with MNPS or NAT HLA authorization operating within, transiting to/from, or operating for part of their flight within/above/below NAT HLA. The reduced minimum requires track-keeping capability.

choices.text: Nonturbojet aircraft operating in WATRS only.
All RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft regardless of supersonic capability.
Supersonic aircraft above FL 275, and aircraft with MNPS or NAT HLA authorization in specified operations.
Any aircraft with a Mode S transponder operating above FL 290.

items.question_text: How does the Caribbean ICAO Region's governing rule differ from the oceanic controlled airspace rule that allows directives and LOAs to supersede Chapter 8?
items.explanation: The Caribbean regional application (8-8-1) does not mention directives or LOAs as superseding authorities. It states that services are provided with the procedures and minima in the section except when noted otherwise. This differs from 8-1-1 which explicitly permits directives and LOAs to prescribe different procedures.

choices.text: The Caribbean rule forbids any exceptions, requiring strict adherence to Section 8 in all cases.
There is no difference; all regional application paragraphs in Chapter 8 are identical.
The Caribbean rule permits only LOAs, not directives, to prescribe different procedures.
The Caribbean rule relies on section-specific exceptions rather than separately listing directives or LOAs as superseding authorities.

items.question_text: What are the two conditions that must both be met before an IFR flight in the Miami CTA may be cleared to climb or descend in VFR conditions?
items.explanation: VFR climbs and descents in the Houston, Miami, and San Juan CTAs require two conditions: (1) pilot request, AND (2) between sunrise and sunset. One hour before sunset satisfies the time condition. The pilot has requested the climb. Both conditions are met.

choices.text: No, because the climb must be completed before noon local time.
No, because VFR climbs are only permitted in the Houston CTA, not Miami.
Yes, but only if the controller also issues alternative instructions in case VFR conditions become impractical.
Yes, because the pilot requested it and it is between sunrise and sunset.

items.question_text: When Section 9 Pacific procedures are silent on a matter, what procedures should a controller apply in the Pacific ICAO Region?
items.explanation: The Pacific regional application states services are provided with Section 9 procedures and minima except when noted otherwise. When Section 9 is silent, the general Chapter 8 procedures provide the fallback framework.

choices.text: The general Chapter 8 procedures, which serve as the underlying framework when the regional section does not address a situation.
Only ICAO Pacific regional standards, disregarding Chapter 8 entirely.
The Caribbean regional procedures, because Pacific and Caribbean regions share the same rules.
The North Atlantic procedures, because they are the default for all oceanic ICAO regions.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, two aircraft on reciprocal tracks have only vertical separation available. How long must vertical separation be maintained relative to the estimated passing time?
items.explanation: For reciprocal track aircraft in the Pacific region where lateral separation is not provided, vertical separation must be provided at least 10 minutes before and after the time the aircraft are estimated to pass or are estimated to have passed. This ensures a 20-minute window of vertical separation centered on the passing point.

choices.text: Only until the aircraft have physically passed each other.
At least 10 minutes before and 10 minutes after the estimated passing time.
At least 15 minutes before and 15 minutes after the estimated passing time.
At least 5 minutes before and 5 minutes after the estimated passing time.

items.question_text: In the Pacific ICAO Region, what lateral separation minimum applies to aircraft operating without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval?
items.explanation: In the Pacific region, lateral separation has three tiers: 23 NM for ADS-C equipped with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180, 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft where authorized, and 100 NM for all other aircraft not covered by those tiers.

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.

items.question_text: How does the North American ICAO Region's application paragraph differ from the other three regional application paragraphs?
items.explanation: The North American ICAO Region application (8-10-1) does not include the phrase 'except when noted otherwise' found in the North Atlantic (8-7-1), Caribbean (8-8-1), and Pacific (8-9-1) application paragraphs. It states services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

choices.text: It omits the 'except when noted otherwise' language found in the other three regional application paragraphs.
It is identical to all other regional application paragraphs.
It permits directives and LOAs to supersede the section, unlike the other regions.
It applies only to supersonic aircraft, unlike the other regions.

items.question_text: What makes the North American ICAO Region's vertical separation rule unique among the four ICAO regions in Chapter 8?
items.explanation: While all four regional vertical separation paragraphs reference Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, only the North American region (8-10-2) additionally requires facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes. This reflects the unique operational need to manage the flight level/metric altitude interface in the Arctic CTA.

choices.text: It is the only one that permits use of ICAO Doc 4444 for vertical separation.
It is the only one that also requires facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
It is the only one that references Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5 for vertical separation.
It is the only one that requires a letter of agreement for vertical separation to be applied.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, what longitudinal minimum applies to nonturbojet aircraft?
items.explanation: In the North American ICAO Region, the basic time-based longitudinal minima are: 15 minutes between turbojet aircraft, Mach number technique minima where applicable, and 20 minutes between other (nonturbojet) aircraft.

choices.text: 30 minutes.
15 minutes.
20 minutes.
10 minutes.

items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, what lateral separation applies to an RNP 4 approved aircraft operating where RNP separation is authorized?
items.explanation: In the North American region, there are three lateral separation tiers: 23 NM for ADS-C equipped aircraft with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180 (in Anchorage Oceanic/Continental CTAs only, not Arctic CTA), 50 NM for RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft where authorized, and 90 NM for aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers.

choices.text: 50 NM.
23 NM.
90 NM.

items.question_text: An LOA permits SUA transit with using agency coordination. The using agency clears two aircraft through the area at different altitudes. What separation responsibility does the controller retain?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-4a, the controller must provide applicable separation minima between aircraft when two or more are transiting the area. Even though the using agency authorizes the transit, separation between transiting aircraft remains the controller's responsibility.

choices.text: Only vertical separation is required; lateral separation is delegated to the using agency.
The using agency assumes all separation responsibility for aircraft inside the SUA.
Separation is not required because the using agency's clearance deconflicts the aircraft.
Provide applicable separation minima between the two aircraft transiting the area.

items.question_text: A TEAL mission aircraft at FL 310 releases a dropsonde while a nonparticipating aircraft is at FL 210, 8 NM ahead on the same track. Who determines whether to delay the dropsonde release, and on what basis?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-19, the aircraft commander bears responsibility to delay release of dropsondes based solely upon traffic as issued by ATC. The controller provides the traffic advisory; the commander decides whether to proceed or delay.

choices.text: The controller, who must cancel the release if any traffic is within 10 NM of the drop point.
The aircraft commander, based solely upon the traffic information issued by ATC.
The supervisory traffic management coordinator, who deconflicts the drop with flow control.
The NOAA mission coordinator on the ground, who relays the decision through HF radio.

items.question_text: An AWACS/NORAD Special flight has requested FL 350. During expedited handling, a trainee assigns FL 320 to resolve a crossing conflict. Is this assignment within acceptable limits?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-18a, en route altitude changes are acceptable within ±2,000 feet of the requested flight level. FL 320 is 3,000 feet below the requested FL 350, exceeding the 2,000-foot tolerance. The maximum descent would be to FL 330.

choices.text: Yes. Any altitude change that resolves a conflict is acceptable for expedited handling.
No. Acceptable altitude changes are limited to ±2,000 feet from the requested flight level.
Yes. The ±2,000-foot limit applies only to climbs, not descents.
No. Altitude changes from the requested flight level are never permitted.

items.question_text: A FLYNET aircraft is already in contact with Center when it enters your approach control airspace. The pilot notes that DEN notification was accomplished by Center. What must your facility do?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-5b, the first facility establishing contact reports to the OS/CIC for DEN notification. Once DEN notification has been accomplished (as noted in the flight plan remarks per 9-2-5c), subsequent facilities do not need to re-notify. However, each facility must still provide expeditious handling. The DEN notification attaches to the flight, not to each facility.

choices.text: Transfer the aircraft back to Center because approach control cannot handle FLYNET aircraft directly.
Re-accomplish DEN notification because every ATC facility that handles a FLYNET aircraft must independently notify the DEN.
Provide routine handling since DEN notification was already done by the first facility.
Provide expeditious handling but do not re-accomplish DEN notification since it is already noted in the flight plan remarks.

items.question_text: A SAMP aircraft requests an altitude change for its nuclear sampling mission. Other IFR traffic is already established at the requested altitude. What is the controller's obligation?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-17, controllers must approve inflight clearance requests for altitude and route changes to the maximum extent possible, and other IFR aircraft may be recleared so that SAMP requests are approved. The standard requires active accommodation — not just processing the request, but potentially moving other traffic to make it work.

choices.text: Approve the change only if the SAMP aircraft accepts responsibility for separation from the other traffic.
Deny the request because the altitude is already occupied by other IFR traffic.
Approve the altitude change to the maximum extent possible; reclear the established traffic if necessary.
Defer the request until the other IFR aircraft has passed the SAMP aircraft's position.

items.question_text: How does the controller's obligation for interceptor aircraft on active air defense scramble missions differ from the obligation for flight inspection aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-7, interceptor assistance continues only until the unknown aircraft is identified — it has a defined termination condition. Flight inspection assistance (9-1-1) continues for the duration of the inspection mission and has no automatic termination upon identification. Additionally, interceptors require expedited transfer of control jurisdiction upon ADCF request, a requirement unique to air defense missions.

choices.text: Both require maximum assistance for the entire flight; there is no operational difference.
Interceptor assistance terminates when the unknown aircraft is identified; flight inspection assistance continues for the mission duration.
Flight inspection aircraft have priority over interceptors in all cases.
Interceptor assistance only applies when the aircraft is squawking emergency codes.

items.question_text: A law enforcement aircraft requests that ATC locate and track a suspect aircraft. The controller's sector is busy with moderate traffic. What limits apply to providing this assistance?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-12a2, assistance to law enforcement aircraft must not compromise approved separation minima or place the aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain, obstructions, or other aircraft. Maximum assistance is required, but it stops at the point where separation standards or safety would be compromised.

choices.text: Law enforcement assistance takes priority over all separation standards when suspect aircraft are involved.
Assistance must not compromise approved separation minima or place aircraft in unsafe proximity to terrain or other aircraft.
Assistance is limited to communicating on a separate frequency; the controller cannot provide tracking.
Controllers must decline all law enforcement assistance requests when sector traffic is moderate or greater.

items.question_text: A parachute jump aircraft requests authorization in Class D airspace. The controller can see the jump aircraft on radar and has two VFR aircraft 5 miles from the jump zone. What is the correct authorization standard?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-3a, Class D parachute jumping is authorized 'with respect to known or observed traffic' — a more permissive standard than the Class A/B/C requirement to separate nonparticipating aircraft from the jump airspace (9-7-2b). In Class D, the controller weighs known traffic conditions and authorizes accordingly, but is not required to provide positive separation.

choices.text: Authorize without restriction; no traffic advisories are needed for Class D operations.
Deny the jump because nonparticipating VFR aircraft are within 10 miles of the drop zone.
Apply the same separation standard as Class A/B/C — separate all nonparticipating aircraft from the jump airspace.
Authorize jumping with respect to known or observed traffic; the standard is more permissive than Class A/B/C separation requirements.

items.question_text: When may a controller request a fuel-dumping aircraft to fly a different route?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons, the controller may request it to fly a different route. Emergency fuel dumps are exempt from route-change requests — the aircraft's emergency takes priority over routing preferences. The rule applies in both VFR and IFR conditions.

choices.text: Only when the aircraft is operating under VFR, not IFR.
Except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons.
At any time, regardless of whether the dump is for emergency reasons.
Only when the aircraft is dumping fuel below 10,000 feet MSL.

items.question_text: What separation procedure applies to Prohibited or Restricted Areas established for security or non-ordnance hazardous activities?
items.explanation: Per 9-3-3c, certain Prohibited/Restricted Areas established for security reasons or to contain hazardous activities that do not involve aircraft ordnance are exempt from the added 500-foot vertical buffer. For these areas, the controller keeps aircraft clear of the airspace without applying the vertical buffer. For all other Prohibited/Restricted Areas, the buffer applies.

choices.text: At least 500 feet above or below the airspace limit, with 1,000 feet above FL 290.
Keep aircraft clear of the airspace without the added 500-foot vertical buffer.
At least 2,000 feet above or below the airspace limit at all altitudes.
Same separation as Warning Areas — 3 miles lateral or 500 feet vertical.

items.question_text: When separating an IFR aircraft from an aircraft dumping fuel, why is the required vertical separation different above and below the dumping aircraft?
items.explanation: Per 9-4-4, separation above the dumping aircraft is 1,000 feet (or per 4-5-1 if greater), while separation below is 2,000 feet. The larger separation below accounts for fuel descending and dispersing, which creates a greater hazard area beneath the dumping aircraft.

choices.text: Above separation is greater because aircraft climbing into falling fuel face the highest risk.
Fuel descends and disperses below the aircraft, creating a greater hazard beneath than above.
The difference is arbitrary and has no operational rationale.
Below separation is greater because ATC radar cannot reliably measure altitude below an aircraft dumping fuel.

items.question_text: How are special use frequencies assigned to ARTCCs?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-15 Note, special use frequencies are assigned to ARTCCs so that adjacent ARTCCs will not have the same frequency. They are used within the ARTCC area from the established FL base of high altitude sectors and above, with each high altitude sector having shared-use capability.

choices.text: All ARTCCs share the same special use frequency nationwide.
Adjacent ARTCCs are assigned different frequencies to prevent interference.
Each ARTCC sector within the same center receives a unique special use frequency.
Special use frequencies rotate between ARTCCs on a monthly schedule.

items.question_text: What three requirements govern parachute operations in Class A, Class B, or Class C airspace?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-2, controllers must: (a) authorize parachute operations only within airspace designated for the jumping activity; (b) separate nonparticipating aircraft from the authorized jump airspace; and (c) impose any conditions and restrictions that would promote safety of the operation.

choices.text: Authorize anywhere in Class A/B/C, provide traffic advisories only, and file a NOTAM after the operation.
Authorize only within designated airspace, separate nonparticipating aircraft, and impose safety conditions as needed.
Coordinate with the using agency, separate only IFR aircraft, and broadcast advisories to all aircraft.
Authorize per pilot request, separate only VFR aircraft, and notify the supervisor after each jump series.

items.question_text: What is a controller's responsibility regarding unusual aircraft activity near special interest sites such as nuclear power plants or dams?
items.explanation: Per 9-2-8, controllers must immediately relay any reports or information regarding unusual aircraft activities near special interest sites to supervisory/CIC personnel. Controllers have no independent monitoring or observation responsibilities unless directed by supervisory/CIC personnel.

choices.text: Vector all aircraft at least 3 NM away from these sites at all times.
Monitor these sites continuously using available radar and visual observation.
Immediately relay any reports or information to supervisory/CIC personnel.
Contact the Department of Homeland Security before taking any action.

items.question_text: At what points during a parachute operation must pertinent information be coordinated with affected ATC facilities?
items.explanation: Per 9-7-1, coordinate pertinent information prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps which begins or ends in your area of jurisdiction. Coordination is with other affected ATC facilities/sectors.

choices.text: Only after the jump is complete and the aircraft has left the area.
Prior to and at the end of each parachute jump or series of jumps.
Only when the jump involves more than 10 parachutists.
At the beginning of each shift regardless of jump scheduling.

items.question_text: When may a controller enlist the services of military radar facilities and other emergency resources to assist an aircraft in distress?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-3, controllers provide maximum assistance and may enlist available radar facilities and emergency services when the pilot requests OR when the controller deems necessary. The controller has independent authority to enlist resources.

choices.text: Only after the pilot specifically requests the additional resource by name.
When the pilot requests assistance or when the controller deems it necessary.
Only when the FAA facility becomes inoperable and can no longer provide any radar service.
Only after obtaining written authorization from the facility air traffic manager.

items.question_text: What obligation does a controller have upon learning that a VFR aircraft without a flight plan is overdue?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-5, controllers must coordinate efforts to the extent possible to assist any aircraft believed overdue, lost, or in emergency status. The obligation applies regardless of flight rules or whether a flight plan was filed.

choices.text: Wait for the aircraft operator to file a missing aircraft report before initiating any action.
Coordinate efforts to the extent possible, because the obligation applies regardless of flight plan status.
Notify only the local FSS and take no further action for VFR aircraft without flight plans.
No obligation exists because VFR aircraft operating without flight plans are outside ATC jurisdiction.

items.question_text: When emergency equipment from outside the airport proper must cross active movement areas to reach an accident scene, what is the controller's traffic management obligation?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-1-6a, the traffic-control obligation applies when routes within the airport proper are required for movement of local emergency equipment going to or from an emergency, even when the emergency occurs outside the airport proper. The controller must control other traffic to avoid conflicts along those routes.

choices.text: Close the airport until emergency vehicles confirm they have reached their destination.
Control other air and ground traffic to avoid conflicts along the routes the emergency vehicles need to use.
No obligation exists because off-airport emergency vehicles are not FAA-operated equipment.
Provide traffic advisories only — emergency vehicles are responsible for their own separation on the movement area.

items.question_text: What is the minimum required information that must be obtained before beginning assistance to an aircraft in an inflight emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-1a, minimum required information for inflight emergencies is: aircraft identification and type, nature of the emergency, and pilot's desires. Assistance must start as soon as enough information has been obtained upon which to act.

choices.text: Aircraft identification, fuel remaining in time, and number of people on board.
Aircraft identification and type, nature of the emergency, and pilot's desires.
Nature of the emergency, pilot's qualifications, and aircraft altitude.
All 17 items listed in the paragraph including both minimum and supplementary information.

items.question_text: A military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude declares an emergency and the controller has the minimum required information. The controller wants to ask the pilot several items from the supplementary list. What does the guidance say about requesting supplementary information from low-altitude military fighters?
items.explanation: Per Note 3 to paragraph 10-2-1b, controllers should normally not request supplementary information from military fighter-type aircraft at low altitudes. The single exception is: request the position of an aircraft that is not visually sighted or displayed on radar if the location is not given by the pilot.

choices.text: There is no exception — the prohibition on requesting supplementary information from low-altitude fighters is absolute.
The controller should request fuel remaining, since fuel status is always critical regardless of aircraft type.
The controller should request the aircraft's position if it is not visually sighted, on radar, or given by the pilot.
The controller should request all 14 supplementary items because the emergency makes every piece of information critical.

items.question_text: What hazard does the note to the altitude-change guidance identify for aircraft with high-bypass turbofan engines that have entered a volcanic ash cloud?
items.explanation: Per the Note to paragraph 10-2-4, damage to engines due to volcanic ash ingestion increases as engine power is increased. Therefore, climb while in the ash cloud is to be avoided where terrain permits. High-bypass turbofan engines (such as B747) are especially vulnerable to this effect.

choices.text: Ash particles clog pitot-static systems at higher altitudes, causing unreliable airspeed and altitude indications.
Engine damage from ash ingestion increases as engine power increases, so climb in the ash cloud is avoided where terrain permits.
The ash cloud's electrical properties cause complete communications blackout above a certain altitude threshold.
Engine thrust actually improves in ash because the particles act as an abrasive that cleans turbine blades.

items.question_text: What is required before an unexpected loss of radar contact combined with loss of radio communications triggers the obligation to consider an aircraft emergency exists?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-5b, the trigger is unexpected loss of BOTH radar contact AND radio communications with any IFR or VFR aircraft. The conjunction 'and' means both conditions must be present. Loss of only one does not satisfy the trigger.

choices.text: Loss of radar contact alone is sufficient, because radar is the primary means of aircraft surveillance.
Both radar contact and radio communications must be unexpectedly lost — loss of only one does not trigger the requirement.
Loss of radio communications alone is sufficient, because voice contact is essential for emergency coordination.
Either loss of radar contact or loss of radio communications independently triggers the requirement.

items.question_text: What steps must a controller complete when a VFR aircraft in weather difficulty requires a frequency change to another facility for better service?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-7a, the controller must advise the pilot of the reason for the change, request the aircraft contact the appropriate facility, and inform that facility of the situation. If the aircraft cannot communicate with the new facility, the original controller must relay information and clearances.

choices.text: Issue the new frequency without explanation to keep transmissions brief during the weather emergency.
Advise the pilot of the reason for the change, request contact with the new facility, and inform that facility of the situation.
Hand off using standard radar procedures — no special coordination is required before an emergency is formally declared.
Transfer communications only after the receiving facility confirms it has established radar contact with the aircraft.

items.question_text: What condition permits a controller to provide radar assistance to a VFR pilot who lacks IFR qualifications, has an unequipped aircraft, and declines to declare an emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-8c, radar assistance may be provided when the pilot refuses to declare an emergency but the controller independently determines that an emergency condition exists or is imminent. The controller's determination under 10-1-1c operates independently of the pilot's declaration.

choices.text: The controller must first obtain supervisory approval before providing any radar assistance without a pilot declaration.
