Radar assistance is automatic for any aircraft in IMC regardless of pilot qualifications or declarations.
When the controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent, even if the pilot refuses to declare one.
Radar assistance may never be provided to a pilot who is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency.

items.question_text: When providing radar assistance to a pilot who lacks IFR qualifications, when should turns be executed and which maneuver should be deferred while the aircraft is in IFR conditions?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-9b and 10-2-9d, turns should be made while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it can fly straight in IFR conditions. Controllers should avoid requiring a climb or descent while the aircraft is in a turn and in IFR conditions, as this increases spatial disorientation risk.

choices.text: Make all turns in IFR conditions so the pilot can practice instrument scanning during the emergency.
Make turns in VFR conditions; avoid requiring climb or descent while turning in IFR conditions.
Combine turns with altitude changes to minimize total time spent in instrument meteorological conditions.
Make turns only after the aircraft has descended to the MVA, regardless of whether in VFR or IFR conditions.

items.question_text: What information must a controller obtain when a pilot facing a bomb threat requests or appears to need technical assistance, and what must the controller refrain from doing?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-11a6, the controller must obtain the aircraft type/series/model, precise location/description of the device if known, and other pertinent details, then notify the supervisor who will contact the FAA aviation explosives expert. The controller must refrain from suggesting what actions the pilot should take concerning the bomb.

choices.text: Suggest that the pilot land immediately and evacuate; then notify the supervisor of the pilot's intended actions.
Obtain aircraft type, device location and description, and other pertinent details; notify the supervisor; refrain from suggesting pilot actions.
Ask the pilot to describe the device in detail so the controller can provide step-by-step bomb-handling instructions.
Transfer the aircraft to the supervisor's frequency so the supervisor can directly advise the pilot on security procedures.

items.question_text: What are the three sequential steps a controller takes when an aircraft requests the location of the nearest explosive detection K-9 team?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-12, the three-step process is: (1) obtain aircraft identification and position, advise supervisor; (2) when the nearest K-9 location is received, relay it to the pilot; (3) if the aircraft wishes to divert to that location, obtain an ETA and advise the supervisor.

choices.text: Contact the K-9 team directly; relay the aircraft's position to the team; issue a landing clearance to the team's airport.
Obtain aircraft ID and position, advise supervisor; relay the K-9 location to the pilot; if diverting, obtain ETA and advise supervisor.
Advise the supervisor; wait for the supervisor to coordinate with the K-9 team; relay the supervisor's instructions to the pilot.
Issue an immediate clearance to the nearest suitable airport; coordinate K-9 availability after the aircraft has landed.

items.question_text: What seven factors must a controller consider when recommending an emergency airport?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-15a, the seven factors are: remaining fuel in relation to airport distances, weather conditions, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot's qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability to the emergency airport. The note indicates weather may deserve weighted consideration.

choices.text: Fuel remaining, weather, runway length, firefighting capability, aircraft type, and pilot's total flight hours.
Fuel remaining, weather, airport conditions, NAVAID status, aircraft type, pilot qualifications, and vectoring or homing capability.
Distance to airport, fuel remaining, weather, aircraft weight, number of passengers, and emergency equipment availability.
NAVAID status, weather, airport elevation, runway surface type, aircraft category, and pilot's medical certificate class.

items.question_text: For a turbine-powered aircraft that has entered a volcanic ash cloud and indicated a distress situation exists, when may a climb clearance be initiated by ATC?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-18b, ATC should not initiate a climb clearance unless the pilot requests it or ATC first obtains the pilot's concurrence. This is because climb while in the ash cloud increases engine damage from ash ingestion (per Note to 10-2-4), especially for high-bypass turbofan engines.

choices.text: Immediately, because exiting the ash cloud is the highest priority regardless of engine damage risk.
Only when the pilot requests a climb or after ATC obtains the pilot's concurrence.
Only after the aircraft has exited the ash cloud and all engines have been verified to be operating normally.
Climb is prohibited for all turbine-powered aircraft in ash clouds — descent is the only authorized vertical maneuver.

items.question_text: What information must a controller obtain upon receiving a report of a death, illness, or other public health risk on board an aircraft?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-2-19a, the controller must obtain: call sign, number of suspected cases of illness, nature of the illnesses or other public health risk (if known), number of persons on board, number of deaths (if any), and departure and destination airports. This information is then relayed to the OMIC/OS/CIC.

choices.text: Call sign, aircraft type, pilot's name, number of persons on board, and estimated time of arrival.
Call sign, number of suspected illness cases, nature of illness if known, number of persons on board, number of deaths if any, and departure and destination airports.
Call sign, nature of illness, fuel remaining, aircraft color, and number of deaths.
Call sign, departure and destination airports, number of persons on board, and pilot's intentions for diversion.

items.question_text: After what three trigger events, combined with 30 minutes without communications or radar contact, is an aircraft considered overdue for ALNOT purposes?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-1a, an aircraft is considered overdue when 30 minutes have passed with neither communications nor radar contact since: (1) its ETA over a specified or compulsory reporting point or at a clearance limit, (2) its clearance void time, or (3) failure of a VFR or IFR aircraft to cancel its flight plan within 30 minutes after destination ETA. The procedures also apply to 'missing' or 'unreported' aircraft.

choices.text: The time the flight plan was filed, the time of last radar contact, or the time the pilot last requested a weather briefing.
ETA over a reporting point or clearance limit, clearance void time, or failure to cancel a flight plan within 30 minutes of destination ETA.
The time the aircraft departed, the time of last fuel stop, or the time the flight plan was activated.
The time of last position report, the time of last altitude change, or the time the aircraft entered the controller's airspace.

items.question_text: When comparing the information forwarded by a terminal facility to the ARTCC with the information the ARTCC later forwards to the RCC, which items are unique to the RCC notification?
items.explanation: Per paragraphs 10-3-2 and 10-3-3, the ARTCC adds two items when forwarding to the RCC that the terminal facility does not send to the ARTCC: 'facility and person calling' and 'action taken by reporting facility and proposed action.' All other items — flight plan, last transmission, last position, beacon code, persons on board, fuel status, ELT information, and remarks — are common to both notification lists.

choices.text: The notification lists are identical — the ARTCC simply forwards the same information the terminal facility provided.
Facility and person calling, and action taken by reporting facility and proposed action.
Aircraft beacon code and number of persons on board — these are terminal-only items withheld from the RCC.
Fuel status and ELT signal information — these are added by the ARTCC when the terminal facility omits them.

items.question_text: What is the normal geographic scope of an ALNOT, and under what circumstances may it be expanded?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-4a, the ALNOT is normally issued to all centers and Area B circuits covering 50 miles on either side of the route from the last reported position to destination. At the recommendation of the RCC or at the controller's discretion, the ALNOT may be expanded to cover the maximum range of the aircraft from the last known position.

choices.text: 100 miles either side of the route — the 50-mile figure in the paragraph refers to nautical miles from each reporting point.
50 miles either side of the route from last known position to destination; expandable at RCC recommendation or controller discretion.
The entire ARTCC airspace — ALNOTs automatically cover all sectors within the issuing center's boundary.
50 miles either side, and this scope is mandatory — there is no authority to expand the distribution area.

items.question_text: What is the two-tier procedure for providing an overdue aircraft's last known position to the RCC when the formal extraction tool will cause delay?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-6b, if necessary to prevent undue delay, utilize any available method to determine an initial latitude and longitude, then follow up as soon as possible with a formal lat/long using the appropriate data extraction tools. This balances SAR urgency with accuracy.

choices.text: Wait for the formal extraction — only the most accurate position data meets the standard for SAR coordination.
Provide an initial position using any available method immediately, then follow up with the formal lat/long as soon as possible.
Advise the RCC that position data is unavailable and request they proceed using the aircraft's last reported waypoint.
Use only the formal extraction tool but request the RCC expedite planning using a rough estimate in the interim.

items.question_text: Who directs the cancellation of an ALNOT, and what information must the cancellation include?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-3-7, the ALNOT is canceled when directed by the RCC after the aircraft is located or the search is abandoned. The cancellation must include pertinent information to aid the RCC, SAR teams, and FAA SAR management, explicitly including the location where the aircraft or wreckage was found.

choices.text: The issuing ARTCC cancels the ALNOT autonomously once the aircraft is located; no additional information is required.
The RCC directs cancellation; the cancellation includes the location of the aircraft or wreckage and other pertinent information.
The ALNOT expires automatically 24 hours after issuance; no formal cancellation message is needed.
The terminal facility that originated the overdue report directs cancellation after confirming the aircraft has landed safely.

items.question_text: What five time references, in order of priority, determine when the 30-minute IFR traffic restriction period begins for an overdue or unreported aircraft?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-1, the five time references in priority order are: (a) approach clearance delivery time, (b) EFC time, (c) arrival time over the NAVAID serving the destination, (d) the controller's current estimate, and (e) the time the flight plan was filed. When a higher-priority reference is available, it takes precedence.

choices.text: EFC time, approach clearance time, flight plan filing time, controller's estimate, and NAVAID estimate.
Approach clearance time, EFC time, NAVAID arrival estimate, controller's current estimate, and flight plan filing time as a last resort.
All five times are used simultaneously, and the restriction clock runs from the earliest of the five.
Only the controller's current estimate and the flight plan filing time — the other references are advisory only.

items.question_text: For what duration must airport lighting systems be operated for an unreported aircraft, and how does the responsibility differ between en route and terminal facilities?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-2, lighting must be operated for at least 30 minutes before the ETA until the aircraft is located or for 30 minutes after fuel exhaustion. En route facilities at nontower/non-FSS locations request airport management to activate the lights. Terminal facilities operate the lights directly.

choices.text: Exactly 30 minutes before ETA until exactly 30 minutes after ETA, regardless of whether the aircraft is located sooner.
At least 30 minutes before ETA until located or 30 minutes after fuel exhaustion; en route requests airport management, terminal operates directly.
Only the runway edge lights are required — approach lights and other systems are activated at the controller's discretion.
Lighting is a pilot responsibility at nontower airports; ATC has no obligation to activate or request airport lighting.

items.question_text: After the 30-minute traffic suspension period for an overdue aircraft expires, what are the requirements for resuming normal ATC?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-4-3, normal ATC may resume if the operators or pilots of other affected aircraft concur, and this concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. If concurrence is withdrawn during that 30-minute window, normal ATC must be suspended again. Concurrence is required from all affected operators/pilots.

choices.text: Normal ATC resumes automatically once the suspension period expires — no concurrence or further action is required.
Affected operators or pilots must concur, and that concurrence must be maintained for 30 minutes after the suspension period expires.
The facility air traffic manager must authorize resumption in writing after verifying the suspension period has fully elapsed.
A simple majority of affected pilots must concur — unanimous concurrence from all affected operators is not required.

items.question_text: Five minutes have elapsed since two-way radio communications with an IFR aircraft were lost. What additional action is required?
items.explanation: After 5 minutes without reestablished communications, consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC under the suspicious-flight procedures. Attempts to reestablish communications and other appropriate lost-communications actions continue; the five-minute mark adds the suspicious-activity reporting requirement.

choices.text: Continue ordinary communications attempts; no additional notification is required until the aircraft becomes overdue.
Consider the aircraft's or pilot's activity suspicious and report it to the OS/CIC while continuing appropriate lost-communications actions.
Immediately issue an ALNOT and notify the RCC, regardless of the aircraft's route or other available information.
Terminate further communication attempts and protect only the aircraft's filed route.

items.question_text: When an aircraft carrying explosive cargo is making an emergency landing, to which entities must the explosive cargo information be relayed?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-5-1, the explosive cargo information must be relayed to the emergency equipment crew, the airport management, and — when requested by the pilot — the appropriate military agencies. The controller must also inform the pilot of the safest or least congested airport areas.

choices.text: Only the emergency equipment crew, because they are the primary first responders to any aircraft emergency.
The emergency equipment crew, airport management, and — when the pilot requests — the appropriate military agencies.
The airport management only, who then assumes responsibility for notifying all other relevant entities.
The emergency equipment crew, airport management, and military agencies — military notification is mandatory regardless of pilot request.

items.question_text: What actions must a controller take for an airborne aircraft already within an activated Debris Response Area (DRA) following a space launch mishap?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-5-2, for aircraft already within an activated DRA, the controller must issue the DRA location, provide hazard information, and advise the pilot to exit the DRA. The alert broadcast must be reissued every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event.

choices.text: Clear the aircraft to land at the nearest airport immediately, regardless of position relative to the debris field.
Issue the DRA location, provide hazard information, and advise the pilot to exit the DRA.
Maintain normal ATC services — once inside the DRA, changing course could increase collision risk.
Issue only the DRA location and let the pilot determine whether to remain in or exit the area.

items.question_text: An oceanic aircraft's expected communication time has passed and 35 minutes have elapsed without contact. Which emergency phase applies?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-2a, the Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA) exists when communication has not been received within 30 minutes after the time a communication should have been received, or after the first unsuccessful attempt to establish communication, whichever is earlier. This is the first and least severe of the three oceanic emergency phases.

choices.text: Alert Phase (ALERFA), because any communication failure beyond 30 minutes escalates to the alert level.
Uncertainty Phase (INCERFA).
Distress Phase (DETRESFA), because loss of communication in oceanic airspace always indicates grave danger.
No emergency phase exists until the aircraft also fails to arrive at its destination.

items.question_text: When an aircraft over water requests sea condition information or surface vessel assistance, from what source is this information obtained?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-4a, weather, sea conditions, ditching information, and surface vessel assistance should be requested from the RCC. An AMVER SURPIC should also be requested if the aircraft asks or if control personnel deem it beneficial.

choices.text: From the aircraft's company dispatch, because the operator arranges maritime assistance for its own flights.
From the nearest Coast Guard station, because maritime rescue coordination is a Coast Guard responsibility.
From the RCC, with an AMVER SURPIC requested if the aircraft asks or the controller deems it beneficial.
From the AMVER Center only, because the automated system provides all necessary weather and sea condition data.

items.question_text: What type of clearance limit should be used for a SAR aircraft being positioned to assist a distressed aircraft in oceanic airspace?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-6-5b, the SAR aircraft must be cleared to a fixed clearance limit rather than to the aircraft in distress, which is a moving fix. Route clearances should be consistent with that of the distressed aircraft. A moving fix creates an unstable clearance boundary.

choices.text: The distressed aircraft's current position, to minimize the time needed to establish visual contact.
A fixed clearance limit, because the distressed aircraft is a moving fix that cannot serve as a stable clearance boundary.
The nearest suitable airport, since all SAR operations in oceanic airspace must terminate at a landing facility.
No clearance limit — SAR aircraft operating under emergency authority receive only heading and altitude assignments.

items.question_text: What entities must issue alerting advisories for a ground missile emergency in the en route environment?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-1, en route facilities must have alerting advisories issued by air carrier company radio stations (for VFR company aircraft in the vicinity) and by FSSs adjacent to the emergency location. Terminal facilities relay all information to the ARTCC and disseminate locally.

choices.text: Only the ARTCC, which assumes all alerting responsibility for ground missile emergencies regardless of facility type.
Air carrier company radio stations for VFR company aircraft and FSSs adjacent to the emergency location.
The nearest approach control facility and the overlying ARTCC, operating jointly.
Only air carrier company radio stations — FSS participation is limited to terminal-area emergencies.

items.question_text: What lateral and vertical separation minima apply when rerouting IFR and SVFR aircraft around a ground missile emergency location?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-2, the minima are: 1 mile lateral separation between the emergency location and the aircraft under radar control (or the protected route airspace), and 6,000 feet vertical separation above the surface over the emergency location. Greater minima apply when suggested by the notifying official.

choices.text: 3 miles lateral and 2,000 feet vertical — the standard IFR separation minima applied to all ground hazards.
1 mile lateral and 6,000 feet vertical, or greater when suggested by the notifying official.
5 miles lateral and 4,000 feet vertical, which is the standard en route emergency separation minimum.
2 miles lateral and 3,000 feet vertical, which is the reduced separation standard authorized for emergency operations.

items.question_text: What must controllers advise all aircraft to do regarding smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency?
items.explanation: Per paragraph 10-7-4, controllers must advise all aircraft to avoid any observed smoke columns in the vicinity of a ground missile emergency. This applies to all aircraft regardless of flight rules or the type of ATC service being received.

choices.text: Fly above the smoke column, since vertical separation is the approved avoidance method.
Avoid any observed smoke columns.
Report the smoke column's position and estimated height for inclusion in the next ATIS broadcast.
No specific smoke advisory is required — the standard separation minima cover all associated hazards.

items.question_text: What is the operational significance of recognizing that the ATCS is integral in the execution of the traffic management mission?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-1c, the ATCS is recognized as integral in the execution of the traffic management mission. This means traffic management is not conducted solely by a separate traffic management unit — controllers on position are essential participants in carrying out the mission. The controller helps execute TMIs and provides real-time feedback; the controller is not merely a recipient of external flow instructions. The plausible misconception is that ATCS personnel are implementers rather than participants, which would relegate them to a passive role the source does not support.
items.supersedes: Under 11-1-1, the ATCS is recognized as integral in the execution of the traffic management mission.

choices.text: Traffic management functions are carried out by a separate unit; controllers receive and implement those decisions without participating in the mission itself.
Air traffic control specialists are essential participants in carrying out the traffic management mission, not merely recipients of external flow instructions.
ATCS personnel may modify or cancel any traffic management initiative independently because their integral role gives them override authority.
The ATCS role in traffic management is limited to reporting observed congestion to the TMU after delays have already developed.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly pairs a TBFM metering-time compliance challenge with its authorized response?
items.explanation: Per 11-1-3b1, when DCT instability prevents reliable metering-time accuracy for specific aircraft, TMIs such as MIT or MINIT may be used between those aircraft to assist delay absorption until stability resumes. The other pairings misstate the authorized response. A sector-wide exception requires explicit authorization in a facility directive (11-1-3b2), not independent controller action. When compliance is not possible for any reason, the controller must coordinate with the OS/CIC, traffic management services, and adjacent facilities as appropriate (11-1-3c); silent deviation is not authorized. TMU resequencing calls for caution to minimize impact on surrounding traffic and user preferences (note), not suspension of metering.

choices.text: When a controller cannot meet a metering time for any reason, the controller may deviate from the time without notification as long as separation from other aircraft is maintained.
When an ARTCC sector consistently struggles to meet the plus-or-minus-1-minute standard, the sector controller may adopt a wider tolerance at their discretion.
When DCT instability prevents reliable metering times for specific aircraft, use MIT or MINIT between those aircraft until stability resumes.
When TBFM-generated times fluctuate due to TMU resequencing, suspend all metering operations for the affected flights and manage without reference to TBFM.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly describes how the separation obligation for a Controlled VFR (CVFR) operation differs from that for an ordinary VFR operation in Canadian controlled airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-2b, ATC applies IFR procedures to CVFR operations, which per 12-1-4 means applying a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum regardless of weather. Ordinary VFR operations fall outside the separation obligation — ATC does not provide separation services to VFR traffic. The distinction is based on clearance type, not weather conditions or pilot request. A common misconception is that CVFR operations receive reduced separation because the pilot maintains visual reference to the ground — this is incorrect; CVFR receives full IFR-equivalent separation.

choices.text: CVFR operations receive separation only when operating in IMC; ordinary VFR operations receive it only in VMC.
Both CVFR and ordinary VFR operations are treated identically — neither receives separation services from ATC in any weather condition.
A lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum must be applied to CVFR operations regardless of weather; ordinary VFR operations do not receive ATC separation services.
CVFR operations receive separation only when the pilot specifically requests it; ordinary VFR operations receive it automatically without a request.

items.question_text: Which two requirements must both be met before a clearance to maintain 'at least 1,000 feet-on-top' may be issued in Canadian airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-3, the two mandatory conditions are (a) the pilot requests the clearance and (b) the pilot will not operate within Class A or Class B airspace. Both must be satisfied — they function as an AND gate. Weather verification is the pilot's responsibility, not a controller issuance condition. The pilot's request serves as confirmation that conditions are adequate (at least 1,000 feet above all cloud, haze, smoke, or other formation, with 3 miles flight visibility).

choices.text: The pilot files a CVFR flight plan and agrees to maintain 3 miles flight visibility throughout the operation.
The pilot requests the clearance and confirms the operation will not enter Class A or Class B airspace.
ATC verifies the ceiling is at least 1,000 feet and the pilot requests the clearance.
The pilot agrees to remain at or below 18,000 feet ASL and files a flight plan with a designated alternate airport.

items.question_text: Which of the following correctly states when a lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation minimum must be applied under Canadian ATC procedures?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-4, the separation obligation covers operations conducted under an IFR or CVFR clearance, regardless of weather conditions. Ordinary VFR operations are excluded — they do not receive ATC separation services. Weather conditions are irrelevant: the obligation applies equally in VMC and IMC. CVFR operations receive the same separation as IFR operations without needing to request it. The strongest distractor is the one limiting separation to IFR operations only in IMC, which misreads the rule's 'regardless of weather' clause and excludes CVFR operations that are equally entitled to separation.

choices.text: Only between two IFR operations when both are in instrument meteorological conditions.
Between any two operations in controlled airspace, regardless of whether they hold an IFR or CVFR clearance.
Between IFR operations in any weather, and between CVFR operations only when the pilot specifically requests separation.
Between operations conducted under an IFR or CVFR clearance, regardless of weather conditions.

items.question_text: Under what condition does the standard Canadian lost-communications 10-minute altitude maintenance period give way to an earlier climb for crossing into U.S. airspace?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-5, the border-crossing exception activates when the operation will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit. In that case, the climb to the border-crossing altitude begins at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary — potentially earlier than the standard 10-minute hold period. This ensures the operation is at the proper altitude upon border crossing rather than climbing through U.S. airspace. The exception is not triggered by the destination country, the distance to the border in nautical miles, or pilot discretion — it depends solely on whether the boundary crossing occurs within the 10-minute window.

choices.text: When the destination airport is located in the United States, the border-crossing climb timing replaces the standard 10-minute hold regardless of the distance from the clearance limit to the border.

When the filed route enters U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after passing the clearance limit, the climb to the border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary.
When the clearance limit is located inside U.S. airspace, the standard 10-minute hold is shortened to 5 minutes for all turbine-powered operations.

When the pilot has filed U.S. airspace in the flight plan, the climb to border-crossing altitude may begin at the pilot's discretion based on terrain and weather encountered after the clearance limit.

items.question_text: What determines whether official ceiling and visibility reports must be evaluated alongside IFR traffic when processing a special VFR request in a Canadian control zone?
items.explanation: Per 12-1-7a and 12-1-7b, the SVFR evaluation criteria depend on whether an airport controller is on duty. With a controller present, the decision is based solely on current or anticipated IFR traffic. Without a controller on duty, both IFR traffic AND official ceiling and visibility reports must be evaluated together — neither factor alone is sufficient. The distinction exists because an on-duty controller can directly assess current conditions, while an absent controller must rely on official reports. The type of operation, time of day, and reported ceiling do not change which criteria apply.

choices.text: The type of operation requesting SVFR — ceiling and visibility reports apply only to fixed-wing requests, not to helicopter operations.
The time of day — during daylight hours only IFR traffic is considered, but at night both traffic and official weather reports must be evaluated.
Whether an airport controller is on duty — when one is on duty, only IFR traffic is considered; when none is on duty, both IFR traffic AND official ceiling and visibility reports must be evaluated.
The reported ceiling — if the ceiling is below 1,000 feet, both traffic and weather are evaluated; if above 1,000 feet, only traffic is considered.

items.question_text: Which authorized trial-plan use covers assessing whether a requested higher altitude can be granted?
items.explanation: 13-1-3(b) authorizes trial planning to evaluate the feasibility of granting user requests. A pilot asking for a different altitude is a user request, and the trial plan is assessing whether it can be granted without creating new conflicts. This is distinct from evaluating solutions to existing predicted conflicts (a), removing a flight direction constraint (c), or removing a static restriction (d).

choices.text: Evaluating the feasibility of removing a static restriction for an aircraft.
Evaluating solutions to a predicted conflict between two aircraft.
Evaluating the feasibility of granting a user request.
Evaluating the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint.

items.question_text: Which trial-plan category covers correcting an altitude inappropriate for direction of flight?
items.explanation: 13-1-3(c) specifically authorizes trial planning to evaluate the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint—defined as an inappropriate altitude for direction of flight. This is a distinct category from evaluating general user requests (b), predicted conflict solutions (a), or static restriction removal (d). The example given in the paragraph is the IAFDOF situation.

choices.text: Evaluating the feasibility of removing a flight direction constraint.
Evaluating the feasibility of granting a user request.
Evaluating solutions to predicted conflicts.
Evaluating the feasibility of removing a static restriction.

items.question_text: Which Free Text Area abbreviation means a clearance was relayed through a non-ATC facility?
items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, abbreviation 'C' means 'ATC clears (when clearance relayed through non-ATC facility).' This is the correct abbreviation for a clearance relayed through a non-ATC facility. 'B' means Center clearance delivered (within the ARTCC), 'N' means clearance not delivered, and 'Z' means Tower jurisdiction.

choices.text: C — ATC clears (when clearance relayed through non-ATC facility).
B — Center clearance delivered.
N — Clearance not delivered.
Z — Tower jurisdiction.

items.question_text: When control information is present, may the ACL Free Text Area be closed?
items.explanation: 13-1-8(d) requires the Free Text Area to remain open and visible when it contains control information. Closing it violates this requirement. The information must be kept visible so it is accessible for reference. Only when the information is no longer relevant may it be updated or deleted.

choices.text: Yes, the Free Text Area may be closed once the information has been entered, because the system retains the data.
No, the Free Text Area must remain open and visible when it contains control information. Closing it violates the management requirement.
Yes, but only if the information has also been relayed verbally to all affected sector positions.
No, the Free Text Area must remain open for the entire duration of the controller's shift regardless of content.

items.question_text: An ESDT overdue aircraft notification appears for a flight. The controller updates the aircraft's route of flight, and the notification is removed from the display. Has the controller satisfied all requirements for responding to the overdue aircraft notification?
items.explanation: No. While updating the aircraft's route of flight removes the ESDT overdue aircraft notification (per the note to 13-1-12), the body of the paragraph requires the controller to also take appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. Removing the automation notification does not replace the required overdue aircraft procedures. The two actions serve different purposes: one clears the display indicator, the other initiates search and rescue or notification procedures.

choices.text: Yes. Updating the route of flight both removes the notification and satisfies the complete overdue aircraft response.
Yes, but only if the route update is entered within 10 minutes of the notification appearing.

No. The controller must also take the appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. The route update removes the display notification but does not replace the required overdue aircraft procedures.
No. The notification can only be removed by the operations supervisor after an investigation is completed.

items.question_text: During a surveillance outage, a controller uses electronic flight data to determine whether two aircraft are maintaining the required separation. The electronic flight data appears current. What does the governing paragraph authorize electronic flight data to be used for during this outage?
items.explanation: 13-1-16 authorizes electronic flight data use during a surveillance or flight data outage only to support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures. It does not authorize using electronic flight data as a substitute for radar separation. The data supports awareness of aircraft positions and intentions during the transition—it is not a separation tool.

choices.text: To support situational awareness while the facility transitions to alternate automation capabilities or nonradar procedures. It is not authorized as a substitute for radar separation.
To provide radar separation between aircraft for the duration of the outage, because all other separation data sources are unavailable.
To serve as the primary surveillance source for separation below FL180 where reduced standards apply during outages.
To be referenced only during post-outage review; it must not be consulted while the outage is in progress.

items.question_text: Does ATOP decision support include aircraft-to-terrain separation?
items.explanation: 13-2-1(a) states ATOP provides controllers with automated decision support tools to establish, monitor and maintain separation between aircraft, and aircraft to airspace and terrain. Unlike ERAM EDST which predicts conflicts between aircraft and between aircraft and special use or designated airspace only, ATOP includes terrain separation as part of its scope. The aircraft-to-terrain separation capability is a distinguishing feature of ATOP.

choices.text: No. Terrain separation is outside ATOP's scope, which is limited to aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace separation, similar to ERAM EDST.
Yes, but only if the terrain is located within special use airspace boundaries.
No. ATOP is strictly an oceanic communication system and does not provide separation decision support tools.
Yes. ATOP provides decision support tools to establish, monitor, and maintain separation between aircraft and terrain, in addition to aircraft-to-aircraft and aircraft-to-airspace separation.

items.question_text: A conflict probe red alert appears for two aircraft in ATOP oceanic airspace whose projected paths remain geometrically separate throughout the protected segment. After verifying adequate spacing using the range/bearing tool, how should the controller proceed?
items.explanation: 13-2-2(a)(3)(a)(1) authorizes overriding conflict probe when applying a separation standard not recognized by conflict probe, as listed in subparagraph a8(a) or identified by facility directive. Nonintersecting paths (8-4-2a2) is listed in a8(a). The controller may override the probe, apply the appropriate nonintersecting-paths separation standard using additional decision support tools, and must continue to ensure separation is maintained until the conflict is resolved.

choices.text: Take no action on the alert, because red conflicts must always be resolved exclusively through conflict probe guidance.

Override conflict probe and use the range/bearing tool to establish and maintain the appropriate separation standard for nonintersecting paths, while continuing to ensure separation until the conflict is resolved.
Resolve the conflict using conflict probe results exclusively, because a red alert always takes precedence over the unsupported-standard provisions.
Suppress the alert and notify the operations supervisor that conflict probe has malfunctioned for this aircraft pair.

items.question_text: Which electronic flight-strip flag identifies Mach Number Technique longitudinal separation?
items.explanation: 13-2-3(c)(1)(b) lists four reduced separation flags that must be selected appropriately. M corresponds to Mach Number Technique (MNT). R is for Reduced MNT, which is a different standard. D is Distance-based longitudinal, and W is RVSM (vertical). Each flag serves a distinct separation method and must be correctly matched.

choices.text: R — Reduced MNT, because all Mach number operations use the reduced standard.
M — Mach Number Technique (MNT).
W — RVSM, because MNT requires reduced vertical separation.
D — Distance-based longitudinal, because MNT is a longitudinal separation method.

items.question_text: What additional buffer does ATOP apply when its weather deviation tool supports RNP distance-based longitudinal separation?
items.explanation: The weather deviation tool adds an additional CDI (Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting) mileage buffer to the required separation calculated by ATOP. This buffer accounts for the navigation uncertainties when aircraft are deviating around weather. It is specific to RNP distance-based longitudinal separation applications, not radar or other separation types.

choices.text: A 10 NM lateral buffer applied in addition to the standard RNP containment value.
A time-based buffer of two minutes added to the longitudinal time standard.
A CDI (Converging, Diverging, and Intersecting) mileage buffer added to the required separation.
No additional buffer is added; the weather deviation tool only provides visual depiction of weather cells.

items.question_text: After TDLS commissioning, how long must ATIS announce PDC availability?
items.explanation: 14-1-1(f) specifies a minimum of 60 days following TDLS commissioning for the ATIS broadcast. The 60-day requirement is a floor, not a ceiling; the facility may broadcast longer if needed. The countdown starts from TDLS commissioning, not from the first PDC transmission or the date the facility directive is published.

choices.text: A minimum of 30 days following the first PDC transmission.
A minimum of 60 days following the commissioning of the TDLS.
A minimum of 90 days following publication of the facility directive.
Indefinitely; every ATIS broadcast must include PDC availability once TDLS is commissioned.

items.question_text: How must a PDC clearance be delivered after its flight plan is amended?
items.explanation: 14-1-1(d) prohibits PDC transmission of amended or revised flight plans. The clearance for any revised or amended flight plan must be verbally issued to the flight crew. This is an absolute prohibition — there is no exception for minor amendments or for amendments that the TAS could technically display. The only exception is in the note: a flight plan that initially generates in the tower with an automation-assigned route (e.g., ADR) is not considered revised or amended.

choices.text: Transmit the original PDC as-is, since the amendment occurred after the PDC was prepared.
Modify the PDC fields to match the amendment and transmit the updated clearance.
Verbally issue the clearance to the flight crew, since PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans.
Cancel the PDC and file a new flight plan; a new PDC must be generated from scratch.

items.question_text: After commissioning CPDLC departure clearance, how long must ATIS announce its availability?
items.explanation: 14-1-2(e) mirrors 14-1-1(f) but with a different trigger: for a minimum of 60 days following the commissioning of CPDLC capability (not TDLS, which triggers the PDC broadcast), the ATIS must broadcast that CPDLC is available. The two requirements are parallel but triggered by different system commissionings.

choices.text: The ATIS must broadcast CPDLC availability only if PDC is also available at the same facility.
No ATIS broadcast is required for CPDLC; only PDC commissioning triggers the broadcast requirement.
The ATIS must broadcast CPDLC availability permanently once the capability is commissioned.
The ATIS must broadcast CPDLC availability for a minimum of 60 days following commissioning of the CPDLC capability.

items.question_text: How does amendment handling differ between PDC and CPDLC departure clearances?
items.explanation: 14-1-1(d) states PDC does not permit amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted at all. 14-1-2(c) states CPDLC permits amended or revised flight plans to be transmitted, but those that cannot be delivered using CPDLC must be verbally issued. The difference is inherent in the systems: PDC has no mechanism to carry amendments, while CPDLC does — but only to the extent the TAS predefined fields can express the amendment. If the amendment cannot be expressed in those fields, CPDLC DCL reverts to the same verbal requirement as PDC.

choices.text: PDC can transmit minor amendments but not major route changes; CPDLC can transmit any amendment.
Both systems can transmit amendments, but only CPDLC requires the ATIS broadcast during the 60-day window.
CPDLC DCL permits amended flight plans to be transmitted; PDC does not permit them at all.
PDC permits amendments only when the ATIS confirms PDC availability; CPDLC has no such restriction.

items.question_text: What do NO SID, ----, and SEE SID represent in their respective DCL selectable fields?
items.explanation: Fields 1, 2, and 7 each have their own distinct convention for indicating that no data is being provided or that the pilot should reference another source. Field 1 uses NO SID (14-1-3(a)(1)(c)), Field 2 uses ---- (14-1-3(a)(2)(c)), and Field 7 uses SEE SID (14-1-3(a)(7)). They are not interchangeable — each field has a specific 'no data' or 'delegated' convention, and using the wrong one for a given field would be procedurally incorrect.

choices.text: All three mean the same thing: the field contains no controller-entered data and the pilot should disregard it.
NO SID, ----, and SEE SID are interchangeable across all nine selectable fields.
These conventions apply only when the TAS drop-down menu has no available entries for that field.
Each field uses its own distinct convention for indicating no data, a delegated reference, or that the field is not populated.

items.question_text: What belongs in Selectable Field 5 when no SID-based initial altitude or vertical guidance exists?
items.explanation: 14-1-3(a)(5): when no SID is assigned or the assigned SID does not contain either an initial altitude or vertical guidance, Selectable Field 5 must contain the instruction MAINTAIN (assigned altitude). Field 5 is the fallback when Field 4 (CLIMB VIA) does not apply because there is no SID-based vertical guidance.

choices.text: CLIMB VIA SID, since Field 4 always applies when a SID reference exists.
Leave Field 5 blank and place the altitude in Field 6 instead.
MAINTAIN (assigned altitude).
SEE SID, directing the pilot to consult the SID chart for the initial altitude.

items.question_text: An aircraft is in level cruise at FL370, well clear of any critical phase of flight, and the controller wants to issue a routine route amendment. Voice frequencies are congested. Another aircraft is on final approach in IMC, and the controller needs to issue an immediate speed reduction. For which aircraft may the controller appropriately use CPDLC?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(c) requires controllers to minimize CPDLC during critical phases of flight (final approach is a critical phase). 14-2-1(d) prohibits using CPDLC for immediate or expeditious clearances unless voice is not operationally feasible. The cruising aircraft has no restriction preventing CPDLC use. The aircraft on final approach faces two prohibitions: critical phase (minimize CPDLC) and immediate clearance (do not use CPDLC). Voice should be used for the approach aircraft.

choices.text: Use CPDLC for the cruising aircraft's routine amendment; use voice for the approach aircraft's immediate speed reduction.
Use voice for both, since any use of CPDLC during busy operations is discouraged.
Use CPDLC for both, since voice frequencies are congested and CPDLC augments voice for all clearance types.
Use CPDLC for the immediate speed reduction since it is time-critical, and voice for the routine amendment.

items.question_text: An aircraft has an open trajectory-altering clearance (TAC) that was sent via CPDLC and awaits a pilot response. The sector is about to transfer communications for this aircraft to the next sector. What must the controller do regarding the open TAC before transfer, unless otherwise coordinated?
items.explanation: 14-2-1(e) requires the controller to ensure there are no open trajectory-altering clearances prior to transfer of communication unless otherwise coordinated. An open TAC represents an unresolved change to the aircraft's flight path — transferring the aircraft with an unresolved TAC could leave the receiving controller unaware of the pending trajectory change. The TAC must be resolved (acknowledged, completed, or canceled) before the transfer.

choices.text: Transfer communications and let the receiving sector manage the open TAC.
Cancel the open TAC and reissue it as a non-trajectory-altering clearance before transfer.
Ensure no open trajectory-altering clearances remain prior to transfer, unless otherwise coordinated.
Leave the TAC open but annotate the handoff with a note about the pending clearance.

items.question_text: An IC mismatch indicator appears in the FDB for an aircraft. Controller A, who is working a different sector in the same ARTCC, sees the indicator on their display and reaches to acknowledge it. Controller B has the aircraft on their voice frequency. Who must respond to the IC mismatch indicator, and how?
items.explanation: 14-2-2(a) assigns the obligation to the controller who has the aircraft on voice frequency (Controller B), who must use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the indicator. The note clarifies that all sectors in the controlling ARTCC displaying an FDB will show the indicator — this means Controller A can see it, but the response obligation belongs exclusively to the voice-frequency controller. Anyone else acknowledging it without coordination would violate the procedure.

choices.text: Either controller may acknowledge the indicator, since all sectors in the controlling ARTCC display it.
Controller A must acknowledge the indicator to clear the FDB for their sector; Controller B issues the altitude verification by voice.
Neither controller should acknowledge it; the system automatically resolves IC mismatches after a preset timeout.
Controller B must use voice communication to verify the assigned altitude and acknowledge the indicator; Controller A sees it but should not acknowledge it.

items.question_text: During a CPDLC shutdown, what two obligations stop new messages and resolve pending uplinks?
items.explanation: 14-2-3(c) imposes two separate obligations during a CPDLC shutdown: (1) broadcast a voice message alerting all pilots to the shutdown and requesting no further downlinks — this prevents new CPDLC traffic from being initiated; and (2) take action to resolve, by voice, any open or abnormally closed uplinks with each affected aircraft — this ensures no clearance ambiguity remains. These are sequential and complementary: first stop new traffic, then clean up existing transactions.

choices.text: Notify the next facility of the shutdown, and log all open uplinks for later resolution when CPDLC is restored.
Broadcast a voice alert to pilots to stop downlinks, and resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice with each aircraft.
Terminate CPDLC connections for all aircraft, and advise pilots to squawk 7600 until further notice.
Cancel all pending uplinks from the ground system, and instruct pilots to disregard any undelivered CPDLC messages.

items.question_text: When does an altitude uplink automatically include an altimeter setting?
items.explanation: Per the note to 14-2-3(a), automated altimeter settings are only sent in response to a monitor transfer of communication (TOC) or an altitude uplink when the assigned altitude is below FL 180. Since FL175 is below FL 180, the system will automatically send the altimeter setting. If the aircraft were at or above FL 180, the system would not automatically include the altimeter, and the controller must issue it manually per Chapter 2, Section 7 if needed.

choices.text: Yes, because FL175 is below FL 180 and the altitude uplink triggers an automated altimeter setting.
No, automated altimeters are only sent on a monitor TOC, never on an altitude uplink.
No, automated altimeters are only sent when the assigned altitude is at or above FL 180.
Yes, automated altimeters are sent with every altitude uplink regardless of the assigned altitude.

items.question_text: A CPDLC-connected aircraft is operating outside VHF coverage when the controller determines that a complex reroute would be more efficiently explained by voice via SATCOM. Under what conditions may the controller use voice for this CPDLC-connected aircraft?
items.explanation: 14-3-1(b) permits voice communications for CPDLC aircraft when it provides an operational advantage and/or when workload or equipment capabilities demand. This is a permissive exception to the primary-means rule in 14-3-1(a). The controller must weigh the operational advantage of voice (clearer explanation of a complex reroute) against the primary-means preference for CPDLC outside VHF coverage. The key is that voice is permitted — not required — when these conditions exist.

choices.text: Voice must never be used outside VHF coverage once a CPDLC connection is active.
Voice may be used only after the CPDLC connection is terminated and the pilot acknowledges the termination.
Voice may be used when it provides an operational advantage and/or when workload or equipment capabilities demand it.
Voice may be used only when the CPDLC message set cannot express the clearance, regardless of operational advantage.

items.question_text: A CPDLC-connected oceanic aircraft is operating within VHF coverage. The pilot sends a route request via CPDLC. What communication medium should the controller use to respond?
items.explanation: 14-3-1(d) establishes a symmetry rule: when a pilot communicates via CPDLC, the response should be via CPDLC. This rule applies regardless of whether the aircraft is inside or outside VHF coverage. The controller should respond on the same medium the pilot used to initiate the exchange, maintaining CPDLC message continuity and ensuring proper closure of the CPDLC transaction.

choices.text: Respond via voice, since the aircraft is within VHF coverage and voice is more efficient.
Respond via CPDLC, since the pilot initiated the communication using CPDLC.
Respond via CPDLC only if the message set contains the appropriate response; otherwise use voice.
It does not matter; the controller may choose either medium based on workload.

items.question_text: A controller is preparing to transfer communications for an oceanic CPDLC aircraft to an adjacent facility. The receiving facility's CPDLC system is temporarily out of service. How must the transfer of communications be handled?
items.explanation: 14-3-2(b): when a receiving facility is not CPDLC capable (including when its CPDLC system is out of service), the transfer of communications must be made in accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement (LOAs). This contrasts with 14-3-2(a) where a CPDLC-capable receiving facility gets an automatic data link transfer within facility-adapted parameters. The non-CPDLC transfer relies on pre-coordinated procedures rather than automated data link handoff.

choices.text: The automatic data link transfer proceeds normally; the receiving facility's CPDLC system will queue the transfer until restored.
The controller must delay the transfer until the receiving facility's CPDLC system is restored to service.
The controller terminates the CPDLC connection and transfers the aircraft as a non-CPDLC flight using voice-only procedures.
The transfer must be made in accordance with local directives and Letters of Agreement, since the receiving facility is not currently CPDLC capable.

items.question_text: How is a normal data-link transfer completed when the receiving facility supports CPDLC?
items.explanation: 14-3-2(a): when the receiving facility is CPDLC capable, the data link transfer is automatic and accomplished within facility-adapted parameters. This means the controller does not manually initiate the CPDLC portion of the transfer — the system handles it based on pre-configured facility parameters. The automatic nature of the transfer reduces controller workload and standardizes the handoff process.

choices.text: The controller must manually send a transfer message through CPDLC to the receiving facility before the voice handoff.
The data link transfer is automatic and accomplished within facility-adapted parameters; no manual CPDLC transfer action is required.
The automatic transfer applies only to the voice frequency; the CPDLC connection requires manual coordination.
The transfer is automatic only for FANS 1/A aircraft; all other aircraft require manual CPDLC transfer.

items.question_text: What phraseology begins initial voice contact when CPDLC clearance delivery is uncertain?
items.explanation: 14-3-3(e) prescribes the exact phraseology for initial voice contact when there has been uncertainty about CPDLC clearance delivery: (Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message). This phraseology alerts the pilot that the controller is following up on a potential CPDLC delivery issue and sets the context for verifying whether the clearance was received. The phraseology is mandatory, not optional, when uncertainty about clearance delivery exists.

choices.text: "(Call Sign) Confirm CPDLC Clearance, (message)."
"(Call Sign) Verify CPDLC Delivery, (message)."
"(Call Sign) CPDLC Failure, (message)."
"(Call Sign) CPDLC Status Check, (message)."

items.question_text: CPDLC service is being canceled for an oceanic aircraft. The controller advises the pilot as early as possible to facilitate a smooth transition to voice. What additional action should the controller take, workload permitting?
items.explanation: 14-3-3(d): the controller must advise the pilot as early as possible when CPDLC service is to be canceled. Workload permitting, the controller should also advise the pilot of the reason for the termination of data link. The mandatory requirement is early notification; the reason is a should-do when workload allows. Understanding the distinction between 'must advise as early as possible' and 'should advise the reason' is important for operational prioritization.

choices.text: Advise the pilot of the reason for the termination of data link, workload permitting.
Send a connection request message (CR1) to reestablish CPDLC as soon as possible.
Instruct the pilot to send an End Service (EOS) message before voice transition.
Terminate the voice backup circuit since it is no longer needed once CPDLC is restored.

items.instruction: For each situation, decide whether the controller must follow a prescribed procedure or may exercise best judgment.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations must the controller exercise best judgment rather than apply a specific published procedure?

choices.text: An aircraft requests an approach to an airport served by a published instrument approach procedure that the controller regularly uses.
An unusual mix of VFR and IFR traffic creates a conflict scenario that is not addressed by any provision in the order, and the controller must resolve it safely.
Two aircraft on the same airway at the same altitude are closing, and the order prescribes a specific separation minimum and vectoring procedure.
A controller receives a handoff using the standard coordination procedure described in the order and must decide whether to accept it.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-1, controllers must exercise best judgment when they encounter situations NOT covered by the order. When a published procedure applies, the controller follows it. Best judgment is the fallback for uncovered situations, not a discretionary alternative to existing procedures.

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether a Letter of Agreement (LOA) or a Waiver is the appropriate mechanism.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is a waiver the appropriate mechanism rather than an LOA?

choices.text: Two civilian TRACONs want to document joint handoff procedures that operate within existing order minima and require mutual coordination.
A civilian facility has an exceptional operational requirement that dictates a procedural deviation below the order's separation minima, and no military operations are involved.
An ARTCC and an adjacent approach control want to formalise standard coordination procedures that both facilities already follow in practice.
Two facilities need to document agreed-upon altitude assignments for arriving traffic that stay within the order's prescribed ranges.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs document jointly applied procedures or minima requiring multi-facility cooperation — but their minima may not fall below the order's except for military-authorised reduced separation between military aircraft. Per 1-1-11, waivers address exceptional or unusual requirements that dictate procedural deviations — this is the mechanism for a civilian facility needing a deviation below the order's minima.

items.instruction: Choose the best answer.
items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies all three channels that FAA Order JO 7110.65 specifies for obtaining additional ATO SMS information?

choices.text: SMS Toolbox, emailing AJI at 9-AJI-SMS@faa.gov, and contacting the service center Quality Control Group.
SMS Toolbox, facility quality assurance program, and FAA Hotline.
Emailing AJI, filing a UCR, and contacting the regional administrator.
MyFAA portal, eLMS training module, and the service center Quality Control Group.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, three channels are identified for obtaining additional SMS information: visiting the SMS Toolbox, emailing AJI at 9-AJI-SMS@faa.gov, and contacting the service center Quality Control Group. Other FAA channels serve different purposes and are not listed as SMS information channels in this paragraph.

items.instruction: Choose the correct notification target for each facility type.
items.question_text: When the order or changes have not been received at least 30 days before the effective date, to whom must an FAA facility report non-receipt, and to whom must a military facility report?

choices.text: An FAA facility notifies its service area office distribution officer; a military facility notifies its appropriate military headquarters.
Both FAA and military facilities notify AJV-P directly.
An FAA facility notifies its military headquarters; a military facility notifies its service area office distribution officer.
Both facility types notify the GovDelivery subscription administrator.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-7b, FAA facilities notify their service area office distribution officer. Per 1-1-7c, military facilities notify their appropriate military headquarters. The notification targets are distinct and based on facility type.

lookup_context: A state aircraft commander plans to operate under due regard
The pilot must meet two obligations and satisfy at least one of four operational conditions
items.task: Verify that a pilot's stated operational plan satisfies the due regard conditions by checking against the complete list in the order.
items.instruction: Use FAA Order JO 7110.65 paragraph 1-2-1o to verify the pilot's plan.
items.question_text: A state aircraft commander states the following plan: they will separate from all other air traffic, have arranged with a military unit for search and rescue monitoring, and will operate in Class G airspace. Has the commander satisfied the minimum requirements for due regard operations?

choices.text: Yes; all requirements are met — both obligations are satisfied plus at least one of the four operational conditions.
No; the commander must also be in communication with ATC even in Class G airspace.
No; the commander must satisfy at least two of the four operational conditions.
No; Class G airspace is not one of the acceptable operational conditions for due regard.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1o, due regard requires: obligation (a) separate from all other air traffic; obligation (b) assure SAR monitoring; and at least one condition from (c): VMC, or ATC surveillance + communications, or airborne radar, or Class G airspace. The commander's plan satisfies both obligations and meets condition (c)(4) — Class G airspace — so all minimum requirements are met.

lookup_context: Course definitions require classification into same, crossing, or opposite/reciprocal based on angular difference
FIG 1-2-1 depicts the angular divergence bands visually
items.task: Use the course-definition figure and the associated paragraph to classify a course relationship by angular difference.
items.instruction: Use FIG 1-2-1 and the course definitions in FAA Order JO 7110.65.
items.question_text: Using the course definitions and FIG 1-2-1, classify two courses whose protected airspaces intersect with an angular difference of 140 degrees.

choices.text: Opposite/reciprocal courses, because the angular difference is greater than 135 degrees and the protected airspaces intersect.
Crossing courses, because the angular difference is between 45 and 180 degrees.
Same courses, because the angular difference is less than 180 degrees.
The classification is undefined because protected airspaces intersect rather than overlap.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-2c, opposite/reciprocal courses are courses whose protected airspaces are coincident, overlap, or intersect AND whose angular difference is greater than 135 degrees through 180 degrees inclusive. At 140 degrees with intersecting protected airspaces, both conditions are satisfied — this is opposite/reciprocal.

items.instruction: What should the controller do?
items.situation: A controller is applying a separation procedure in FAA Order JO 7110.65 and sees a reference block citing another FAA order. The controller assumes the referenced order must be consulted and followed before any action can be taken.
items.para_context: A reference directs attention to additional or supporting information; it does not independently add a mandatory procedural step.
items.question_text: Does a reference block in FAA Order JO 7110.65 create an additional mandatory procedure that the controller must follow before acting?

choices.text: Yes; all references create binding obligations that must be satisfied before the referencing procedure can be applied.
Yes, but only if the reference cites another chapter within JO 7110.65 itself.
No; references direct attention to supporting information but do not, by themselves, create additional mandatory procedural steps.
No; references are decorative elements that have no operational function.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information. They alert the reader to where related or supporting material can be found, but they do not independently create new mandatory procedures. The controller should be aware of the referenced material but need not treat every reference as a prerequisite.

items.instruction: Evaluate whether the controller's action is consistent with the order's guidance on rephrasing prescribed phraseology.
items.question_text: A controller issues a landing clearance using the prescribed phraseology, then rephrases the message with different words after the pilot sounds uncertain. Is this permitted under FAA Order JO 7110.65?

choices.text: Yes; controllers may rephrase after first using the prescribed phraseology, provided they exercise good judgment and ensure the content is understood.
No; once prescribed phraseology is used, no further transmission on the same subject is permitted.
Yes; controllers may rephrase at any time and need not use prescribed phraseology first.
No; rephrasing is only permitted when the annotation EXAMPLE appears in the paragraph.

items.explanation: Per the note following 1-2-5g, controllers may, after first using the prescribed phraseology for a specific procedure, rephrase the message to ensure the content is understood. Good judgment must be exercised. The prescribed words come first; rephrasing is permitted as a follow-up comprehension aid.

items.instruction: Interpret the abbreviation in context.
items.situation: A controller reading a traffic management directive sees the abbreviation AAR used in the sentence: 'The AAR for ATL is currently 32.' A trainee claims AAR always means Adapted Arrival Route. The controller needs to determine the correct meaning.
items.para_context: When an abbreviation has more than one approved meaning, determine the intended meaning from its operational context.
items.question_text: In the sentence 'The AAR for ATL is currently 32,' what does AAR most likely mean, and how should the controller make this determination?

choices.text: Adapted Arrival Route, because AAR always defaults to the first definition listed in TBL 1-2-1.
Airport Arrival Rate, because the numeric value (32) in context of traffic management indicates a flow rate, not a route.
The controller must use both meanings simultaneously — 32 Adapted Arrival Routes at a rate.
Adverse Assumption Obstacle, because traffic management directives use safety abbreviations.

items.explanation: Per TBL 1-2-1, AAR has two listed meanings: Adapted Arrival Route and Airport Arrival Rate. Context determines which applies. A numeric value ('32') used with an airport identifier (ATL) in a traffic management context indicates a flow rate — Airport Arrival Rate.

items.instruction: For each person, determine whether FAA Order JO 7110.65 applies to them.
items.question_text: To which of the following persons does FAA Order JO 7110.65 apply?

choices.text: A military contractor using ATO-published separation standards while developing procedures for a DoD range operation.
A civilian software engineer developing a flight-planning tool who has never read or used any ATO directive.
An airline dispatcher who does not use FAA ATC directives in their work.
A private pilot who flies recreationally and does not use ATO directives.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, the order applies to all ATO personnel AND anyone using ATO directives. The military contractor using ATO separation standards falls under 'anyone using ATO directives.' The other persons listed do not use ATO directives and are not ATO personnel, so the order does not apply to them.

items.instruction: Determine whether the stated reason is a valid basis for deviating from JO 7110.65 procedures and minima.
items.situation: A controller believes a local course reversal would be safer than the published procedure. No emergency exists, this is not covered by an LOA or military document, and the U.S. is not providing service outside its territory.
items.question_text: Is the controller's independent safety judgment, without any of the three authorized conditions being present, a valid basis to deviate from 7110.65 procedures?

choices.text: No — deviation requires one of the three specific conditions: ICAO/special agreement conformance outside U.S. territory, LOA/directive/military document, or a declared emergency.
Yes — a controller may deviate whenever their safety judgment indicates a safer alternative exists.

items.explanation: Controllers must provide service in accordance with 7110.65 except when one of three specific conditions exists. A controller's independent belief that a different procedure is safer, without any of those conditions, does not authorize deviation.

items.instruction: Determine the correct priority handling for each aircraft, then select the one that must receive service first.
items.situation: Approach control is sequencing four aircraft: (A) a standard air carrier on a scheduled arrival, (B) a civil aircraft whose pilot has verbally stated 'MEDEVAC November One Two Three,' (C) an aircraft that has declared an emergency with engine failure, and (D) a HOSP flight whose pilot has verbally requested priority handling.
items.para_context: An aircraft in distress has right of way over all other air traffic. Properly identified MEDEVAC and requested HOSP priority follow below distress traffic.
items.question_text: Which aircraft must receive ATC service first?

choices.text: Aircraft C — the aircraft in distress has the right of way over all other air traffic.
Aircraft B — the MEDEVAC flight with proper verbal identification takes precedence.
Aircraft D — the HOSP flight with verbal request for priority handling.
Aircraft A — the standard air carrier that is closest to the airport.

items.explanation: An aircraft in distress has the right of way over all other air traffic. MEDEVAC flights with proper verbal identification receive priority handling but are subordinate to an aircraft in distress. HOSP flights also receive priority when verbally requested, but below distress. All others receive 'first come, first served' service.

items.instruction: Select the correct terrain/obstruction safety alert transmission for the given situation.
items.situation: Cessna N345J is at an altitude the controller believes places it in unsafe proximity to terrain. The aircraft is not yet on final approach, and the MVA in that area is 2,500 feet.
items.question_text: Which transmission uses correct phraseology?

choices.text: LOW ALTITUDE ALERT CESSNA THREE FOUR FIVE JULIETT, CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY. THE MVA IN YOUR AREA IS TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.
TERRAIN ALERT NOVEMBER THREE FOUR FIVE JULIETT, PULL UP IMMEDIATELY, MINIMUM VECTORING ALTITUDE TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.
SAFETY ALERT CESSNA THREE FOUR FIVE, YOU ARE BELOW THE MVA, CLIMB TO TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED.
LOW ALTITUDE ALERT CESSNA THREE FOUR FIVE JULIETT, MINIMUM SAFE ALTITUDE IS TWO THOUSAND FIVE HUNDRED, DESCEND AT YOUR DISCRETION.

items.explanation: The terrain/obstruction alert phraseology is 'LOW ALTITUDE ALERT (call sign), CHECK YOUR ALTITUDE IMMEDIATELY.' When not on final approach, add 'THE MVA IN YOUR AREA IS (altitude).'

items.instruction: Determine whether the controller's proposed action is correct.
items.situation: United 321 reports a TCAS RA and begins a climb. Delta 456 is 3 miles behind United 321 at the same altitude and has not reported any RA. The controller issues a descent instruction to Delta 456 to maintain separation.
items.para_context: Continue appropriate control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories to aircraft that have not reported responding to a TCAS resolution advisory.
items.question_text: Is the controller's action to issue the descent instruction to Delta 456 correct?

choices.text: Yes — the controller must continue providing control instructions to other aircraft not involved in the RA, since Delta 456 has not reported an RA.
No — the controller must not issue any control instructions to any aircraft in the vicinity until the RA is resolved.
No — the controller should assume Delta 456 will also receive an RA and no action is needed.
Yes — but only because Delta 456 is behind United 321, not in front of it.

items.explanation: Unless advised by other aircraft that they are also responding to a TCAS RA, the controller must not assume they are involved. The controller must continue to provide control instructions, safety alerts, and traffic advisories as appropriate.

items.instruction: Identify the non-RVSM aircraft that qualifies for excepted handling in RVSM airspace.
items.situation: A non-RVSM aircraft requests to operate in RVSM airspace. The operations supervisor/CIC must determine whether the aircraft meets any of the excepted categories.
items.question_text: Which aircraft qualifies as an excepted non-RVSM operation?

choices.text: A corporate jet with inoperative RVSM equipment on a routine business flight.
A charter aircraft whose operator prefers an RVSM altitude for fuel efficiency.
A MEDEVAC flight with the pilot verbally identifying as MEDEVAC.
A general aviation aircraft unable to verify its RVSM approval status.

items.explanation: MEDEVAC flights are one of the listed excepted non-RVSM categories. Other exceptions include DoD aircraft, specified DoD-certified NASA aircraft, manufacturer aircraft used for development or certification, and Foreign State aircraft. Equipment failure, operator preference, or uncertain approval status does not independently create an exception.

items.instruction: Place the following actions in the correct order that a controller must take before issuing a takeoff clearance when an LOA-covered vehicle is operating in the RSA near the active runway.
items.question_text: Arrange in correct sequence: (1) Issue takeoff clearance to the departing aircraft. (2) Verify the runway is free of all known ground vehicles, equipment, and personnel. (3) Authorize the LOA vehicle to proceed as requested in the RSA. (4) Confirm the vehicle remains at or behind the runway edge and has not entered the runway surface.
items.explanation: The correct sequence: first authorize the RSA operation using PROCEED AS REQUESTED (the vehicle may operate up to the runway edge under the LOA), then verify the runway itself is clear of all vehicles and personnel (even LOA vehicles cannot be on the runway surface before takeoff), confirm the vehicle remains at the edge, and only then issue takeoff clearance. The LOA authorizes RSA operations but does not permit vehicles on the runway surface when a departure is about to begin.

items.instruction: For each capability below, select whether it is permitted on an uncertified tower display workstation, a certified tower radar display, both, or neither.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) Visually locating aircraft relative to known geographic points. (B) Providing radar traffic advisories. (C) Determining an aircraft's exact location or spatial relationship to other aircraft. (D) Issuing suggested headings as an advisory aid to navigation.

choices.text: A: uncertified only; B: certified only; C: certified only; D: certified only
A: both; B: both; C: certified only; D: certified only
A: uncertified only; B: neither; C: both; D: both
A: both; B: certified only; C: both; D: both

items.explanation: (A) The permitted use of uncertified displays is to assist in visually locating aircraft or determining spatial relationship to known geographic points. Certified displays can also do this, but 'uncertified only' is the minimum correct classification. (B) Radar traffic advisories may only be provided using certified displays. (C) Determining exact location or spatial relationships is explicitly listed as a certified display capability. (D) Suggested headings are a certified display function. Uncertified displays cannot provide (B), (C), or (D).

items.instruction: A pilot is approaching Class D airspace. Place the following controller actions in the correct operational sequence when traffic conditions initially permit services but then become saturated.
