items.question_text: Arrange in correct sequence: (1) Controller determines workload now prevents immediate provision of services. (2) Pilot establishes initial radio contact with the tower. (3) Controller issues 'REMAIN OUTSIDE DELTA AIRSPACE AND STANDBY.' (4) Controller responds with aircraft call sign and 'standby.'
items.explanation: Correct sequence: (2) the pilot calls the tower first. (4) The controller responds with call sign + 'standby,' which establishes two-way communications and legally permits Class D entry. (1) The controller then realizes workload prevents immediate service. (3) At this point, the controller must issue the explicit 'REMAIN OUTSIDE' instruction because the earlier 'standby' alone permits entry but services cannot be provided.

items.instruction: For each airport condition, indicate whether the controller must include an additional specific detail beyond the basic condition description when issuing timely information.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) Volcanic ash on the airport surface. (B) Construction work adjacent to the movement area. (C) Braking conditions caused by ice. (D) Parked aircraft on the movement area.

choices.text: A: must state whether ash is wet or dry (if known); B, C, D: basic description suffices
A: basic description suffices; B: must state exact construction boundaries; C: must state ice depth; D: must state aircraft type
A and C: must include additional detail; B and D: basic description suffices
All four conditions require additional specific detail beyond the basic description

items.explanation: Only volcanic ash (item g) carries the unique requirement to state whether the ash is wet or dry if that information is known. Dry ash and wet ash create different operational hazards — dry ash may necessitate minimum use of reverse thrust, while wet ash may produce degraded braking. The other listed conditions carry no equivalent requirement.

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether the emergency-lighting rule requires action.
items.question_text: Classify: (A) A pilot declares an emergency with a MAYDAY call and reports engine failure. (B) A controller overhears a pilot report serious mechanical trouble but no emergency has been declared. (C) A vehicle reports a minor fluid leak on a taxiway with no aircraft affected. (D) An aircraft reports it will need to make an emergency landing in approximately 10 minutes due to low fuel.

choices.text: A, B, and D require action; C does not
Only A requires action because a formal MAYDAY was declared
A, B, C, and D all require action under the emergency-lighting rule
A and D require action; B and C do not

items.explanation: The trigger is controller awareness that an emergency 'has or will occur' — a formal MAYDAY is not required. (A) A declared emergency clearly triggers action. (B) The controller's awareness of serious trouble that could become an emergency triggers the 'will occur' prong. (D) An aircraft reporting a pending emergency landing directly triggers the 'will occur' prong. (C) A minor fluid leak is an airport condition issue, not an emergency.

items.instruction: For each ASDE safety logic alert scenario, select the correct controller response.
items.question_text: Classify the required response: (A) An arrival aircraft still airborne before the landing threshold activates a warning alert. (B) An arrival aircraft activates a warning alert to a taxiway. (C) Two arrival aircraft simultaneously activate an alert. (D) A known formation flight crosses the landing threshold and activates a warning alert, with all other factors acceptable.

choices.text: A: issue go-around; B: issue go-around; C: take appropriate action per assessed situation; D: may disregard
A: issue go-around; B: issue go-around; C: issue go-around to both; D: issue go-around
A: may disregard if runway appears clear; B: issue go-around; C: issue go-around; D: may disregard
A: issue go-around; B: advise pilot to check alignment; C: issue go-around to lower aircraft; D: may disregard

items.explanation: When a pre-threshold arrival activates a warning alert (A), issue go-around — the intent is that the aircraft does not land on that approach. Same for taxiway-targeted alerts (B). For two-aircraft alerts (C), the controller assesses and takes appropriate action per the situation. For known formation flights crossing the threshold (D), the rule provides an explicit exception allowing the alert to be disregarded if all other factors are acceptable.

items.instruction: Select the taxi clearance that includes all required elements and proper construction.
items.question_text: An aircraft at the general aviation ramp needs to taxi to Runway 27 for departure, with an intermediate hold short of Runway 18. Which clearance is properly constructed?

choices.text: "Taxi to Runway Two-Seven, hold short of Runway One-Eight on the way."
"Runway Two-Seven, taxi via Alpha, cross Runway One-Eight at Taxiway Alpha, hold short of Runway Two-Seven."
"Cleared to Runway Two-Seven, taxi at your discretion."
"Runway Two-Seven, taxi via Alpha, you'll need to hold somewhere before Runway One-Eight."

items.explanation: A proper taxi clearance must include the runway assignment, the specific taxi route in concise terms, any required crossing instructions with the crossing point, and the hold-short point. The correct choice includes the runway (Runway 27), route (via Alpha), crossing instruction with location, and hold position. The distractors omit route specificity, use vague language, or lack required elements.

items.instruction: Match each maneuver to the point at which the aircraft transitions from arriving to departing status.
items.question_text: Match: (A) Touch-and-go — (1) touchdown; (B) Stop-and-go — (2) complete stop; (C) Low approach — (3) crossing the landing threshold. Which pairing is correct?
items.explanation: Touch-and-go: transition at touchdown (the aircraft touches and immediately goes to full power). Stop-and-go: transition at complete stop (must come to a full stop before takeoff). Low approach: transition at crossing the landing threshold (never touches down). These transition points determine which separation rules apply.

items.instruction: Place the following gate-hold procedure elements in the correct sequence of events.
items.question_text: Arrange in correct operational sequence: (1) ATC advises aircraft of engine start time. (2) Gate-hold procedures are initiated. (3) ATC advises aircraft to start engines. (4) ATC announces gate-hold procedures are in effect and provides contact frequency. (5) Gate-hold procedures are terminated. (6) Pilot reports ready to taxi.
items.explanation: Correct sequence: (2) gate-hold procedures go into effect; (4) ATC announces the procedures and provides the contact frequency per the prescribed phraseology; (1) ATC provides the expected engine start time; (3) at the appropriate time, ATC advises to start engines; (6) pilot reports ready to taxi; (5) when gate-hold ends, ATC announces procedures no longer in effect.

items.instruction: For each situation, decide whether the altitude and distance limitation tables must be applied.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations must the controller consult the altitude and distance limitation tables before issuing the route?

choices.text: Specifying a route that lies entirely within uncontrolled airspace for its whole length.
Clearing an aircraft via an established Jet Route that is depicted on the high-altitude en route chart.
Specifying a direct route between two VORs where the straight-line path is not part of any established airway, and the entire route lies within controlled airspace.
Issuing a reroute that substitutes one published airway segment for another published airway segment.

items.explanation: The body of 4-1-1 states the tables apply 'when specifying a route other than an established airway or route' and only for 'any portion of the route which lies within controlled airspace.' Published airways and routes entirely in uncontrolled airspace are outside the trigger condition.

lookup_context: Three listed exceptions for not applying altitude and distance limitations
Each exception has distinct conditions that must all be met
items.task: Match each operational situation to the correct exception, or determine that no exception applies.
items.instruction: For each situation, identify which exception from 4-1-2 (if any) permits the altitude and distance limitations not to be applied.
items.question_text: Situation: A pilot requests a direct routing that exceeds the VOR service-volume limits. Radar monitoring is available and will be provided. The requested route does not use an MTR, and no operational-necessity approval has been obtained. Which exception, if any, applies?

choices.text: Exception (c): requested routing via an MTR.
Exception (b): operational necessity with office approval.
Exception (a): routing requested by the pilot with radar monitoring provided.
No exception applies; the routing must stay within the limitations.

items.explanation: Exception (a) requires two conditions: routing initiated by ATC or requested by the pilot, AND radar monitoring provided. Both are satisfied. Exception (b) requires office approval, which was not obtained. Exception (c) requires the routing to be via an MTR, which it is not.

items.source_label: 4-1-5 Fix Use, subparagraphs b3 and b4
lookup_context: Unpublished fix divergence angle rules in 4-1-5b4(a)-(d)
Holding operations at unpublished fixes
Distance-based divergence angle calculations
items.task: Apply the unpublished-fix divergence angle rules to determine whether the proposed fix layout is valid.
items.instruction: Determine whether the proposed unpublished fix satisfies the divergence angle requirements for the described operation.
items.question_text: An unpublished fix is 35 NM from the off-course NAVAID and 25 NM from the on-course NAVAID. The angle of divergence between the on-course and off-course radials is 32 degrees. Holding operations are planned at this fix. Is the fix valid?

choices.text: No. At 35 NM, the divergence angle must increase 1 degree per NM past 30 NM, requiring 35 degrees minimum.
Yes. 32 degrees exceeds the 30-degree minimum for NAVAIDs both within 30 NM, and subparagraph (a) does not override subparagraph (b).
Yes. The 45-degree holding floor only applies when the NAVAIDs are more than 30 NM from the fix.
No. Although the 30-degree minimum from subparagraph (b) is met at 32 degrees, subparagraph (a) requires at least 45 degrees when holding operations are involved.

items.explanation: When holding operations are involved, subparagraph 4-1-5b4(a) requires a minimum of 45 degrees divergence regardless of NAVAID proximity. While the 30-degree minimum from subparagraph (b) (for NAVAIDs both within 30 NM) is satisfied at 32 degrees, the holding-operations rule imposes a 45-degree floor that overrides the lower value.

items.instruction: For each transmission, determine whether the correct relay prefix was used.
items.question_text: Which of the following uses the correct relay prefix for the type of communication being relayed through a non-ATC facility?

choices.text: Relaying an advisory: 'A-T-C requests November One Two Three, weather deviation reported 20 miles ahead of you.'
Relaying a request for information: 'A-T-C clears November One Two Three, say altitude.'
Relaying a clearance: 'A-T-C clears November One Two Three to the Springfield Airport via direct.'
Relaying a clearance: 'A-T-C advises November One Two Three, cleared to the Springfield Airport via direct.'

items.explanation: The three prefixes match their communication type: 'A-T-C clears' for clearances, 'A-T-C advises' for information/advisories, and 'A-T-C requests' for requests for information. Using 'A-T-C clears' for a request or 'A-T-C requests' for a clearance mismatches the prefix to the communication type.

items.instruction: Identify which amendment method is being used in each transmission.
items.question_text: A controller issues: 'Cleared direct ODESA intersection, rest of route unchanged.' Which of the four amendment methods for a previously-issued clearance does this transmission exemplify?

choices.text: Stating which portion is amended and then stating the amendment (method 1).
Issuing a clearance direct to a point on the previously issued route (method 3).
Stating the amendment and then that the rest is unchanged (method 2).
Issuing the entire route by stating the amendment (method 4).

items.explanation: Method 3 is 'Issue a clearance direct to a point on the previously issued route.' The transmission 'Cleared direct ODESA intersection' satisfies this. Adding 'rest of route unchanged' does not convert it to method 2; the note clarifies that this phrase is optional for method 3 and may be used to clarify where the route resumes.

items.source_label: 4-2-8 IFR-VFR and VFR-IFR Flights, subparagraph d
lookup_context: Pop-up IFR procedures when aircraft cannot climb in VFR to minimum IFR altitude
Controller obligations: terrain/obstruction clearance question, then clearance or VFR instruction
Paragraph 10-2-7 and 10-2-9 as fallback provisions
items.task: Determine the correct controller action based on the pilot's response to the terrain and obstruction clearance question.
items.instruction: For the situation described, identify the correct controller response for a VFR aircraft requesting IFR clearance below the minimum IFR altitude.
items.question_text: A VFR aircraft at 2,500 feet requests an IFR clearance. The minimum IFR altitude in the area is 5,000 feet. The pilot states they are in and out of clouds and cannot maintain VFR to 5,000 feet. The controller asks if the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching 5,000 feet. The pilot responds 'unable.' What should the controller do?

choices.text: Issue the IFR clearance with a void time, allowing the pilot to find VFR conditions within 30 minutes.
Issue the IFR clearance anyway because the pilot has declared an inability to maintain VFR, which constitutes an emergency requiring immediate IFR handling.
Instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions. If appropriate, apply emergency assistance procedures or radar assistance procedures.
Vector the pilot to the nearest VFR airport because an IFR clearance cannot be issued without terrain and obstruction clearance assurance.

items.explanation: Per 4-2-8d3, if the pilot states they are unable to maintain terrain and obstruction clearance, the controller must instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions. Subparagraph d4 adds that the provisions of paragraph 10-2-7 (VFR Aircraft In Weather Difficulty) or 10-2-9 (Radar Assistance Techniques) may be applied if appropriate. The controller does not automatically issue the IFR clearance when the pilot cannot assure terrain/obstruction clearance.

items.instruction: Identify whether each cancellation instruction matches the airport type.
items.question_text: An arriving IFR aircraft is approaching an airport without an operating control tower and without an air/ground communications station. Which of the following is the correct cancellation instruction?

choices.text: 'Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with tower.'
'Report cancellation of IFR on 122.2.'
'Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.'
'Cancel IFR with flight service after landing.'

items.explanation: Per 4-2-10a2, for airports without an air/ground communications station, the phraseology is: '(Call sign) REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH FLIGHT SERVICE' or the variant naming the specific FSS or ATC facility. The first choice matches the prescribed wording. The third choice incorrectly substitutes 'tower' for 'flight service,' which is the alternate form for stations WITH an air/ground communications station.

lookup_context: Three categories of airports for specifying direction of takeoff/turn
Airports with ATC service: specify as necessary consistent with DPs
Non-towered airports in Class E surface area: coordinate before issuing turn/heading
All other airports: do not specify direction of takeoff or turn; heading applies only in controlled airspace
items.task: Determine whether the controller may specify a departure turn or heading at each type of airport.
items.instruction: For each airport type, determine whether the controller's action is correct when specifying departure turns or headings.
items.question_text: At a satellite airport without Airport Traffic Control Service but located within Class E surface area, the controller issues a departure clearance specifying: 'Fly heading one two zero after departure.' Before issuing this clearance, what must the controller do?

choices.text: Nothing additional — the controller may specify any departure heading at any airport.
Coordinate with the airport management or obtain the pilot's concurrence, because this is a non-towered airport within Class E surface area.
Issue the heading only as an advisory, not as a clearance instruction, because non-towered airports cannot receive mandatory departure instructions.
Confirm that the airport has a published Diverse Vector Area before issuing the heading.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-2c1(b), at locations without ATC service but within Class E surface area, the controller must coordinate with the airport management or obtain the pilot's concurrence before issuing a turn or heading in the clearance. This is a distinct requirement that falls between airports with ATC service (where headings may be specified as necessary) and all other airports (where takeoff direction/turn should not be specified).

items.instruction: Determine whether the conditions for an abbreviated departure clearance are met.
items.question_text: A flight's route was changed by the company operations officer after the flight plan was filed but before the pilot received the clearance. The pilot is unaware of the change. The controller, seeing the amended route in the system, offers an abbreviated departure clearance. Is this appropriate?

choices.text: Yes. Condition 1 only applies to changes made by the pilot, not by the company or operations officer.
Yes. The controller has the amended route and the pilot will accept any clearance the controller issues.
No. Condition 1 is not met because the route was changed before departure; the pilot has not accepted the amended route.
No. Abbreviated clearances are never appropriate when the route has been changed by anyone.

items.explanation: Per 4-3-3a1, the first condition is: 'The route of flight filed with ATC has not been changed by the pilot, company, operations officer, input operator, or in the stored flight plan program prior to departure.' The note explains that a pilot will not accept an abbreviated clearance if the route was changed before departure, and the company or operations officer is responsible for informing the pilot of changes. Since the company changed the route and the pilot is unaware, condition 1 is not satisfied.

lookup_context: Random RNAV route requirements for GNSS-equipped aircraft when not radar monitored
Points must be published NAVAIDs, waypoints, fixes, or airports recallable from navigation database
Points must be displayed on controller video maps or chart
items.task: Determine whether the proposed random RNAV route meets the requirements for non-radar operations.
items.instruction: Apply the random RNAV route requirements from paragraph 4-4-1.
items.question_text: A GNSS-equipped RNAV aircraft on a random RNAV route will not be radar monitored. The route is defined by four points: PUBLISHED_WP1, PUBLISHED_WP2, and two fixes that are in the aircraft's navigation database but are NOT displayed on the controller's video map or chart. Does this route meet the requirements?

choices.text: Yes. As long as the points are recallable from the aircraft's navigation database, the controller does not need to see them.
No. When not radar monitored, all points must be displayed on controller video maps or depicted on the controller chart at the position.
Yes. The requirement for controller visibility only applies to the terminal environment, not en route.
No. Random RNAV routes are not permitted at all when radar monitoring is unavailable.

items.explanation: Per 4-4-1c1(a), for non-radar-monitored GNSS RNAV aircraft on random routes, the points must BOTH be published NAVAIDs/waypoints/fixes/airports recallable from the aircraft's navigation database AND be displayed on controller video maps or depicted on the controller chart. The two non-displayed fixes fail the second requirement.

lookup_context: Seven methods for transitioning within or between route structures
Method g for RNAV aircraft: random RNAV route between ATS routes requires radar monitoring
items.task: Identify which transition method applies and whether any special requirements attach.
items.instruction: Match the operational situation to the correct route structure transition method.
items.question_text: An RNAV aircraft needs to transition from one Jet Route to another. The controller clears it via a random RNAV route to a fix on the new route. What additional requirement applies to this transition method?

choices.text: A SID or STAR must be assigned in conjunction with the random RNAV transition.
No additional requirement; RNAV aircraft always self-navigate on random routes.
The aircraft must report established on the new route before radar monitoring can be terminated.
Radar monitoring must be provided to the aircraft while transitioning via the random RNAV route.

items.explanation: Method g of 4-4-2 states: 'Clear RNAV aircraft between designated or established ATS routes via random RNAV routes to a NAVAID, waypoint, airport or fix on the new route. Provide radar monitoring to aircraft transitioning via random RNAV routes.' Radar monitoring is a specific requirement attached to this method.

items.source_label: 4-4-3 Degree-Distance Route Definition, subparagraphs a1-a3 and b
lookup_context: Degree-distance fix format: location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, distance in NM
Distance limits: below FL180 = 80 NM, FL180+ = 260 NM, celestial = 260 NM
Authorized special military operations in subparagraph b
items.task: Verify whether the flight plan's degree-distance route meets all definition and procedural requirements.
items.instruction: Evaluate the military flight plan against paragraph 4-4-3 requirements.
items.question_text: A military flight plan for a refueling anchor operation at 15,000 feet defines the route with degree-distance fixes spaced 75 miles apart using NAVAIDs within published service volume. The fixes use the format 'MKE 030025.' Is the route definition valid?

choices.text: No. Degree-distance fixes at any altitude must use celestial navigation NAVAIDs, not VORTAC facilities like MKE.
No. Refueling anchor operations are not listed among the authorized special military operations in subparagraph b.
Yes. At 15,000 feet (below FL 180), the 80-mile maximum applies and 75 miles is within that limit. The fix format is correct.
Yes, but only if the flight plan was filed at least 4 hours before the estimated time of departure.

items.explanation: The fix format 'MKE 030025' correctly includes location identifier, azimuth in degrees magnetic, and distance in NM. Below FL 180, the maximum fix spacing is 80 miles — 75 miles complies. Refueling operations are among the authorized special military operations listed in subparagraph b (air refueling). Flight plans must be filed at least 2 hours (not 4 hours) before ETD per subparagraph a5(b).

items.instruction: For each altitude band and aircraft pairing, identify the correct vertical separation minimum.
items.question_text: A non-RVSM aircraft is at FL300. Another aircraft at FL310 is also non-RVSM. What vertical separation minimum applies between them?

choices.text: 1,000 feet, because FL300 and FL310 are both at or below FL 410.
2,000 feet, because at or above FL 290 between non-RVSM aircraft and all other aircraft, 2,000 feet applies.
2,000 feet, because above FL 410 the standard minimum is always 2,000 feet.
1,000 feet, because these are both non-RVSM and the 2,000-foot rule only applies between RVSM and non-RVSM aircraft.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-1b: 'Apply 2,000 feet at or above FL 290 between non-RVSM aircraft and all other aircraft at or above FL 290.' The rule triggers on the non-RVSM status of one aircraft — the 'all other aircraft' includes both RVSM and non-RVSM aircraft. Since both are non-RVSM, this rule applies to both directions of the pairing, requiring 2,000 feet separation.

items.source_label: 4-5-6 Minimum En Route Altitudes, subparagraph a
lookup_context: Exceptions permitting clearance below MEA but not below MOCA
Three conditions: VOR/VORTAC/TACAN within 22 NM, radar with MVA/MIA + lost comm, or GNSS-equipped
Additional requirement: altitude at least 300 feet above floor of controlled airspace
items.task: Determine whether the aircraft qualifies for a below-MEA clearance under paragraph 4-5-6.
items.instruction: Apply the below-MEA exception rules to each situation.
items.question_text: An aircraft using VOR navigation is 30 NM from the VORTAC defining the route. The MEA is 5,000 feet and the MOCA is 3,500 feet. The controller wants to clear the aircraft at 4,000 feet, which is 400 feet above the floor of controlled airspace. Radar service is not being provided and the aircraft is not GNSS-equipped. Is the clearance at 4,000 feet valid?

choices.text: Yes. 4,000 feet is above the MOCA and at least 300 feet above the floor of controlled airspace, which satisfies the general below-MEA rule.
No. The aircraft is beyond 22 NM from the VORTAC, radar is not provided, and the aircraft is not GNSS-equipped — none of the three conditions is met.
Yes. The MOCA takes precedence over all other altitude restrictions when the aircraft is beyond 22 NM from the NAVAID.
No. The aircraft must be at or above the MEA (5,000 feet) regardless of other conditions because no exception applies at 30 NM from the NAVAID.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-6a, to clear below MEA but not below MOCA, one of three conditions must be met: (1) within 22 NM of VOR/VORTAC/TACAN, (2) radar with MVA/MIA and lost comm instructions, or (3) GNSS-equipped. At 30 NM, without radar, and without GNSS, none of the conditions is satisfied. The general rule (at or above MEA) applies.

items.instruction: Distinguish between a 'maintain' and a 'cruise' altitude instruction based on the operational context.
items.question_text: A controller clears an aircraft to an airport without a published instrument approach procedure, using an unpublished route. The controller wants to give the aircraft flexibility to descend at pilot discretion while ensuring obstacle clearance. Which altitude instruction is appropriate?

choices.text: Issue a cruise clearance with a specific crossing restriction to ensure terrain clearance until the FAF.
Issue 'maintain (altitude)' because cruise clearances are prohibited when using unpublished routes.
Issue a cruise clearance without a crossing restriction. When no published approach exists, a crossing altitude cannot satisfy the terrain-clearance requirement, so a cruise clearance is appropriate.
Issue 'maintain (altitude) until established on a published segment' because cruise clearances do not provide obstruction clearance.

items.explanation: Per 4-5-7a and Note 2: when issuing a cruise clearance to an airport without a published instrument approach, it is not possible to satisfy the crossing-altitude requirement. Under those conditions, a cruise clearance without a crossing restriction is authorized, and the pilot determines the minimum IFR altitude per 14 CFR 91.177.

items.instruction: Determine whether a frequency change is permitted under the SFA procedure.
items.question_text: A military single-piloted turbojet aircraft on an IFR flight plan is conducting an approach under SFA procedures per a letter of agreement. The aircraft is at 3,000 feet in IMC, on the intermediate approach segment. The controller needs the aircraft to change to the tower frequency for sequencing. Is this frequency change permitted?

choices.text: No. Under SFA procedures for military turbojet aircraft, frequency changes are prohibited at any point between initial contact during en route descent and landing or low approach completion.
Yes. Frequency changes to the tower for sequencing purposes are routine and are permitted under SFA procedures regardless of weather or flight conditions.
Yes. The SFA frequency-change restriction applies only during the final approach segment, so making a change on the intermediate approach segment is permitted.

No. Under SFA procedures, a frequency change is not permitted after the approach begins unless a listed exception applies (daylight VFR, pilot request, IFR cancellation, emergency, or visual approach).

items.explanation: SFA procedures prohibit frequency changes after the approach begins or after initial contact during an en route descent until landing or low approach is completed, except in five specific conditions: (a) daylight VFR, (b) pilot request, (c) IFR cancellation, (d) emergency, or (e) visual approach clearance. None of these exceptions applies to the situation described.

items.instruction: Match each operational scenario with the correct terminal radar separation minimum.
items.situation: Scenario 1: Single sensor ASR, aircraft is 25 miles from antenna. Scenario 2: Single sensor ASR, aircraft is 55 miles from antenna. Scenario 3: Single sensor MSSR, aircraft is 45 miles from antenna.
items.question_text: What separation minimum applies to each scenario?

choices.text: Scenario 1: 3 miles; Scenario 2: 3 miles; Scenario 3: 5 miles.
Scenario 1: 3 miles; Scenario 2: 5 miles; Scenario 3: 3 miles.
All scenarios: 3 miles because all are terminal operations.
Scenario 1: 5 miles; Scenario 2: 5 miles; Scenario 3: 5 miles.

items.explanation: Single sensor ASR: 3 miles when less than 40 miles from the antenna, 5 miles when 40 miles or more. Single sensor MSSR: 3 miles when less than 60 miles from the antenna. Scenario 1 is within 40 miles (3 miles), Scenario 2 is beyond 40 miles (5 miles), and Scenario 3 is within 60 miles for MSSR (3 miles).

items.instruction: For each scenario, determine the minimum angular difference required to apply passing or diverging separation in terminal operations.
items.situation: Scenario 1: A terminal controller is using a single site ARSR sensor and has FUSION mode with ISR displayed. Two aircraft are on crossing assigned radar vectors. Scenario 2: A terminal controller is using a single site ASR sensor in FUSION mode. Two aircraft are on crossing courses and one has crossed the projected course of the other.
items.question_text: What angular difference is required in each scenario?

choices.text: At least 15 degrees for both scenarios.
At least 45 degrees for Scenario 1; at least 15 degrees for Scenario 2.
At least 45 degrees for both scenarios.
At least 15 degrees for Scenario 1; at least 45 degrees for Scenario 2.

items.explanation: For single site ARSR or FUSION mode when ISR is displayed, the required minimum angular difference is at least 45 degrees. For single site ASR or FUSION mode (without ISR), it is at least 15 degrees. The difference reflects the refresh rate and precision of the sensor type — ARSR provides less frequent updates, therefore requiring more angular separation for the controller to confirm passing or diverging.

items.instruction: For each scenario, determine whether the controller may vector the aircraft and under what authority.
items.situation: Scenario 1: A pilot in Class G airspace requests vectors to avoid weather. Scenario 2: A controller in controlled airspace vectors an aircraft for noise abatement. Scenario 3: A controller wants to vector a VFR aircraft in Class G airspace without a pilot request for traffic sequencing.
items.question_text: In which scenarios may the controller legally vector the aircraft?

choices.text: All three scenarios — controllers may vector aircraft in any airspace class for any operational reason.
Scenarios 1 and 2 only — Class G requires a pilot request, and controlled airspace allows vectors for noise abatement.
Scenario 2 only — vectors are only permitted in controlled airspace for safety purposes.
Scenarios 1 and 3 — Class G vectoring is always at controller discretion regardless of pilot request.

items.explanation: In controlled airspace, aircraft may be vectored for separation, safety, noise abatement, operational advantage, confidence maneuver, or when a pilot requests. In Class G airspace, vectoring is only upon pilot request and as an additional service. Scenario 3 lacks a pilot request, making it unauthorized.

items.instruction: Select the complete set of arrival instructions required before the aircraft reaches the approach gate.
items.situation: A controller is preparing arrival instructions for an aircraft approaching the approach gate. The aircraft is being vectored to intercept the final approach course. The pilot has already stated the current ATIS code.
items.question_text: Which set of instructions satisfies the approach-gate requirement?

choices.text: Position relative to the final approach course, a descent clearance to the MVA, and the current ATIS in full.
Position relative to an appropriate final-course fix or reference, a vector to intercept final if required, and the approach clearance after the applicable establishment or altitude condition is met.
A vector to the airport, landing clearance, and a reminder to report the runway in sight.
The current ATIS code, distance from the runway threshold, and instructions to maintain present speed until the final approach fix.

items.explanation: The required elements are position relative to a fix on the final approach course, or to the NAVAID or airport when no fix is available; a vector to intercept final when required; and the approach clearance, except for a radar approach, after the aircraft is established or has been assigned an altitude to maintain until established. Repeating a received ATIS broadcast is not one of these three elements.

items.instruction: Match each runway centerline separation range with its required minimum diagonal radar separation during simultaneous dependent approaches.
items.situation: A terminal facility conducts simultaneous dependent approaches to parallel runways with the following configurations: Runways A-B: 3,000 ft apart; Runways C-D: 5,000 ft apart; Runways E-F: 8,500 ft apart.
items.question_text: Match the correct diagonal separation minimum to each runway pair:

choices.text: A-B: 1.5 miles; C-D: 2 miles; E-F: 3 miles.
A-B: 1 mile; C-D: 1.5 miles; E-F: 2 miles.
All pairs: 1.5 miles, since all are between 2,500 and 9,000 feet.
A-B: 1 mile; C-D: 1 mile; E-F: 1.5 miles.

items.explanation: The diagonal separation minima are tiered by runway centerline spacing: at least 2,500 ft but no more than 3,600 ft requires 1 mile; more than 3,600 ft but no more than 8,300 ft requires 1.5 miles; more than 8,300 ft but no more than 9,000 ft requires 2 miles.

items.instruction: Classify each approach scenario as SOIA, simultaneous dependent, or neither.
items.situation: Scenario A: Parallel runways 2,000 ft apart, one course offset by 2.8 degrees, FAA authorization in place. Scenario B: Parallel runways 4,500 ft apart, nil offset, dependent approaches in use. Scenario C: Parallel runways 500 ft apart with no offset authorization.
items.question_text: How should each scenario be classified?

choices.text: A: SOIA; B: simultaneous dependent; C: neither.
A: simultaneous dependent approaches; B: SOIA approaches; C: SOIA approaches.
All three scenarios qualify as SOIA approaches under the applicable criteria.
A: SOIA; B: SOIA; C: simultaneous dependent approaches.

items.explanation: SOIA requires parallel runways with centerlines at least 750 ft and less than 3,000 ft apart with one course offset by 2.5-3.0 degrees and FAA authorization. Scenario A meets all criteria. Scenario B has runways outside the SOIA range (4,500 ft) but within dependent approach range (2,500-9,000 ft). Scenario C's runways at 500 ft are below the SOIA minimum of 750 ft.

items.instruction: Identify the correct statement about when radar approaches may be given.
items.situation: A controller is reviewing the rules for providing radar approaches.
items.question_text: Which statement correctly describes when radar approaches may be given?

choices.text: A radar approach may only be given to IFR aircraft operating in instrument meteorological conditions.
A radar approach may be given to any aircraft upon request, offered to aircraft in distress regardless of weather, or offered to expedite traffic.
A radar approach may be given only when the ceiling is below 1,000 feet or visibility is less than 3 miles.
A radar approach may be given only when specifically requested by the pilot in writing before the flight.

items.explanation: The paragraph authorizes radar approaches to any aircraft upon request, and they may be offered to aircraft in distress regardless of weather conditions or to expedite traffic. There is no weather minimum restricting when a controller may offer one, though the pilot remains responsible for determining whether the approach and landing are authorized under existing weather minima.

items.instruction: For each scenario, determine which E-MSAW suppression function is appropriate: specific alert suppression or indefinite alert suppression.
items.situation: Scenario 1: A military fighter on a low-level training route repeatedly triggers E-MSAW alerts across multiple MIA areas within the ARTCC because the route altitudes are below established minimum IFR altitudes by operational necessity. Scenario 2: A single air carrier aircraft briefly descends below the MIA due to turbulence and triggers one E-MSAW alert. The aircraft is otherwise flying at normal IFR altitudes.
items.question_text: Which suppression function applies to each scenario?

choices.text: Specific alert suppression for both scenarios.
Indefinite alert suppression for Scenario 1; specific alert suppression for Scenario 2.
Indefinite alert suppression for both scenarios.
Specific alert suppression for Scenario 1; indefinite alert suppression for Scenario 2.

items.explanation: Indefinite alert suppression must be used exclusively for aircraft known to fly at altitudes that will activate the alert feature of one or more MIA areas within an ARTCC — the military low-level route fits this pattern. The specific alert suppression message is used to inhibit E-MSAW alerting on a single flight for a specific alert — the single turbulence-triggered alert fits this pattern. The two functions serve different operational purposes and are not interchangeable.

items.instruction: Which communications scenario permits use of mileage-based separation?
items.question_text: In which of the following situations may the controller apply mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima?

choices.text: The pilot communicates through a relay aircraft on the same frequency.
The pilot and controller are in direct VHF voice communication.
The pilot sends position reports via datalink while a third party relays voice.
The pilot communicates through company dispatch which relays to ATC.

items.explanation: The rule requires direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. Direct communication means the controller and pilot speak to each other without an intermediary. Relayed communications, regardless of the relay method, do not satisfy this requirement.

items.instruction: For each missed position report, determine whether the 5-minute action rule applies.
items.question_text: Three aircraft miss position reports. Aircraft A misses a report at a fix with no known traffic within 50 miles. Aircraft B misses a report at a fix where another IFR aircraft is on a converging course 8 minutes away. Aircraft C misses a report at a fix where the controller is spacing it behind another aircraft on the same airway. Which missed reports trigger the mandatory action requirement?

choices.text: Aircraft A and C only.
Aircraft B and C only.
All three aircraft trigger the requirement.
Aircraft A only.

items.explanation: The rule applies to position reports affecting separation. Aircraft B's report affects separation with the converging aircraft. Aircraft C's report affects spacing. Aircraft A's report does not affect separation and therefore does not trigger the rule.

items.instruction: Identify which situation violates the duplicate position report rule.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is the duplicate position report rule being violated?

choices.text: The center asks a pilot to report a fix, and the tower independently asks the same pilot to report the same fix — neither facility coordinates with the other.
The tower asks a pilot to report a fix that the center also requested, but the facilities coordinated the request beforehand.
The center asks a pilot to report five different fixes along the route to provide periodic position updates.
The tower asks a pilot to report the same fix to two different tower controllers working different positions.

items.explanation: The rule prohibits requiring an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility. The first option describes two different facilities independently requiring the same report — the prohibited conduct. Multiple reports to controllers within the same facility is not a violation of this rule.

items.instruction: For each category pair, identify which requires 3 minutes of wake turbulence separation in terminal adjacent-airport operations.
items.question_text: In terminal adjacent-airport operations, which of the following category pairs requires 3 minutes rather than 2 minutes of wake turbulence separation?

choices.text: Category D behind Category B.
Category H behind Category C.
Category F behind Category A.
Category I behind Category E.

items.explanation: Subparagraph a1 specifies 3 minutes for any Category B through I behind Category A. All other terminal adjacent-airport pairs use 2 minutes: Category B-I behind B or D (a2), Category E-I behind C (a3), and Category I behind E (a4).

items.instruction: Compare en route arrival minima for same-runway versus parallel/crossing-runway operations for a small aircraft behind a B757.
items.question_text: In en route arrival operations, a small aircraft behind a B757 requires 3 minutes to the same runway but only 2 minutes to a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet. What operational factor explains the difference?

choices.text: Same-runway arrivals fly through the same wake path for longer, while parallel-runway flight paths diverge, reducing wake exposure time.
The B757 produces more wake on the same runway than when landing on a parallel runway.
Parallel-runway arrivals always use radar separation as the primary method.
The same-runway minimum includes an extra minute for coordination between approach and tower.

items.explanation: When aircraft land on the same runway, the trailing aircraft flies directly through the leading aircraft's wake. On parallel runways, the flight paths diverge, reducing the duration of wake exposure. This reduced risk permits a smaller minimum for the parallel/crossing case, but only for the B757 — super and heavy lead aircraft have the same minima in both cases.

items.instruction: Arrange the three diverging-course departure minima in order from smallest to largest separation value.
items.question_text: Which list correctly orders the three diverging-course departure minima from the smallest separation value to the largest?

choices.text: 1 minute immediately after takeoff; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD.
3 miles within 13 miles DME/ATD; 2 minutes within 5 minutes after takeoff; 1 minute immediately after takeoff.
2 minutes within 5 minutes; 3 minutes immediately; 5 miles within 13 miles.
1 minute immediately; 3 minutes within 5 minutes; 5 miles within 13 miles.

items.explanation: The three-tier progression is: immediately after takeoff → 1 minute; within 5 minutes → 2 minutes; within 13 miles DME/ATD → 3 miles. Each tier uses a larger value because divergence occurs later, increasing the period during which aircraft are on potentially conflicting paths.

items.instruction: Determine whether the 5-mile DME/ATD minimum may be applied in each mixed-equipment scenario. The DME aircraft must be either at or below 10,000 feet OR outside 10 miles from the DME NAVAID.
items.question_text: A controller has three aircraft pairs on the same course. Pair 1: DME aircraft at 9,000 feet and 5 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. Pair 2: DME aircraft at 15,000 feet and 15 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. Pair 3: DME aircraft at 12,000 feet and 8 miles from the NAVAID, following an ATD aircraft. For which pairs is the 5-mile same-course minimum authorized?

choices.text: Pairs 1 and 2 only.
All three pairs.
Pair 3 only.
Pair 1 only.

items.explanation: For mixed DME/ATD use, the DME aircraft qualifies if EITHER at/below 10,000 feet OR outside 10 miles. Pair 1: 9,000 feet satisfies the altitude prong. Pair 2: 15 miles satisfies the distance prong. Pair 3: 12,000 feet (above 10,000) AND 8 miles (inside 10) fails both prongs.

items.instruction: Compare the fix-based and time-based departure/arrival separation methods.
items.question_text: When the takeoff direction differs by at least 45 degrees from the reciprocal of the final approach course, the fix-based method has the departure take off before the arrival leaves a fix at least 4 miles from the airport. The time-based method has the departure take off 3 minutes before the arrival is estimated at the airport. Why is the fix-based method preferred when an appropriate fix exists?

choices.text: The fix-based method uses a precise geographic reference rather than an estimate, reducing position uncertainty.
The fix-based method always requires less time between the departure and arrival.
The time-based method requires DME equipment that is not available at all airports.
The fix-based method eliminates the need for the 45-degree divergence requirement.

items.explanation: The fix-based method uses an actual geographic reference — the aircraft's position relative to a known fix — which is more precise than an estimated arrival time. Subparagraph c makes the time-based method the fallback, reinforcing that fix-based is the preferred method.

items.instruction: Construct the correct phraseology for a fix-crossing deadline.
items.question_text: A controller needs an aircraft to cross FIXXX no later than 1430Z. Which phraseology correctly conveys this restriction?

choices.text: CROSS FIXXX AT OR BEFORE 1430Z.
CROSS FIXXX NO LATER THAN 1430Z.
CROSS FIXXX AT OR AFTER 1430Z.
PROCEED TO FIXXX, ARRIVE BY 1430Z.

items.explanation: The published phraseology is 'CROSS (fix) AT OR BEFORE (time).' This is the prescribed wording for a fix-crossing time restriction. 'AT OR AFTER' serves the opposite purpose (earliest crossing time). Nonstandard variations are not the authorized phraseology.

items.instruction: Match each speed advantage to the correct longitudinal separation minimum for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft.
items.question_text: Match each leading-aircraft speed advantage to the correct longitudinal separation minimum for DME/ATD-equipped aircraft: (1) 50 knots faster; (2) 30 knots faster; (3) 15 knots faster.

choices.text: (1) 5 miles; (2) 10 miles; (3) 20 miles.
(1) 10 miles; (2) 20 miles; (3) 30 miles.
(1) 5 miles; (2) 5 miles; (3) 10 miles.
(1) 3 minutes; (2) 5 minutes; (3) 10 minutes.

items.explanation: The rule provides three tiers: ≥44 knots → 5 miles (a), ≥22 knots → 10 miles (b), and neither → 20 miles fallback (d). 50 knots qualifies for the 5-mile tier, 30 knots for the 10-mile tier (≥22 but <44), and 15 knots qualifies for neither, requiring the 20-mile fallback.

items.instruction: Determine which opposite-course vertical separation discontinuation method is satisfied in each situation.
items.question_text: For which of the following situations may vertical separation on opposite courses be discontinued?

choices.text: Both aircraft report passing the same VOR and are 2 minutes apart.
Two RNAV aircraft on a route 6 miles wide report passing the same position and are 8 miles apart.
Both aircraft report passing NAVAIDs indicating they have passed each other, but only 5 minutes have elapsed since the estimated passing time.
Two RNAV aircraft on a route 12 miles wide report passing the same position and are 15 miles apart.

items.explanation: The second option satisfies subparagraph c: RNAV aircraft on a route ≤8 miles wide need 8 miles. The first fails subparagraph b because 2 min < 3 min. The third fails subparagraph a because the 10-minute window hasn't closed. The fourth fails subparagraph c: >8 miles wide requires 18 miles, and 15 < 18.

items.instruction: The controller issued pilot-maintained longitudinal separation authorization. Evaluate the pilot readback.
items.clearance: MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES OR TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM N123AB.
items.question_text: Controller: "MAINTAIN AT LEAST ONE ZERO MINUTES OR TWO ZERO MILES SEPARATION FROM N123AB." Which pilot response correctly interprets the instruction?

choices.text: "Wilco, we will maintain at least 10 minutes or 20 miles from N123AB."
"Roger, maintaining 10 miles from N123AB."
"Wilco, we will maintain at least 5 minutes or 10 miles separation from N123AB."
"Roger, we'll maintain at least 20 minutes separation from N123AB."

items.explanation: The phraseology 'ONE ZERO MINUTES/TWO ZERO MILES' means 10 minutes or 20 miles. The pilot correctly reads back both options. The 5-minute/10-mile values are controller-applied longitudinal separation under 6-4-2b, not pilot-maintained separation.

lookup_context: Non-DME diverging-radials table
Same VOR radial divergence
Actual divergence angle falls between two table rows
items.task: Apply the table rule for an angle between listed values.
items.instruction: Use the diverging-radials table interpolation rule.
items.question_text: Two aircraft are on radials of the same VOR diverging by 22 degrees. The non-DME divergence table lists values for 20 degrees and 25 degrees. What distance applies under the table rule?

choices.text: Use the 20-degree row, requiring 12 NM.
Use the 25-degree row, requiring 10 NM.
Average the adjacent values and use 11 NM.
Treat the aircraft as separated once the radials diverge by 15 degrees.

items.explanation: When the divergence angle falls between two table values, use the lesser divergence value to obtain the distance. With 22 degrees between 20 and 25 degrees, use the 20-degree row and the larger 12 NM distance.

items.instruction: Construct the correct DME arc assignment phraseology.
items.question_text: A controller assigns an aircraft to fly a 25-mile DME arc south of the ABC VOR. Which phraseology is correct?

choices.text: VIA 25 MILE ARC SOUTH OF ABC VOR.
FLY THE 25 DME ARC SOUTH OF ABC.
PROCEED VIA 25 MILE ARC SOUTH OF ABC.
MAINTAIN 25 MILE DME ARC SOUTH OF ABC.

items.explanation: The published phraseology is 'VIA (number of miles) MILE ARC (direction) OF (name of DME NAVAID).' Deviations from this format are not the authorized phraseology.

items.instruction: Compare overflown-side protection for different course change angles.
items.question_text: At FL200, the overflown-side protection is 14 miles for a 75-degree turn but 28 miles for a 120-degree turn. Why does the protection double for the larger turn angle?

choices.text: Larger course changes sweep through more airspace on the overflown side before the aircraft establishes on the new course.
The 91-180 degree range always requires exactly double the protection because aircraft accelerate during sharper turns.
The larger value includes a buffer for crossing traffic that only exists during turns greater than 90 degrees.
Protection values are based on turn radius, and a 120-degree turn has exactly double the radius of a 75-degree turn.

items.explanation: Subparagraphs b and c establish two angle ranges (16-90° and 91-180°) with different protection values. A larger turn sweeps through a larger area before the aircraft establishes on the new course. At FL180-230, the values are 14 miles (16-90°) and 28 miles (91-180°).

items.instruction: For each situation, determine whether the basic rule or the stricter altitude reassignment rule applies.
items.question_text: In which situation must the controller wait until the previous aircraft reports at or passing through another altitude separated by the appropriate minimum before reassigning the altitude?

choices.text: An aircraft reports leaving FL240 in level flight and the controller wants to assign FL240 to another aircraft.
Severe turbulence has been reported and the controller wants to assign FL240 to another aircraft.
Two aircraft are in level flight vertically separated by 2,000 feet.
An aircraft reports reaching its newly assigned altitude.

items.explanation: The stricter rule applies when any exception is triggered: severe turbulence, military aerial refueling, pilot's-discretion clearance, or cruise clearance. Severe turbulence is one of those exceptions; the basic rule (report leaving) does not apply.

items.instruction: Compare en route and terminal wake turbulence intervals for timed approaches.
items.question_text: In timed approaches, a Category H aircraft behind a Category A aircraft in terminal operations requires 4 minutes or 8 miles. Which en route weight-class pair requires the same interval?

choices.text: Small behind super — 4 minutes or 8 miles.
Small behind heavy — 3 minutes or 6 miles.
Heavy behind super — 3 minutes or 6 miles.
Large behind super — 3 minutes or 7 miles.

items.explanation: Both Category H/I behind A (terminal) and small behind super (en route) represent maximum wake vulnerability — the smallest aircraft behind the largest wake producer — and both require 4 minutes or 8 miles.

items.instruction: An aircraft on a timed approach misses. Identify the correct sequence of controller actions.
items.question_text: An aircraft in a timed-approach sequence misses the approach. The next aircraft has an alternative missed approach procedure. What is the correct sequence of controller actions?

choices.text: Allow the next aircraft to continue; retain radar control or hold remaining aircraft until traffic conditions permit issuing approach clearances.
Hold all aircraft at assigned altitudes, cancel all approach clearances, and restart the sequence.
Clear the next aircraft for a straight-in approach and release remaining aircraft to the tower immediately.
Allow the next aircraft to continue and clear all remaining aircraft for their approaches without delay.

items.explanation: Subparagraph b instructs: (1) allow the next aircraft to continue if it has an alternative missed approach procedure, and (2) retain radar control or hold remaining aircraft until traffic conditions permit. The controller must not release remaining aircraft until the traffic situation resolves.

lookup_context: Tower-applied visual separation has six conditions under 7-2-1a1; pilot-applied visual separation has different procedures under 7-2-1a2.
items.task: Determine under which subparagraph — tower-applied (7-2-1a1) or pilot-applied (7-2-1a2) — the controller in each scenario should operate, and identify the controlling condition.
items.instruction: For each of the following operational situations, identify whether the controller should apply tower-applied visual separation (7-2-1a1) or pilot-applied visual separation (7-2-1a2), and cite the specific condition that controls.
items.question_text: A terminal controller observes two aircraft on the tower radar display and wants to apply visual separation. The tower visually observes both aircraft. One pilot has been told about the other aircraft. Under which subparagraph does this fall, and what is the controlling requirement?

choices.text: Tower-applied (7-2-1a1): the tower visually observes the aircraft, issues timely traffic advisories, and provides visual separation — this matches (b).
Pilot-applied (7-2-1a2): the pilot must see the other aircraft and be instructed to maintain visual separation.
Neither; visual separation requires both the tower and the pilot to have the aircraft in sight simultaneously.
Tower-applied (7-2-1a1) only if wake turbulence separation is also required.

items.explanation: In this situation, the tower is applying visual separation by visually observing both aircraft and issuing traffic advisories. This is tower-applied visual separation under 7-2-1a1(b). The controller must also maintain communication with at least one aircraft (a) and issue control instructions as necessary (c). Pilot-applied visual separation under 7-2-1a2 requires the pilot to report the other aircraft in sight and accept responsibility for maintaining visual separation.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-3-1b lists five conditions that must be met before an aircraft may be cleared to climb through clouds to VFR-on-top.
items.task: Determine which conditions from 7-3-1b are met in the scenario and whether the controller may issue the clearance.
items.instruction: Review the five conditions in 7-3-1b. For the scenario below, identify which conditions are satisfied and whether the clearance may be issued.
items.question_text: A pilot on an IFR flight plan at 6,000 feet requests a climb through an overcast layer to reach VFR-on-top conditions. The controller has a tops report of 9,000 feet. The controller issues a clearance to climb to 9,000 feet and report reaching VFR-on-top, with an alternative to maintain 9,000 feet and advise if not on top. Upon reaching 9,000 feet, the pilot reports VFR-on-top. Is the controller's handling correct?

choices.text: Yes — all five conditions are met: pilot requested, tops reported, alternative clearance issued, separation ensured, and aircraft recleared VFR-on-top upon reaching.
No — the controller failed to issue the correct phraseology for the alternative clearance.
No — VFR-on-top through a cloud layer requires the pilot to be IFR-rated and current, which was not verified.
Yes — but only the first three conditions apply when tops are reported; conditions 4 and 5 are not required.

items.explanation: Per 7-3-1b, the five conditions are: (1) pilot requests the clearance, (2) pilot is informed of reported tops height, (3) pilot is informed if no tops report is available, (4) when necessary, separation is ensured by issuing an alternative clearance, and (5) aircraft is recleared VFR-on-top upon reaching. All five conditions are satisfied in this scenario.

items.instruction: Choose the response that correctly describes the controller's obligation.
items.question_text: Compare the controller's responsibility during a go-around from a visual approach at an airport with an operating control tower versus at an airport without an operating control tower. Which statement is correct?

choices.text: At both types of airports, the pilot assumes full responsibility and the controller has no further separation obligations.

At a towered airport, the aircraft may enter the traffic pattern or proceed as instructed, with ATC providing separation. At a non-towered airport, the pilot lands as soon as possible or contacts ATC.
At a towered airport, the pilot must always execute the published missed approach procedure. At a non-towered airport, the pilot must contact ATC for an IFR clearance.
The controller must issue a go-around instruction with a specific altitude in both cases.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-1a (towered airport), the aircraft may be directed to enter the traffic pattern with no altitude assignment required, or proceed as instructed; ATC must provide approved or visual separation. Per 7-4-1b (non-towered airport), the pilot is expected to complete a landing as soon as possible or contact ATC; ATC must maintain approved separation. A visual approach has no missed approach segment — the go-around is handled as any go-around.

items.instruction: Identify the correct statement about the requirement.
items.question_text: A controller is conducting simultaneous visual approaches to parallel runways separated by 2,000 feet. An aircraft on approach to Runway 27L reports the preceding aircraft on approach to Runway 27R in sight. What must the controller do?

choices.text: Issue the visual approach clearance to Runway 27L without further action, since the pilot has reported the parallel traffic in sight.

Advise the aircraft to maintain visual separation from the preceding aircraft on the adjacent parallel runway, because for runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, sighting the preceding aircraft triggers the visual separation instruction.
Cancel the visual approach to Runway 27L because simultaneous visual approaches are never authorized to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet.
Issue the clearance but vector the aircraft 30 degrees from the extended centerline to reduce overshoot potential.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-4c1, for parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet, unless approved separation is provided, an aircraft must report sighting a preceding aircraft making an approach to the adjacent parallel runway. When the pilot reports the other aircraft in sight and visual separation is applied, the controller must advise the succeeding aircraft to maintain visual separation. The 30-degree intercept angle mentioned in the notes relates to reducing overshoot potential but is not the controlling requirement here.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-4-7 lists five conditions (a through e) that must be met before clearing an aircraft for a contact approach.
items.task: Identify which condition from the source text is not met in each scenario, or confirm that all conditions are met.
items.instruction: Review the conditions for a contact approach in 7-4-7a through 7-4-7e. Determine whether the controller may issue the clearance.
items.question_text: An IFR aircraft requests a contact approach to a non-towered airport. The reported ground visibility is 3 miles. The airport has a published and functioning VOR approach. The controller applies approved separation from other IFR traffic by clearing the aircraft at or below 3,000 feet (1,000 feet below the lowest IFR traffic at 4,000 feet) but not below the MSA of 2,200 feet. May the contact approach be issued?

choices.text: Yes — all conditions are met: pilot requested, visibility at least 1 mile, functioning instrument approach published, approved separation applied with correct vertical separation, and no indication weather makes it impracticable.
No — contact approaches may only be issued at airports with operating control towers.
No — the controller initiated the contact approach, which is prohibited; only the pilot may request it. Since the pilot did request it, this is met, but the visibility must be at least 5 miles.
No — the vertical separation minimum for a contact approach is 2,000 feet below any IFR traffic, not 1,000 feet.

items.explanation: Per 7-4-7, all five conditions are met: (a) pilot requested it, (b) ground visibility at least 1 statute mile, (c) published standard or special instrument approach is functioning, (d) approved separation applied — vertical separation uses 'at or below an altitude which is at least 1,000 feet below any IFR traffic but not below the MSA prescribed in 14 CFR 91.119,' and (e) no indication weather makes it impracticable.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-5-1a lists six numbered conditions under which SVFR operations are authorized.
items.task: For each scenario, determine whether all required SVFR authorization conditions are met. Identify any missing condition by number and content.
items.instruction: A pilot requests an SVFR clearance. Using the six conditions in 7-5-1a, determine whether the controller may authorize SVFR.
items.question_text: A pilot of a fixed-wing aircraft requests an SVFR clearance to depart an airport within Class D surface area. The airport weather reports a 600-foot overcast ceiling and 2 miles visibility. The pilot states that VFR cannot be maintained. The airport is not listed in 14 CFR part 91, Appendix D. May the controller issue the SVFR clearance?

choices.text: Yes — all applicable conditions are met: not prohibited by Appendix D, within Class D surface area below 10,000 feet MSL, pilot requested, based on reported weather, and weather is below basic VFR minima.
No — SVFR is never authorized for fixed-wing aircraft.
No — the ceiling must be at least 1,000 feet before SVFR can be authorized.

Yes — but the controller must also apply the provisions of 7-5-1b which prohibits VFR operations beneath a broken or overcast ceiling when the reported ceiling is less than 1,000 feet; an SVFR clearance satisfies this requirement.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-1a, all six conditions are met: (1) the location is not prohibited by 14 CFR part 91 Appendix D, (2) N/A for fixed-wing, (3) within lateral boundaries of Class D surface area below 10,000 feet MSL, (4) pilot requested, (5) based on weather reported at the airport, and (6) N/A — weather IS reported. Per 7-5-1b, VFR operations beneath a broken or overcast ceiling when the reported ceiling is less than 1,000 feet require an SVFR clearance — which is exactly what the pilot is requesting. This condition actually triggers the need for SVFR rather than prohibiting it.

items.instruction: Identify the correct statement about the separation requirement.
items.question_text: Compare the SVFR separation requirements for a fixed-wing aircraft versus a helicopter. Which correctly states the difference?

choices.text: SVFR fixed-wing aircraft may be separated using radar, visual, or nonradar separation, while helicopters may only use visual separation.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft require nonradar or visual separation only; SVFR helicopters may use nonradar, visual, or IFR radar separation.
Both SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and helicopters may use any separation method including radar separation.
SVFR fixed-wing aircraft require radar separation only; helicopters may use visual separation only.

items.explanation: Per 7-5-3a, only nonradar or visual separation may be applied between SVFR fixed-wing aircraft and IFR aircraft. Radar separation is not authorized due to the cloud clearance and 1-mile flight visibility requirements. Per 7-5-3b, SVFR helicopters may use nonradar, visual, or IFR radar separation. This is a critical operational distinction — controllers must know which separation methods are available based on the aircraft category operating under SVFR.

items.instruction: Choose the statement that correctly identifies the operational difference.
items.question_text: A controller is handling a fixed-wing SVFR aircraft in low visibility at two different airports. At Airport A, the ground visibility is officially reported as 3/4 mile. At Airport B, no weather is reported and the pilot advises that flight visibility is 3/4 mile. Both aircraft are inside their respective surface areas. What is the key operational trigger that distinguishes how the controller handles each aircraft?

choices.text: The distinction is based on aircraft type: helicopters receive different treatment than fixed-wing aircraft.
An official measurement triggers an advisory citing the reported value; a pilot's estimate triggers a request for intentions without an official measurement.
The distinction is based on arrival versus departure: arriving aircraft receive different treatment than departing aircraft.
There is no operational difference; the controller handles both aircraft identically.

items.explanation: When ground visibility is officially reported below 1 mile at the airport, the controller uses the specific phraseology '(Airport) VISIBILITY LESS THAN 1 MILE. ADVISE INTENTIONS' and informs the pilot of the reported value. When weather is not reported and the pilot advises flight visibility is below 1 mile, the controller requests the pilot's intentions without citing an official visibility value. The key distinction is the source and nature of the visibility information — official ground report versus pilot-reported flight visibility.

items.instruction: Select the choice that lists all four required basic radar services for VFR aircraft in the correct order as specified.
items.question_text: What are the four components of basic radar services for VFR aircraft that a terminal controller must provide?

choices.text: Safety alerts, traffic advisories, limited radar vectoring when requested, and sequencing where procedures exist.
Traffic advisories, safety alerts, approach clearances, and landing sequence assignments.
Radar identification, speed adjustments, vectoring for approaches, and traffic advisories.
Sequencing, separation from IFR aircraft, altitude assignments, and traffic advisories.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-1a, basic radar services for VFR aircraft must include: (1) safety alerts, (2) traffic advisories, (3) limited radar vectoring when requested by the pilot, and (4) sequencing at locations where procedures have been established and/or when covered by a LOA. These are the four mandatory components; approach clearances, landing sequence assignments, and IFR separation are not part of basic radar services for VFR aircraft.

lookup_context: Paragraph 7-6-7a requires radar contact before instructing a VFR aircraft to enter the traffic pattern or vectoring to the approach sequence. Paragraph 7-6-7b provides for holding when no position is available.
items.task: Identify the required controller action based on the conditions described.
items.instruction: Use the procedures in 7-6-7 to determine the correct controller action.
items.question_text: A VFR aircraft calls approach control for sequencing to the primary airport. The approach sequence for the active runway is full. The controller has radar contact with the aircraft. What should the controller do?

choices.text: Vector the aircraft into the approach sequence anyway, fitting it between two IFR arrivals.
Direct the aircraft to a point near the airport to hold until a position becomes available in the approach sequence.
Instruct the aircraft to contact tower directly and coordinate with approach control for landing sequence.
Terminate radar service and have the aircraft proceed VFR to the airport.

items.explanation: Per 7-6-7b, when a position is not available in the approach sequence for the runway in use, the controller directs the VFR aircraft to a point near the airport to hold. The aircraft may be vectored to another runway after coordination with the tower. This is the appropriate action — not forcing the aircraft into a full sequence or terminating service prematurely.

items.instruction: Choose the statement that correctly describes the controller's obligation.
items.question_text: A controller is holding two VFR aircraft in the TRSA — one inside the TRSA boundary and one outside the TRSA boundary. Does the controller have the same obligation to issue an expected further clearance time for both aircraft?

choices.text: The controller must inform both aircraft when to expect further clearance, because 7-7-2 requires EFC issuance when VFR aircraft are held either inside or outside the TRSA.
The controller must inform only the aircraft inside the TRSA; the aircraft outside the TRSA does not receive TRSA services.
The controller must issue an EFC only if the delay exceeds 10 minutes, regardless of where the aircraft is holding.
EFC issuance is optional in a TRSA; the pilot must request it.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-2, the controller must inform the pilot when to expect further clearance when VFR aircraft are held either inside OR outside the TRSA. The obligation extends to both locations. This is a specific TRSA requirement that differs from some other airspace types where holding procedures may be less prescriptive.

items.instruction: Select the choice that correctly identifies which separation method is available under which condition.
items.question_text: A TRSA controller needs to separate a participating VFR aircraft from an IFR aircraft. The controller is using a display that shows Increased Situation Recognition (ISR). Which separation methods are available and which is affected by ISR?

choices.text: Visual separation and 500 feet vertical separation remain available. Target resolution is available except when ISR is being displayed.
All three methods (visual, vertical, target resolution) are equally available regardless of ISR status.
Only visual separation is available when ISR is being displayed.
Target resolution is only available when ISR is being displayed.

items.explanation: Per 7-7-3, TRSA separation may be achieved by: (a) visual separation, (b) 500 feet vertical separation, or (c) target resolution — except when ISR is being displayed. ISR affects only the third method (target resolution). The other two methods remain fully available.

items.source_label: FAA Order JO 7110.65, Paragraph 7-8-3 and Paragraph 7-9-4
lookup_context: Paragraph 7-8-3 lists three separation methods for Class C. Paragraph 7-9-4 lists separation methods for Class B with two weight-based tiers.
items.task: Compare the separation methods available in Class C versus Class B airspace and identify the key operational difference.
items.instruction: Review the separation methods in 7-8-3 (Class C) and 7-9-4 (Class B). Identify the key structural difference.
items.question_text: A controller is separating VFR aircraft from IFR aircraft in Class C airspace. How does the separation framework differ from Class B airspace?

choices.text: Class C uses uniform separation methods for all aircraft. Class B separates methods by weight: 1.5 miles for heavier aircraft, target resolution for lighter ones.
Class C and Class B use identical separation frameworks; there is no difference.
Class C requires 3 miles separation between all VFR and IFR aircraft; Class B requires 5 miles.
Class C has no visual separation option; Class B permits visual separation.

items.explanation: Per 7-8-3, Class C separation applies uniformly to all VFR aircraft being separated from IFR: visual, 500 feet vertical, or target resolution (except when ISR displayed). Per 7-9-4, Class B has a two-tier weight-based system: VFR aircraft separated from aircraft over 19,000 lbs/turbojets must use 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; VFR aircraft separated from aircraft 19,000 lbs or less use target resolution (except ISR), 500 feet vertical, or visual. The Class B framework is more complex with weight as the differentiator.

items.instruction: Identify the correct separation requirement for each scenario.
items.question_text: A Class B controller needs to separate VFR aircraft from two different IFR aircraft: an Airbus A320 (turbojet, over 19,000 lbs) and a Cessna 172 (under 19,000 lbs). Which separation methods are available for each?

choices.text: From the A320: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual. From the Cessna 172: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual.
From both: 1.5 miles, 500 feet vertical, or visual; target resolution is available for both.
From the A320: 3 miles only. From the Cessna 172: 1.5 miles only.
From both: 500 feet vertical or visual only; radar miles separation is not available in Class B.

items.explanation: Per 7-9-4b, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing more than 19,000 lbs and turbojets by: 1.5 miles (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. Per 7-9-4c, VFR aircraft must be separated from VFR/IFR aircraft weighing 19,000 lbs or less by: target resolution (except when ISR displayed), 500 feet vertical, or visual. The weight threshold creates two distinct separation tiers.

items.instruction: Where would you find the applicable rule?
items.question_text: You are working offshore airspace and encounter a wake turbulence separation situation. Chapter 8 does not address wake turbulence criteria. Where do you find the applicable separation rule?

choices.text: Elsewhere in JO 7110.65, because offshore airspace rules state that provisions in the Order apply for situations not covered by Chapter 8.
Only in ICAO documents, because offshore airspace is governed exclusively by international standards.
In facility directives only; the Order outside Chapter 8 does not apply to offshore airspace.
Nowhere — situations not covered by Chapter 8 cannot be resolved in offshore airspace.

items.explanation: Offshore airspace rules state that Chapter 8 procedures and minima apply first, and for situations not covered by Chapter 8, the provisions in the Order must apply. This means the controller should look to other chapters of JO 7110.65 for the wake turbulence criteria.

items.instruction: Identify which area permits the described operation.
items.question_text: Match each VFR flight plan operation to the area where it is permitted: (1) Operations at FL 190, (2) Operations 80 NM seaward from the shoreline, (3) Operations in airspace below the Oceanic CTAs in any FIR. Which area permits operations 80 NM seaward within controlled airspace?

choices.text: The Miami, Houston, and San Juan CTAs below FL 180.
The Oakland FIR, when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace.
All Oceanic FIR airspace below the Oceanic CTAs.
All three areas permit operations at 80 NM seaward.

items.explanation: The Oakland FIR permits VFR flight plan operations when operating less than 100 NM seaward from the shoreline within controlled airspace. The CTAs limit altitude (below FL 180) rather than distance. The all-FIR airspace category applies below Oceanic CTAs.

items.instruction: Identify the correct exception trigger.
items.question_text: An oceanic control area has an en route chart depicting a different altimeter setting basis for a specific route. The facility also has a written directive prescribing local altimeter settings for another route. A controller is working a third route with no charted or directed exception. Which routes use 29.92 inches Hg and flight levels?

choices.text: Only the directed route uses an exception; charted procedures cannot override the 29.92 standard.
All three routes use 29.92, because charted and directed exceptions do not override the oceanic standard.
Only the third route with no exception — the charted route and the directed route use their respective exceptions.
All three routes use the charted setting, because charted procedures always take precedence everywhere.

items.explanation: The oceanic altimeter rule uses flight levels and 29.92 inches Hg unless directed and/or charted otherwise. Both directed and charted exceptions are recognized triggers. The third route with no exception follows the default 29.92 rule.

items.instruction: Select the statement that correctly describes the reportable oceanic error categories.
items.question_text: Which list correctly identifies the five reportable oceanic error categories under JO 7210.632?

choices.text: Transponder failures, CPDLC logon rejections, altitude deviations, speed deviations, and route deviations.
Lateral deviations, vertical deviations, speed deviations, communication failures, and surveillance failures.
Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time errors, speed errors, and communication failures.
