Gross Navigation Errors, height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations verification.

items.explanation: JO 7210.632 establishes procedures for reporting five categories: Gross Navigation Errors (GNE), height errors, time (longitudinal) errors, intervention, and Special Area of Operations (SAO) verification. The other lists contain items not found in the source.

items.instruction: When must control estimates be used?
items.question_text: A controller is applying both time-based and distance-based separation between oceanic aircraft. For which type of separation are control estimates required to be used?

choices.text: Only for time-based separation minima.
For both time-based and distance-based separation minima.
Control estimates are optional for all separation types.
Only for distance-based separation minima.

items.explanation: Control estimates are the estimated position of aircraft with reference to time. They must be used when applying time-based separation minima. For distance-based separation, other position references apply.

items.instruction: Select the statement that correctly describes the RVSM exception rules.
items.question_text: Which statement correctly describes the rules for non-RVSM aircraft in oceanic RVSM airspace?

choices.text: Supervisor approval alone is sufficient for any non-RVSM aircraft to enter RVSM airspace.
Supervisor approval alone, without meeting the excepted aircraft criteria, is sufficient for any non-RVSM aircraft to enter RVSM airspace.
Non-RVSM exceptions are mandatory requirements that the controller must accommodate regardless of current workload or traffic conditions.
A non-RVSM aircraft that meets the excepted criteria may enter RVSM airspace without any further approval or coordination.

items.explanation: The RVSM exception rule has three layers: (1) the aircraft must meet the criteria of an excepted aircraft, (2) prior supervisor/CIC approval is required, and (3) exceptions are accommodated on a workload or traffic-permitting basis. All three layers apply.

items.instruction: Identify which ARTCC coordination duty is being described.
items.question_text: An ARTCC forward's a flight's updated estimate from 1345Z to 1349Z to the receiving oceanic facility. Which coordination duty is the ARTCC performing?

choices.text: Forwarding current flight plan and control information.
Coordinating with adjacent ATS facilities when the airspace to be protected will overlap the common boundary.
Forwarding to appropriate ATS facilities the current flight plan and control information as the flight progresses.
Coordinating IFR/VFR service continuation.

items.explanation: The 4-minute revision meets the 3-minute-or-more threshold, so this action falls under the duty to forward revisions of estimates of 3 minutes or more to the appropriate ATS facility.

items.instruction: Identify the correct rule for the situation.
items.question_text: Nonradar separation is being applied. You are transferring an oceanic flight to the next facility. The aircraft is estimated to reach the boundary at 1535Z. By what time must air-ground communications be transferred?

choices.text: 1545Z, 10 minutes after the boundary crossing.
1535Z, at the time of boundary crossing.
1525Z, 10 minutes before the estimated boundary crossing.
1530Z, 5 minutes before the estimated boundary crossing.

items.explanation: Where nonradar separation minima are applied, communications transfer must occur 5 minutes before the aircraft is estimated to reach the boundary, unless otherwise agreed.

items.instruction: Which minimum applies?
items.question_text: Two aircraft on the same track require different time-based longitudinal standards. Aircraft A, a turbojet using Mach number technique, is subject to a 10-minute minimum. Aircraft B, a nonturbojet, is subject to a 30-minute minimum. Which minimum applies between them?

choices.text: 30 minutes, the larger of the two standards.
10 minutes, the smaller standard applicable to the lead aircraft.
15 minutes, the general Chapter 8 default.
20 minutes, the average of the two standards.

items.explanation: When one aircraft requires a different time-based longitudinal standard than another, apply the larger of the two standards between the aircraft concerned. The more restrictive minimum protects both aircraft.

items.instruction: Put the reciprocal track separation steps in the correct order.
items.question_text: For aircraft on reciprocal tracks, what is the correct sequence for applying longitudinal separation?

choices.text: Wait for both aircraft to report passing each other, then apply vertical separation retroactively.
Wait for both aircraft to report passing each other visually or by reference to ground-based aids, then apply vertical separation retroactively.
Apply the crossing-track minimum at the point of intersection; reciprocal tracks use the same rule as crossing tracks.
Apply the same-direction longitudinal minimum for the entire crossing without using vertical separation, because reciprocal tracks and same tracks use identical separation rules.

items.explanation: Reciprocal track separation requires: (1) vertical separation before and after estimated passing for the applicable minimum interval, then (2) vertical separation may be discontinued only after aircraft have reported passing a significant point AND the same-direction minimum is achieved.

items.instruction: Where would you find the applicable lateral separation minimum?
items.question_text: A controller is working an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region and needs to verify the applicable lateral separation minimum for that region. Where in Chapter 8 should the controller look?

choices.text: Section 1, General, which contains all lateral separation minima.
Section 4, Lateral Separation, which applies uniformly to all regions.
Section 7, North Atlantic ICAO Region.
Section 10, North American ICAO Region, which is the default for all regions.

items.explanation: The note in 8-4-1 states that lateral separation minima are contained in the regional sections: Section 7 (North Atlantic), Section 8 (Caribbean), Section 9 (Pacific), and Section 10 (North American-Arctic CTA). The controller should look in the relevant regional section.

items.instruction: Identify which method establishes lateral separation.
items.question_text: Two aircraft are on nonintersecting flight paths. The protected airspace of their flight paths does not overlap, and reduced route protected airspace is applicable. Does lateral separation exist?

choices.text: Yes, but only if the aircraft are at least 50 NM apart.
No, nonintersecting flight paths always require the full required distance between flight paths.
No, lateral separation for nonintersecting paths requires radar identification of both aircraft.
Yes, when reduced route protected airspace is applicable and the protected airspace does not overlap.

items.explanation: For nonintersecting flight paths, lateral separation exists either when the required distance is maintained between flight paths OR when reduced route protected airspace is applicable and the protected airspace does not overlap. The second method provides an alternative to the full distance requirement.

items.instruction: Compare the two altitude-based reduction standards.
items.question_text: Compare the route protected airspace reduction rules below FL 240 versus at and above FL 240. Which values are correct for the reduced width on each side of centerline?

choices.text: Below FL 240: 5 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 10 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 10 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 5 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 10 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 15 miles each side.
Below FL 240: 5 miles each side. At and above FL 240: 5 miles each side — there is no difference.

items.explanation: Below FL 240, protected airspace reduces to 5 miles on each side to 57.14 miles from the NAVAID. At and above FL 240, it reduces to 10 miles on each side to 114.29 miles. Both then increase on a 5-degree angle. Higher altitude = wider reduced protection.

items.instruction: Identify the pressure-setting risk addressed below FL 180.
items.question_text: Why must controllers account for QNE and QNH differences below FL 180 when aircraft cross the offshore/oceanic boundary?

choices.text: Above FL 180, vertical separation is never applied across the offshore/oceanic boundary.
At and above FL 180, all aircraft use the standard altimeter setting of 29.92, so there is no QNE/QNH difference to manage.
Below FL 180, all aircraft use local altimeter settings that differ from the oceanic standard.
Below FL 180, aircraft are always in controlled airspace requiring special handling.

items.explanation: The transition level in oceanic airspace is typically FL 180. At and above this level, all aircraft use QNE (29.92 inches Hg / standard pressure setting). Below FL 180, aircraft may be using local QNH settings. The rule ensures the difference between QNE and QNH does not erode vertical separation.

items.instruction: Identify both timing conditions.
items.question_text: An outbound aircraft in offshore-to-oceanic transition needs to climb through an opposite-direction oceanic aircraft. Vertical separation must be established before the outbound crosses the boundary. What is the other timing requirement?

choices.text: Vertical separation must be established 30 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Vertical separation must be established 10 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
Vertical separation must be established 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.
No additional timing requirement exists beyond the boundary crossing condition.

items.explanation: Both timing conditions must be met: vertical separation must be established (a) before the outbound crosses the offshore/oceanic boundary, AND (b) 15 minutes before the aircraft are estimated to pass.

items.instruction: Determine whether the same-direction climb-through procedure is authorized.
items.question_text: A following aircraft requests a same-direction climb through the preceding aircraft's altitude. The preceding aircraft is level and faster, 5 minutes spacing exists, the following is 3,000 ft below, and the following will begin climb 8 minutes after the preceding's reporting point. Direct communication is maintained. May the controller use the procedure?

choices.text: No. The following aircraft must wait exactly 10 minutes after the reporting point.
No. The altitude difference must be exactly 4,000 feet.
No. The preceding aircraft must be slower than the following aircraft.
Yes. The scenario satisfies all five conditions for the same-direction climb-through procedure.

items.explanation: All five conditions are met: (a) preceding is level and equal or greater speed, (b) 5 minutes maintained, (c) following not more than 4,000 ft below (3,000 ft satisfies this), (d) climb begins within 10 minutes after the reporting point (8 minutes satisfies this), (e) direct communication maintained.

items.instruction: Determine whether the procedure is authorized.
items.question_text: A turbojet at Mach 0.84 is transitioning from oceanic airspace into radar coverage at high speed. The controller estimates insufficient time to establish another form of separation if radar identification is delayed. May the radar identification transition procedure be applied?

choices.text: No, the procedure is not authorized if there is insufficient time to establish other approved separation if radar ID is delayed.
Yes, the procedure applies automatically whenever an aircraft enters radar coverage from oceanic airspace.
No, because the procedure requires the transiting aircraft to be radar identified before crossing the boundary.
Yes, but only for aircraft at or below FL 290.

items.explanation: The radar identification transition procedure has an explicit exception: it is not authorized if there is insufficient time for the controller to establish other approved separation in the event of a delay or inability to establish radar identification, considering factors such as aircraft performance, speed, weather, traffic, workload, and frequency congestion.

items.instruction: Choose the correct separation method.
items.question_text: A controller needs to separate an aircraft from a temporary stationary airspace reservation along a route where the protected airspace would overlap the reservation. How can the aircraft still transit the area?

choices.text: Cancel the aircraft's clearance and reroute to a completely different airway.
Cancel the aircraft's IFR clearance and instruct the pilot to reroute to a completely different airway that avoids the reservation area entirely.
No separation method works; aircraft must always be rerouted around stationary reservations.
Use longitudinal separation by measuring the time interval from the reservation activation to the aircraft's estimated crossing point.

items.explanation: Two methods are available for stationary reservations. If lateral separation is unavailable (protected airspace would overlap), vertical separation may be used. The protected area for vertical separation includes the reservation plus a buffer of one-half the lateral separation minimum.

items.instruction: Order the controller actions when a pilot refuses an avoidance clearance.
items.question_text: A pilot refuses a clearance to avoid an active airspace reservation. Put the controller's required actions in the correct sequence.

choices.text: Inform the using agency first, then advise the pilot of the hazard, then issue a new clearance.
Approve the refusal, request the pilot's intentions, then inform the using agency.
Inform the pilot of the potential hazard, then advise that services will not be provided in the reservation, then if possible inform the using agency.
Request the pilot's intentions, then inform the pilot of the hazard, then approve or deny the refusal.

items.explanation: The three actions are: (1) inform the pilot of the potential hazard, (2) advise that services will not be provided while the flight is within the reservation, and (3) if possible, inform the appropriate using agency. The third is conditional but should be attempted.

items.instruction: Identify which separation method matches the description.
items.question_text: For a temporary moving airspace reservation, which method clears aircraft so that the appropriate longitudinal minimum exists ahead of the first or behind the last aircraft in the reservation?

choices.text: Vertical separation.
Lateral separation.
Radar separation.
Longitudinal separation.

items.explanation: Moving reservations have three separation methods: lateral (non-overlapping protected airspace), longitudinal (minimum ahead of first or behind last aircraft in the reservation), and vertical (reservation plus perimeter buffer). The longitudinal method treats the reservation as a moving block in time/distance.

items.instruction: Where would you confirm the regional rules apply?
items.question_text: A controller is working an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region and needs to confirm that Section 7 lateral separation minima apply rather than the general Chapter 8 lateral minima. Where is the governing rule that establishes Section 7 as the controlling authority?

choices.text: Section 1 General provisions, because the general rules in Chapter 8 apply uniformly across all ICAO regions regardless of the regional sections.
The lateral separation note, which lists the regional lateral minima but does not establish which section controls for a given aircraft in the region.
ICAO Document 7030, the North Atlantic Regional Supplementary Procedures, which operates independently of JO 7110.65 for this region's airspace.
Section 1, General, which applies uniformly to all regions regardless of regional provisions.

items.explanation: Each regional section has an application paragraph that establishes the section as the governing authority for that region. For the North Atlantic, 8-7-1 states that services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 7 except when noted otherwise.

items.instruction: Where would you find the vertical separation rules?
items.question_text: You need to verify vertical separation procedures for an aircraft in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. Where should you look?

choices.text: ICAO Doc 4444, Procedures for Air Navigation Services.
Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification.
Chapter 8, Section 7, North Atlantic ICAO Region only.
Chapter 8, Section 5, Offshore/Oceanic Transition Procedures.

items.explanation: All four regional vertical separation paragraphs (8-7-2, 8-8-2, 8-9-2, 8-10-2) reference Chapter 4, IFR, Section 5, Altitude Assignment and Verification as the source for vertical separation procedures.

items.instruction: Match each aircraft type to its longitudinal minimum.
items.question_text: In the North Atlantic ICAO Region, which longitudinal minimum matches which aircraft category? Match: (A) Supersonic, same type cruise climb with common point — (B) Nonturbojet outside WATRS — (C) Turbojet subsonic not using Mach technique.

choices.text: A = 10 minutes, B = 20 minutes, C = 10 minutes.
A = 15 minutes, B = 20 minutes, C = 10 minutes.
A = 10 minutes, B = 30 minutes, C = 15 minutes.
A = 15 minutes, B = 30 minutes, C = 15 minutes.

items.explanation: In the NAT Region: supersonic same-type cruise climb with common point = 10 minutes; nonturbojet outside WATRS = 30 minutes; turbojet subsonic not using Mach technique = 15 minutes. Each category has a distinct minimum reflecting the aircraft's navigation and speed characteristics.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An RNP 4 approved aircraft with CPDLC and ADS-C contracts maintained is operating in airspace designated for 23 NM lateral separation in the North Atlantic ICAO Region. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 60 NM.
50 NM.
30 NM.
23 NM.

items.explanation: 23 NM applies to approved aircraft (minimum RNP 4, RCP 240, RSP 180) operating within designated airspace with direct controller/pilot communications via voice or CPDLC, and required ADS-C contracts maintained and monitored by an automated flight data processor.

items.instruction: Where would you confirm the regional rules?
items.question_text: A controller working in the Caribbean ICAO Region encounters a situation where Section 8 procedures differ from general Chapter 8 procedures. Which source confirms that Section 8 controls?

choices.text: The Caribbean application paragraph, which states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 8 except when noted otherwise.
The general Chapter 8 rule, because it applies uniformly over all regional sections.
The ICAO Caribbean Regional Supplementary Procedures.
The Caribbean lateral separation section only; the vertical and longitudinal sections defer to Chapter 8 generally.

items.explanation: The application paragraph for each ICAO region establishes that the region-specific section controls for that area. For the Caribbean, 8-8-1 states that services are provided with Section 8 procedures and minima except when noted otherwise.

items.instruction: Identify the correct longitudinal minimum for each aircraft type.
items.question_text: In the Caribbean ICAO Region, match: (1) supersonic aircraft of the same type in cruise climb with common point, (2) nonturbojet aircraft in the WATRS, (3) turbojet aircraft below FL 200 in the San Juan Oceanic CTA outside WATRS.

choices.text: 1 = 10 minutes, 2 = 15 minutes, 3 = 30 minutes.
1 = 10 minutes, 2 = 20 minutes, 3 = 20 minutes.
1 = 15 minutes, 2 = 20 minutes, 3 = 20 minutes.
1 = 15 minutes, 2 = 30 minutes, 3 = 15 minutes.

items.explanation: Caribbean longitudinal minima: supersonic same-type with common point = 10 minutes; nonturbojet in WATRS = 20 minutes; turbojet below FL 200 in San Juan (outside WATRS), Miami, and Houston CTAs/FIRs = 20 minutes. Note that 20 minutes appears for multiple categories.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum for the described operation.
items.question_text: Two aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval are operating west of 55 degrees West between the U.S. and Bermuda. What Caribbean lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 90 NM.
60 NM.

items.explanation: In the Caribbean, 90 NM applies to aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 operating within WATRS, or west of 55 West between the U.S./Canada/Bermuda and Caribbean points. The 100 NM tier applies to other aircraft west of 55 West not covered by narrower tiers. The 120 NM tier applies east of 55 West.

items.instruction: What must the controller do?
items.question_text: An IFR flight in the San Juan CTA has been cleared to climb in VFR conditions. The controller notices weather deteriorating along the route, and VFR conditions may become impractical. What must the controller do?

choices.text: Cancel the IFR flight plan and have the aircraft continue VFR.
Nothing; the pilot is responsible for maintaining VFR conditions.
Immediately recall the clearance and instruct the aircraft to return to its previously assigned altitude.
Issue alternative instructions, and issue traffic information to aircraft not separated per the section minima.

items.explanation: When an IFR flight is cleared for VFR climb or descent in the CTAs, and there is a possibility that VFR conditions may become impractical, the controller must: (1) issue alternative instructions, and (2) issue traffic information to aircraft not separated in accordance with the minima in the section.

items.instruction: Where is the governing rule?
items.question_text: You are providing ATC service in the Pacific ICAO Region. A colleague says general Chapter 8 procedures always apply. Where do you confirm that Section 9 procedures take precedence for this region?

choices.text: The Pacific application paragraph, which states services are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 9 except when noted otherwise.
The general oceanic rule in Section 1, which states directives and LOAs may prescribe different procedures.
The ICAO Pacific Regional Supplementary Procedures; JO 7110.65 does not independently govern the Pacific Region.
Chapter 4, which is the universal source for all oceanic airspace procedures.

items.explanation: The Pacific application paragraph (8-9-1) establishes that ATC services in the Pacific ICAO Region are provided with the procedures and minima in Section 9 except when noted otherwise. It is the regional governing rule.

items.instruction: Match the minimum to the situation.
items.question_text: Which Pacific longitudinal minimum applies when turbojet aircraft follow the same track or continuously diverging tracks until another form of separation is provided?

choices.text: 20 minutes.
10 minutes.
5 minutes.
15 minutes.

items.explanation: In the Pacific Region, the time-based longitudinal minima include: 15 minutes between aircraft (general), 10 minutes between turbojet aircraft on same/continuously diverging tracks until another form of separation is provided, and Mach number technique minima.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An RNP 10 approved aircraft is operating in the Pacific ICAO Region in an area where RNP separation is authorized. CPDLC is not currently available and ADS-C contracts are not maintained. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.
30 NM.

items.explanation: 50 NM applies to RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved aircraft in areas where RNP separation is authorized. The 23 NM tier additionally requires CPDLC/voice communications and ADS-C contracts maintained with automated flight data processing. Without CPDLC and ADS-C, the aircraft qualifies for 50 NM based on RNP 10 approval.

items.instruction: Identify the key difference.
items.question_text: Compare the four regional application paragraphs. The North Atlantic, Caribbean, and Pacific paragraphs all contain four words not found in the North American application paragraph. What are they?

choices.text: 'Contained in this section.'
'With the procedures and minima.'
'Provide air traffic control services.'
'Except when noted otherwise.'

items.explanation: The North American application paragraph omits 'except when noted otherwise' — language present in all three other regional application paragraphs. The North American region simply states services are provided with the procedures and minima contained in Section 10 without a stated exception mechanism.

items.instruction: What makes the North American vertical separation unique?
items.question_text: What additional document beyond Chapter 4, Section 5 is required for vertical separation in the North American ICAO Region?

choices.text: Facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes.
A letter of agreement with the Anchorage ARTCC.
No additional document is required; the North American rule is identical to other regions.
An ICAO regional air navigation agreement.

items.explanation: The North American vertical separation paragraph is unique in requiring both Chapter 4, Section 5 AND facility directives depicting the transition between flight levels and metric altitudes. This reflects the operational need to manage flight level/metric altitude interfaces in the Arctic CTA.

items.instruction: How long may DME/RNAV separation continue without direct voice communications?
items.question_text: In the North American ICAO Region, two turbojet aircraft are being separated using DME/RNAV in the NOPAC Route System. Both are radar identified. One aircraft's voice communications become unreliable. How long may DME/RNAV separation continue?

choices.text: Not more than 60 minutes without direct voice communications.
Not more than 90 minutes without direct voice communications.
DME/RNAV separation requires direct voice communications at all times and cannot continue without it.
Indefinitely, because both aircraft are radar identified.

items.explanation: Per the North American DME/RNAV separation rules, unless both aircraft are radar identified, both must provide DME/RNAV distance reports via direct voice. Application without direct voice communications may not continue for more than 90 minutes.

items.instruction: Select the correct lateral minimum.
items.question_text: An aircraft without RNP 4 or RNP 10 approval is operating in the North American ICAO Region outside the Anchorage Oceanic and Continental CTAs. What lateral minimum applies?

choices.text: 23 NM.
50 NM.
90 NM.

items.explanation: The North American region has three lateral tiers: 23 NM (ADS-C with RNP 4/RCP 240/RSP 180 in Anchorage Oceanic/Continental CTAs only), 50 NM (RNP 4 or RNP 10 approved where authorized), and 90 NM (aircraft not covered by the narrower tiers).

items.instruction: Identify the operation to which Section 3 SUA/ATCAA/ALTRV separation procedures still apply.
items.question_text: For which operation must the controller continue applying Section 3 separation procedures?

choices.text: The pilot informs ATC that permission has been obtained from the using agency to operate in the airspace.
The using agency informs ATC they have given permission for the aircraft to operate in the airspace.
The restricted area, MOA, ATCAA, or stationary ALTRV has been released to the controlling agency.
The aircraft is IFR and in radar contact at least 20 NM from the SUA boundary.

items.explanation: The IFR aircraft in radar contact remains subject to the procedures because those facts do not create an exception. Exceptions include using-agency permission, release of the airspace to the controlling agency, an approved ALTRV when the airspace is not an ATCAA, or an applicable letter of agreement. Alert Areas and Controlled Firing Areas are excluded from these procedures.

items.instruction: Determine whether the statement about special air traffic rules is correct.
items.question_text: A trainee states: 'Every airspace area listed in 14 CFR Part 93 is a Special Flight Rules Area.' Is this correct?

choices.text: Correct. Every airspace area listed in 14 CFR Part 93 is, by definition, a Special Flight Rules Area.
Incorrect. Not all Part 93 areas are SFRAs, but all SFRAs follow Part 93 rules.
Correct. SFRAs and SATRs are identical categories with the same operational rules.
Incorrect. Special Flight Rules Areas are defined in 14 CFR Part 91, not in Part 93.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-9, Special Flight Rules Areas are areas where flight is subject to special air traffic rules in 14 CFR Part 93 unless otherwise authorized by ATC. Not all areas listed in Part 93 are SFRAs — the distinction is that SFRAs have specific flight rules, while other Part 93 areas may have different regulatory structures.

items.instruction: Construct the correct phraseology for this situation using the prescribed format.
items.situation: An aircraft squawking the assigned SFRA code has been radar-identified. You need to advise the aircraft to proceed on course as requested while remaining outside Class B, C, and D airspace.
items.question_text: Which transmission uses the correct prescribed phraseology?

choices.text: N123AB, RADAR CONTACT, PROCEED AS FILED BUT STAY CLEAR OF ALL CONTROLLED AIRSPACE.
N123AB, TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AS REQUESTED BUT REMAIN OUTSIDE OF CLASS B, C, AND/OR D AIRSPACE AS APPROPRIATE.
N123AB, TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, CLEARED TO PROCEED ON COURSE, REMAIN CLEAR OF CLASS B AIRSPACE ONLY.
N123AB, SQUAWK OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AND MAINTAIN VFR AT ALL TIMES IN THE SFRA.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-10a phraseology, the correct transmission is: '(ACID) TRANSPONDER OBSERVED, PROCEED ON COURSE AS REQUESTED BUT REMAIN OUTSIDE OF CLASS B, C, AND/OR D AIRSPACE AS APPROPRIATE.' The key elements are 'TRANSPONDER OBSERVED' (not 'radar contact') and the specific airspace exclusion language covering B, C, and/or D as appropriate.

items.instruction: Evaluate the pilot readback against the prescribed IR clearance phraseology.
items.clearance: CLEARED INTO IR 801. MAINTAIN IR 801 ALTITUDES.
items.situation: The controller issues: 'CLEARED INTO IR 801. MAINTAIN IR 801 ALTITUDES.'
items.question_text: Which pilot readback correctly acknowledges the clearance?

choices.text: Roger, entering IR 801 at this time, Callsign 123.
Cleared into IR 801, maintaining flight level two zero zero, Callsign 123.
Cleared direct IR 801, will maintain VFR, Callsign 123.
Cleared into IR 801, maintaining IR 801 altitudes, Callsign 123.

items.explanation: Per 9-2-6 phraseology, the clearance is 'CLEARED INTO IR (designator). MAINTAIN (altitude)' or 'MAINTAIN IR (designator) ALTITUDE(S).' The correct readback mirrors the clearance: 'Cleared into IR 801, maintaining IR 801 altitudes.' The readback must include the IR designator and altitude restriction as issued.

items.instruction: Determine whether the controller may request a different route in each situation.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations may the controller request the fuel-dumping aircraft to fly a different route?

choices.text: An aircraft dumping fuel because of a dual hydraulic failure; the controller prefers an offshore route.
An aircraft dumping fuel because of an engine fire; the dump route passes over a populated area.
An aircraft dumping fuel for any reason — emergency routing changes are always permitted.
An aircraft dumping fuel to reduce weight for an overweight landing; the dump route passes over a populated area.

items.explanation: Per 9-4-2, the controller may request a different route except when the aircraft is dumping fuel for emergency reasons. The overweight landing (first option) is a non-emergency operation, so a route change may be requested. The engine fire and dual hydraulic failure are emergency situations where the aircraft cannot be asked to deviate. The rule applies to both VFR and IFR conditions.

items.instruction: Choose the action that respects the pilot in command's emergency decision.
items.question_text: A controller recommends an alternative diversion airport to a distressed pilot, but the pilot insists on the original destination. What is the correct controller action?

choices.text: Continue assisting toward the original destination — the controller recommends, the pilot decides.
Override the pilot and issue an amended clearance to the alternative airport the controller recommended.
Withhold further vectors until the pilot acknowledges the safety advantages of the recommended airport.
Transfer the aircraft to a supervisor to resolve the destination disagreement.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-2a requires assistance consistent with the pilot's requests. The controller may recommend alternatives but must not override the pilot's decision. 14 CFR § 91.3 reinforces pilot-in-command authority.

items.instruction: Select the correct combination of controller actions for a military fighter-type aircraft emergency at low altitude.
items.question_text: Which combination of controller actions correctly follows the guidance for an inflight emergency involving a military fighter-type aircraft at low altitude?

choices.text: Keep the current frequency and transponder code, and transmit only essential communications.
Switch to 121.5 MHz, assign a discrete emergency squawk, and request a full aircraft status report.
Change to a clear frequency, assign a new transponder code for better tracking, and maintain normal communication flow.
Transfer communications to the military base frequency and instruct the pilot to report all system statuses.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-1-7a states that frequency and transponder code changes should be avoided and radio transmissions held to a minimum, especially at low altitude. The pilot's full attention is required to maintain control of the aircraft.

items.instruction: Identify the correct sequence for gathering and acting on emergency information.
items.question_text: Which sequence correctly describes how a controller should gather information and begin assisting an inflight emergency?

choices.text: Obtain the three minimum items, begin assistance, then gather supplementary items as necessary.
Gather all 17 information items, then begin assistance once the complete operational picture is known.
Begin assistance immediately upon hearing any distress call, without waiting for any specific information items.
Gather the 14 supplementary items first, then ask for aircraft identification and emergency nature last.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-1a requires starting assistance as soon as the three minimum items are obtained. The 14 supplementary items in paragraph 10-2-1b are obtained after initiating action, as necessary.

items.instruction: Identify which event triggers the requirement to consider an aircraft emergency exists.
items.question_text: Which situation requires a controller to consider that an aircraft emergency exists and inform the RCC or ARTCC?

choices.text: An emergency is declared by a system-generated transmission from an aircraft.
An aircraft makes a routine position report two minutes later than the expected reporting time.
A pilot reports a minor avionics cooling fan failure that has no effect on flight safety.
An aircraft requests a deviation around a small area of light precipitation while remaining in VMC.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-5a lists specific emergency declaration sources including system-generated transmissions. Routine operational events, minor equipment issues without safety impact, and standard weather deviations do not trigger the emergency consideration requirement.

items.instruction: Select the condition that allows radar assistance to a VFR pilot who is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency.
items.question_text: A VFR pilot in IMC is not IFR qualified and refuses to declare an emergency. Under which condition may the controller provide radar assistance?

choices.text: The controller determines an emergency condition exists or is imminent, regardless of the pilot's refusal to declare one.
Radar assistance is automatic for any aircraft that enters IMC, regardless of pilot qualifications or declarations.
Radar assistance may be provided only after the pilot files an IFR flight plan with the appropriate controlling agency.
Radar assistance is prohibited unless the pilot explicitly declares an emergency or holds a valid instrument rating.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-8c allows radar assistance when the pilot refuses to declare an emergency but the controller independently determines emergency conditions exist or are imminent. The controller's determination under 10-1-1c operates independently of the pilot's declaration.

items.instruction: Choose the correct radar-assistance technique for a non-IFR-qualified pilot.
items.question_text: Which technique should a controller apply when providing radar assistance to a pilot not qualified for IFR operations?

choices.text: Make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions so it can fly a straight course while in IFR conditions.
Require a descent while the aircraft is in a turn, because combining maneuvers reduces total time spent in IMC.
Change frequencies frequently to identify the clearest communications channel available.
Have the pilot lower the landing gear after entering IMC, since configuration changes are less disorienting in clouds.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-9b instructs controllers to make turns while the aircraft is in VFR conditions. Paragraph 10-2-9d states that climb or descent should be avoided while turning in IFR conditions. Paragraph 10-2-9a advises against frequency changes except when necessary. Paragraph 10-2-9c says to configure the aircraft while in VFR conditions.

items.instruction: Identify the correct ELT notification responsibility.
items.question_text: How does the ELT signal notification responsibility differ between en route and terminal facilities?

choices.text: En route notifies the RCC directly; terminal notifies the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC.
Both facility types notify the RCC directly, and terminal facilities make an additional courtesy call to the ARTCC.
En route notifies the ARTCC; terminal notifies the RCC directly.
Both facility types notify only the ARTCC, which determines whether RCC notification is warranted.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-10a establishes en route facilities notify the RCC directly. Paragraph 10-2-10b establishes terminal facilities notify the ARTCC, which coordinates with the RCC. Terminal facilities never contact the RCC directly for ELT events.

items.instruction: Identify when use of the EOVM or EAM is authorized.
items.question_text: Under which condition may the EOVM or EAM be used to provide emergency advisory service to an aircraft?

choices.text: The pilot has declared an emergency, or the controller has determined an emergency condition exists or is imminent.
The controller wants to provide a more direct route that would descend an aircraft below the MVA during routine operations.
The pilot asks for a demonstration of the EOVM capabilities during a routine VFR training flight.
The controller needs to vector an aircraft around weather and the shortest available path goes below the published minimum altitude.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-2-17a states the EOVM/EAM must only be used under two conditions: the pilot has declared an emergency, or the controller has determined that an emergency condition exists or is imminent because the pilot cannot maintain appropriate terrain/obstacle clearance. Routine operations do not qualify.

items.instruction: Identify which information item is added when the ARTCC forwards an overdue aircraft report to the RCC.
items.question_text: When the ARTCC forwards information about an overdue aircraft to the RCC, which item is added that was not on the terminal facility's notification to the ARTCC?

choices.text: Action taken by the reporting facility and proposed action.
Aircraft beacon code.
Flight plan including color of aircraft.
Last position report and how determined.

items.explanation: The ARTCC adds 'facility and person calling' and 'action taken by reporting facility and proposed action' when forwarding to the RCC. These two items are not on the terminal-to-ARTCC list in paragraph 10-3-2. Items like beacon code, flight plan, and position report appear on both lists.

items.instruction: Identify the correct time reference for the 30-minute traffic restriction.
items.question_text: An overdue IFR aircraft was both given an approach clearance at 1415Z and assigned an EFC time of 1430Z. Which time reference starts the 30-minute IFR traffic restriction clock?

choices.text: 1415Z — the approach clearance time takes precedence over the EFC time.
1430Z — the EFC time is the mandatory reference for all lost-communication time calculations.
1445Z — the restriction clock begins 30 minutes after the earlier of the two references.
The time the flight plan was filed — it is the only time reference common to all overdue aircraft situations.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-1 lists five time references in priority order. The approach clearance time (subpara a) has higher priority than EFC time (subpara b). When multiple references apply, use the highest-priority one that is available.

items.instruction: Determine the effect of withdrawing concurrence after the traffic suspension period expires.
items.question_text: After the 30-minute suspension period for an overdue aircraft expires, all affected pilots concur to resume normal ATC. Fifteen minutes later, one pilot withdraws that concurrence. What happens to normal ATC operations?

choices.text: Normal ATC must be suspended again because concurrence must be maintained for a full 30 minutes after the suspension period expires.
Normal ATC continues because concurrence was valid at the moment the suspension period originally expired.
Normal ATC continues for all aircraft except the one whose pilot withdrew concurrence.
The controller must restart the 30-minute suspension period from the time concurrence was withdrawn.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-4-3 requires that concurrence be maintained for a period of 30 minutes after the suspension period expires. If concurrence is withdrawn during that 30-minute window, normal ATC must be suspended again.

items.instruction: Identify which ATC action is permitted at an airport underlying an active Debris Response Area.
items.question_text: A DRA has been activated and an airport lies underneath it. Which action may ATC take at that airport?

choices.text: Continue broadcasting the mishap alert to affected aircraft on all appropriate frequencies.
Issue a takeoff clearance to an aircraft holding short of the runway.
Issue a landing clearance to an arriving aircraft on final approach.
Issue taxi instructions for an aircraft to reposition from the ramp to the active runway.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-5-2 prohibits ATC from issuing takeoff or landing clearances at airports underlying an active DRA. However, the alert broadcast obligation continues — it must be reissued every 5 minutes for 20 minutes following the last known event.

items.instruction: Identify the correct ordering of oceanic emergency phases.
items.question_text: Which sequence correctly orders the three oceanic emergency phases from least to most severe?

choices.text: INCERFA (Uncertainty), ALERFA (Alert), DETRESFA (Distress).
ALERFA (Alert), INCERFA (Uncertainty), DETRESFA (Distress).
DETRESFA (Distress), ALERFA (Alert), INCERFA (Uncertainty).
INCERFA (Uncertainty), DETRESFA (Distress), ALERFA (Alert).

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-2 describes three escalating emergency phases. INCERFA (Uncertainty) is the least severe, triggered by loss of communication for 30+ minutes. ALERFA (Alert) is intermediate, triggered when communication attempts fail. DETRESFA (Distress) is most severe, triggered when fuel is exhausted or forced landing is imminent.

items.instruction: Identify the correct conditions for discontinuing IFR separation between SAR and distressed aircraft.
items.question_text: A SAR aircraft has established visual contact with a distressed aircraft in oceanic airspace. The SAR pilot concurs to discontinue IFR separation, but the distressed pilot has not yet responded. May IFR separation be discontinued?

choices.text: No — both pilots must concur in addition to visual or radar contact being established by the SAR aircraft.
Yes — the SAR pilot's concurrence alone is sufficient once visual contact has been established.
Yes — visual contact by the SAR aircraft automatically terminates the IFR separation requirement.
No — IFR separation may never be discontinued between SAR aircraft and aircraft in distress in oceanic airspace.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-6-5a requires BOTH visual or radar contact by the SAR aircraft AND concurrence of pilots of BOTH aircraft before IFR separation may be discontinued.

items.instruction: Identify the correct IFR/SVFR lateral separation minimum for a ground missile emergency.
items.question_text: When rerouting an IFR aircraft under radar control around a ground missile emergency location that is accurately depicted on radar, what lateral separation minimum applies?

choices.text: 1 mile between the emergency location and the aircraft.
3 miles, which is the standard terminal radar separation minimum.
5 miles, which is the standard en route radar separation minimum.
2 miles, which is the reduced separation standard authorized for emergency reroutes.

items.explanation: Paragraph 10-7-2a specifies 1 mile lateral separation between the emergency location and an aircraft under radar control when the location can be accurately determined by reference to the radar scope.

items.instruction: Identify which set of items correctly and completely represents the traffic management framework.
items.question_text: Which of the following correctly states all three elements of the traffic management framework?

choices.text: Balance demand with capacity for maximum NAS utilization; use MIT in preference to TBFM to the maximum extent feasible; the ATCS is integral to mission execution.
Eliminate all holding delays system-wide; use TBFM in preference to MIT; delegate all flow decisions to the ATCSCC without facility input.
Balance demand with capacity for maximum NAS utilization; use TBFM in preference to MIT; controllers may override any TMI independently when they determine it is necessary.
Balance demand with capacity for maximum NAS utilization; use TBFM in preference to MIT to the maximum extent feasible; the ATCS is integral to mission execution.

items.explanation: The traffic management framework establishes three elements: the mission — balancing demand with capacity for maximum efficient NAS utilization; the TBFM preference rule — TBFM must be used to the maximum extent feasible in preference to MIT; and the ATCS role — controllers are integral to mission execution. Choice 1 reverses the TBFM/MIT preference relationship. Choice 2 substitutes an entirely different mission and adds an incorrect claim about ATCSCC exclusivity. Choice 3 adds an unauthorized controller override authority that the source does not grant.

items.instruction: Identify the role responsible for each duty described.
items.question_text: A facility is preparing for the day shift. Three duties must be handled: (1) ensure an operational briefing is conducted with the minimum required participants; (2) keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of developing weather that may cause congestion; (3) enforce active TBM operations and TMIs on position. Which role is responsible for each of these three duties, in order?

choices.text: OS/CIC for the briefing; STMCIC for the notification; Personnel providing traffic management services for the enforcement.
STMCIC for the briefing; OS/CIC for the notification; Personnel providing air traffic services for the enforcement.
STMCIC for the briefing; STMCIC for the notification; OS/CIC for the enforcement.
OS/CIC for the briefing; OS/CIC for the notification; Personnel providing air traffic services for the enforcement.

items.explanation: Per 11-1-2a1, the STMCIC must ensure the operational briefing is conducted at least once during day and evening shifts. Per 11-1-2b1, the OS/CIC must keep the TMU and affected sectors apprised of situations that may cause congestion or delays. Per 11-1-2c1, personnel providing air traffic services must ensure TBM operations and TMIs are enforced within their area of responsibility. Each role has distinct duties, and the enforcement groups differ — the STMCIC ensures enforcement by personnel providing traffic management services (a3), while the OS/CIC ensures enforcement by personnel providing air traffic services (b4). Cross-role confusion is a common operational risk when duties appear similar across the role structure.

items.instruction: Identify the correct Canadian airspace class for the description.
items.question_text: Controlled airspace of defined dimensions within which activities must be confined because of their nature, or within which limitations are imposed upon operations not part of those activities. This airspace may be classified as either advisory or restricted.

choices.text: Class C airspace.
Class E airspace.
Class G airspace.
Class F airspace.

items.explanation: Class F is unique among Canadian airspace classes — it is the only class defined by the purpose or nature of activities within it rather than solely by altitude limits and flight rules. It is airspace of defined dimensions established because activities must be confined or because limitations must be imposed on non-participating operations. It may be classified as Class F advisory or Class F restricted, per 12-1-2f. Class C is defined by VFR clearance requirements, Class E by the absence of special VFR requirements, and Class G as uncontrolled airspace with no ATC authority.

items.instruction: Identify the operations that receive required ATC separation under Canadian procedures.
items.question_text: Which statement correctly describes when ATC applies lateral, longitudinal, or vertical separation?

choices.text: Only between IFR operations in instrument meteorological conditions.
Between operations conducted under IFR or CVFR clearances, regardless of weather conditions.
Between all VFR operations in controlled airspace whenever radar service is available.
Only when both operations are on the same airway or route segment.

items.explanation: The separation obligation applies to operations conducted under IFR or CVFR clearances regardless of weather. It is based on clearance type, not IMC, radar availability, or whether the aircraft share the same route. Ordinary VFR operations are outside this specific separation requirement.

items.instruction: Choose the correct sequence of altitude actions.
items.question_text: A turbine-powered operation loses communications after departure in Canadian airspace, was cleared to a fix short of its destination, and will enter U.S. airspace within 10 minutes after the clearance limit. Which of the following represents the correct sequence of altitude actions?

choices.text: Climb immediately to the filed altitude as soon as the clearance limit is crossed, maintain that filed altitude all the way to the destination, and then descend for the approach once the destination is in sight.

Maintain the last assigned altitude until the operation physically enters U.S. airspace at the border, climb to the border-crossing altitude upon that entry, and maintain filed altitude for the remainder of the route.

Maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) for 10 minutes after the clearance limit, then descend to the en route minimum altitude, and finally climb to the filed altitude upon reaching the destination.

Maintain the last assigned altitude or MEA (whichever is higher) until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, commence the border-crossing climb at the estimated time of crossing the Canada/U.S. boundary, and then proceed at the filed altitude(s) or flight level(s).

items.explanation: The correct sequence combines two rules from 12-1-5: (1) the standard rule — maintain the higher of the last assigned altitude/flight level or MEA until 10 minutes beyond the clearance limit, then proceed at filed altitudes; and (2) the border-crossing exception — when U.S. airspace is entered within 10 minutes of the clearance limit, the climb to the border-crossing altitude commences at the estimated time of crossing the boundary, which occurs before the 10-minute hold period expires. The first distractor skips the hold entirely. The second uses border entry as the climb trigger instead of the estimated crossing time. The third incorrectly adds a descent after the hold period.

items.instruction: Identify which operation meets the Canadian SVFR visibility minimum.
items.question_text: A helicopter and a fixed-wing aircraft both request special VFR at the same Canadian control zone. The reported ground visibility is 3/4 mile. Which statement correctly describes the visibility requirement for each?

choices.text: Both meet their minimums because 3/4 mile exceeds the common 1/2 mile threshold that applies to all SVFR operations in Canada.
The helicopter meets its 1/2 mile minimum; the fixed-wing aircraft does not meet its 1 mile minimum.
Both must have at least 1 mile flight visibility — the helicopter minimum applies only during daylight operations below 500 feet.
The helicopter requires 1 mile because ground visibility is available; the fixed-wing aircraft requires 1/2 mile if flight visibility can be confirmed.

items.explanation: Per 12-1-7c, Canadian SVFR weather minimums differ by operation type: aircraft other than helicopters require 1 mile flight visibility (using ground visibility when reported), while helicopters require only 1/2 mile flight visibility (using ground visibility when available). At 3/4 mile reported ground visibility, the helicopter meets its 1/2 mile minimum but the fixed-wing aircraft falls short of its 1 mile minimum. The second distractor correctly identifies the split minimum but reverses which type gets which value.

lookup_context: ERAM ACL or DL Free Text Area entry
Clearance abbreviation
Meaning: cleared to airport, point of intended landing
items.instruction: Using TBL 13-1-1 Clearance Abbreviations, select the correct abbreviation for the clearance described.
items.question_text: A controller needs to annotate that an aircraft has been cleared to the airport (its point of intended landing). Which clearance abbreviation should be used?

choices.text: F — Cleared to the fix.
A — Cleared to airport (point of intended landing).
D — Cleared to depart from the fix.
V — Cleared over the fix.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, abbreviation 'A' means 'Cleared to airport (point of intended landing).' 'F' means cleared to a fix, 'D' means cleared to depart from a fix, and 'V' means cleared over a fix. These are all distinct clearance types with different operational meanings, and using the wrong abbreviation could mislead the receiving controller.

lookup_context: ERAM ACL or DL Free Text Area entry
Miscellaneous abbreviation
Pilot reports a TCAS resolution advisory
items.instruction: Using TBL 13-1-2 Miscellaneous Abbreviations, select the correct abbreviation for the situation described.
items.question_text: A pilot reports a TCAS Resolution Advisory during a climb. Which abbreviation should the controller use to annotate this event in the Free Text Area?

choices.text: TA — TACAN approach.
RX — Report crossing.
RA — Resolution advisory (Pilot-reported TCAS event).
RP — Report immediately upon passing.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-2, 'RA' means 'Resolution advisory (Pilot-reported TCAS event).' This is the correct abbreviation for annotating a pilot-reported TCAS RA. 'TA' means TACAN approach, a completely different concept. Using 'TA' for a TCAS event would create confusion since TCAS also uses the abbreviation TA for Traffic Advisory, but the table reserves RA specifically for the pilot-reported TCAS resolution advisory event.

items.instruction: Determine which abbreviation source to consult for the control information described, and select the correct abbreviation.
items.question_text: A controller needs to annotate in the Free Text Area that an aircraft has been cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion. Which source should the controller consult, and what abbreviation applies?

choices.text: TBL 13-1-3 (EDST Equivalents) — 'CR' for Cruise.
TBL 13-1-1 (Clearance Abbreviations) — 'PD' for Cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion.
TBL 13-1-2 (Miscellaneous Abbreviations) — 'PT' for Procedure turn.
TBL 13-1-1 (Clearance Abbreviations) — 'Q' for Cleared to fly specified sectors.

items.explanation: Per TBL 13-1-1, 'PD' means 'Cleared to climb/descend at pilot's discretion.' This is a clearance abbreviation, so TBL 13-1-1 is the correct source table. TBL 13-1-2 covers miscellaneous abbreviations like approach types and pilot reports. TBL 13-1-3 covers EDST equivalents for control information symbols. Knowing which table to consult is a source-use skill—controllers must be able to quickly locate the correct abbreviation reference.

items.instruction: For each coding type, determine whether it is acknowledged on the ACL or the DL.
items.question_text: Match the automated notification coding to the correct list where it must be acknowledged: IAFDOF coding, UTM coding, and EDCT coding. Which codings are acknowledged on the ACL, and which on the DL?

choices.text: IAFDOF and UTM on the ACL; EDCT on the DL.
IAFDOF and EDCT on the ACL; UTM on the DL.
All three are acknowledged on the ACL only.
UTM and EDCT on the ACL; IAFDOF on the DL.

items.explanation: IAFDOF and UTM coding must be acknowledged on the ACL, while EDCT coding must be acknowledged on the DL. The distinction is important: the ACL handles en route flight data, while the DL handles departure-related information. EDCT relates to departure clearance times, which is why it is acknowledged on the DL rather than the ACL.

items.instruction: Determine the correct sequence of actions for handling ERT coding.
items.question_text: A sector displays ERT coding for an aircraft that it has track control for. Put the following actions in the correct operational order: (1) Initiate hand-off to the next sector. (2) Issue and send/acknowledge the route. (3) Verify the sector has track control for the flight.

choices.text: Verify track control, then issue and send/acknowledge the route, then initiate hand-off.
Initiate hand-off, then issue and send/acknowledge the route, then verify track control.
Issue and send/acknowledge the route, then initiate hand-off, then verify track control.
Verify track control, then initiate hand-off, then issue and send/acknowledge the route.

items.explanation: The first sector displaying ERT must issue and send or acknowledge the route before initiating a handoff. ERT coding must not be sent or acknowledged unless the sector has track control or the action has been otherwise coordinated. Therefore, verify track control first, address the route second, and initiate the handoff last.

items.instruction: Distinguish between what triggers or removes the ESDT overdue aircraft notification and what actions the controller must take in response.
items.question_text: An ESDT overdue aircraft notification appears. Which statement correctly distinguishes between the automation's function and the controller's required response?

choices.text: The notification is based on radar track data, and updating the route of flight will remove it. The controller must also take overdue aircraft actions per Chapter 10, Section 3.
The notification is based on the flight plan ETA, and the controller must file an amended flight plan to remove it. No further action is required.
The notification appears when the pilot reports being overdue, and the controller must immediately declare an emergency.
The notification is removed automatically after 30 minutes, and the controller's only duty is to log the event.

items.explanation: The note to 13-1-12 states the ESDT overdue aircraft notification is based on radar track data, and updating the route of flight will remove it. However, the body of the paragraph requires the controller to take the appropriate actions set forth in Chapter 10, Section 3, Overdue Aircraft. The automation handles the display notification; the controller handles the operational response. Both are required—the route update removes the automation indicator but does not fulfill the overdue aircraft procedural requirements.

items.instruction: Determine whether each proposed GPD use is authorized or prohibited.
items.question_text: A controller proposes three uses of the Graphic Plan Display: (1) using GPD to assess whether a reroute would avoid active SUA, (2) using GPD trajectory-based position as a substitute for radar track position, and (3) using GPD to transfer radar identification to the next sector. Which uses are authorized?

choices.text: Only use (1) is authorized. Uses (2) and (3) are explicitly prohibited.
Uses (1) and (2) are authorized. Only use (3) is prohibited.
All three uses are authorized when EDST is operational and the controller is trained on GPD.
None of the uses are authorized. GPD may only be used for post-event analysis.

items.explanation: GPD is authorized for situational awareness and strategic planning, which includes assessing SUA avoidance. It may not substitute trajectory-based positions for radar track position or transfer radar identification. Its authorized scope supports planning and awareness, not real-time separation or identification functions.

items.instruction: Compare the operational response to two different data-loss scenarios.
items.question_text: Compare the situation where forecast wind data become unavailable (13-1-14) with a situation where interfacility connectivity to an adjacent ERAM facility is lost (13-1-15). What is the critical operational difference in what data may be affected?

choices.text: For forecast wind loss, both alert and trajectory data may be affected. For connectivity loss, alert data may be affected.
There is no difference; both scenarios affect only alert data.
For forecast wind loss, only trajectory data is affected. For connectivity loss, only alert data is affected.
For forecast wind loss, only conflict probe is affected. For connectivity loss, only trial planning is affected.

items.explanation: When forecast wind data are unavailable, controllers continue using conflict probe and trial planning while recognizing that alert and trajectory data may be affected. When interfacility connectivity is lost, controllers continue using EDST while recognizing that alert data may be affected; trajectory data is not identified. Forecast winds feed the trajectory model, while connectivity loss primarily affects the sharing of alerts between facilities.

items.instruction: Determine the correct sequence of actions when modifying an Airspace Configuration Element assigned as a sector responsibility.
items.question_text: A sector team has been assigned responsibility for Airspace Configuration Elements by facility directive. The team needs to deactivate an SAA that is no longer in use. Put the required actions in the correct order: (1) Update the Airspace Configuration Element to reflect the deactivated status. (2) Notify the operations supervisor that the status has been modified.

choices.text: First update the element to reflect current status, then notify the operations supervisor of the modification.
First notify the operations supervisor and obtain approval, then update the element.
Update the element as a standalone action. Notification is required only for SAA activation, not deactivation.
The operations supervisor must perform both the update and the notification; the sector team only identifies the need for change.

items.explanation: When Airspace Configuration Elements are assigned as a sector responsibility, the sector team first updates the element to reflect current status and then notifies the operations supervisor that the status changed. Both duties are required. Updating the SAA Airspace Status View does not itself constitute coordination unless an LOA or facility directive provides otherwise.

items.instruction: Discriminate between situations where CPDLC must be primary, voice may be used, or voice must be used.
items.question_text: In which of the following situations is CPDLC required as the primary means of communication, and in which may voice be the primary method? (1) A CPDLC-connected aircraft is outside VHF coverage and CPDLC is available. (2) A CPDLC-connected aircraft is within VHF coverage and voice would provide an operational advantage. (3) CPDLC has failed and connections cannot be reestablished.

choices.text: (1) CPDLC must be primary. (2) Voice may be used for operational advantage. (3) Voice must be used because CPDLC has failed.
(1) Voice must be primary. (2) CPDLC must be primary. (3) Either may be used.
CPDLC must be primary in all three situations whenever the aircraft is CPDLC-connected.
Voice must be primary in all three situations because CPDLC is always a supplementary communication method.

items.explanation: The communication hierarchy depends on operational conditions. Outside VHF coverage, use available CPDLC as the primary means. Voice may be used when it provides an operational advantage or workload or equipment capability demands it. If CPDLC fails, use voice communications.

items.instruction: Arrange the PDC pre-transmission review steps in the correct sequence.
items.question_text: A controller has a PDC ready to transmit to a departing aircraft. In what order must the review and verification steps be performed?

choices.text: Review the clearance for accuracy and route integrity, then ensure the information is complete, understandable, and the route is continuous, then confirm no amendments are present.
Ensure the information is complete and understandable, then review for accuracy and route integrity, then check for amendments.
Check for amendments first, then if none are present, review for accuracy and route integrity.
Review for route integrity only, then ensure the ATIS broadcast is current, then verify accuracy.

items.explanation: The PDC review sequence flows from the procedural requirements in 14-1-1: first review for accuracy and route integrity (para b), then ensure all information is complete, understandable, and the route is continuous (para c). Separately, the controller must confirm that the flight plan has not been revised or amended, since PDC cannot transmit amendments (para d). The accuracy and integrity check is the foundational step; the completeness check follows.

items.instruction: Choose the correct Field 4 entry for each SID scenario.
items.question_text: For each of the following SID scenarios, what must Selectable Field 4 contain? Scenario 1: SID with vertical guidance from take-off to a published altitude to maintain, aircraft will follow the SID. Scenario 2: SID with vertical guidance but no initial altitude to maintain. Scenario 3: SID with a published altitude to maintain, but the controller needs to assign a different altitude.

choices.text: Scenario 1: MAINTAIN (altitude); Scenario 2: CLIMB VIA SID; Scenario 3: CLIMB VIA SID.
Scenario 1: CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude); Scenario 2: CLIMB VIA SID; Scenario 3: MAINTAIN (altitude).
All three scenarios require CLIMB VIA SID, with the actual altitude in Field 5.
Scenario 1: CLIMB VIA SID; Scenario 2: CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude); Scenario 3: CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN (altitude).

items.explanation: Field 4 has three distinct applications. Use CLIMB VIA SID when the SID provides vertical guidance to an altitude to maintain and the aircraft will follow it. Use CLIMB VIA SID EXCEPT MAINTAIN with an assigned altitude when the SID has no initial altitude or when the assigned altitude differs from the published SID altitude.

items.instruction: Arrange the controller actions in the correct sequence for a CPDLC service shutdown.
items.question_text: CPDLC service is being shut down. The controller must take several actions. What is the correct sequence?

choices.text: First resolve open uplinks with each aircraft by voice, then broadcast the shutdown alert to all pilots.
First, broadcast a voice alert that CPDLC is no longer in use; then, resolve any open or abnormally closed uplinks by voice with each aircraft.
Broadcast the shutdown alert and resolve open uplinks simultaneously on different frequencies.
Terminate all CPDLC connections first, then notify pilots that CPDLC is no longer available.

items.explanation: Broadcast the shutdown first to stop new downlinks, then resolve open or abnormally closed uplinks with each aircraft by voice. Stopping new traffic first prevents additional open transactions from accumulating while the controller resolves existing ones.

items.instruction: Choose the correct transfer procedure for each scenario.
items.question_text: Compare two oceanic CPDLC transfer scenarios. Scenario A: The receiving facility is fully CPDLC capable. Scenario B: The receiving facility has no CPDLC capability. Which correctly describes the transfer procedure for each?

choices.text: Scenario A: Controller manually initiates CPDLC transfer. Scenario B: Automatic transfer proceeds but with reduced functionality.
Both scenarios use the same automatic transfer process; the receiving facility's capability does not affect the procedure.
Scenario A: No CPDLC transfer occurs; voice handoff is always used. Scenario B: Controller must terminate CPDLC before transfer.
Scenario A: Data link transfer is automatic within facility-adapted parameters. Scenario B: Transfer must follow local directives and Letters of Agreement.

items.explanation: Oceanic CPDLC transfer follows two paths. Transfer to a CPDLC-capable facility is automatic within adapted parameters. Transfer to a facility without CPDLC capability follows local directives and Letters of Agreement. The receiving facility's capability determines the applicable path.

items.instruction: Arrange the controller actions for a failed automated CPDLC transfer in the correct sequence.
items.question_text: An automated CPDLC data link transfer to the next facility has failed. What is the correct sequence of actions the controller should take?

choices.text: Advise the pilot to log on to the next facility first, then attempt a manual transfer to clear the failed automated attempt.
Terminate the CPDLC connection and revert to voice-only communications for the remainder of the flight.
Attempt to initiate the transfer manually; if unable to complete, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an End Service (EOS) message.
Send a connection request message (CR1) to the receiving facility, then advise the pilot to maintain the current connection.

items.explanation: For a failed automated transfer, first attempt to initiate the transfer manually. If the manual attempt also fails, advise the pilot to log on to the next facility and send an EOS message to close the current connection. Exhaust the manual transfer option before instructing the pilot to act.
